INTRODUCTION 


NOW that^he New Era is at hand — *he davuning of 
the Aquarian Ago — thousands under tlje general title df 
Advance Thought are begipjiing to respond actively to 
♦he influence of “the water-bearer," and because this 
advance guard of the Army of^Truth and Peace requires 
insight to the mysteries of Nature in order to best pro- 
mote their cause of enlightenment', we take this oppor- 
tunity to prerefrt for consideration and study the high- 
est t of all sciences, astrology. Highest, because when 
rightly understood, it opens the way clearly to an under- 
standing of the manifestations of Nature through human 
and mundane affairs. 

The science of astrology is not confined to those of 
technical training, for much of it can be learned by per- 
sons of ordinary education. But astrology is best ac- 
quired by those who have an inborn love of mystical 
subjects, and who at che same time possess an active 
sixth sense faculty, that of intuition. A peculiar temper-, 
ament like * that of the metaphysically inclined, rather 
than extraordinary schooling, is the particular requisite 
of those who would become adepts in this branch of 
useful investigation. 

Astrology is taken from the Records of astral phenom- 
ena anjl reduced to a science by observing the effects of 
planetary influence, commencing with the history of man ; 
these observations being conjpilecl and^recordod b^ some 
of the brightest intellects kn^wn, both ancielu and mod- 
ern. To test its reliability, its truths, and the advantages 
it ofFtVs, requires duly earnest, uhprejudiced investiga- 
tion. By it the inequalities of humanity are explained 
and much.light is shed upon the path leading to improve- 
ment of conditions for all living beings. 

^Astrology was th£ first science known toyman and J the 
presen| age is beginning tft resize that it is fhS greatest, 
^thje parent of them all, W^h a knowledge of astrology 
Mixqh of, the unknown becomes kngwn, the mysteries 
becomes plain and light J is shed in "all directions. 



10 „ O ENETHLIACfAl< ASTROLOGY . 

Anyofie with ordinary* ability can Jearn much <af astrdP 
8gy . It is no longer a difficult £tudy. ^Nothing .out of

With proper instruction *and a rfcasofiable amount of

that it becomes a guide regarding changes, health, maM 
riage, business and all important affairs of.life. 

There is not only pleasure but* also satisfaction*and 
profit in the knowledge which a^tjolog^ •gives. # Evfcn 
a slight investigation of the science tends to make people 
•broader in their views and more charitable toward their 
fellow beings. Astrology is a subject which becomes 
'•more and more scientific with age. As the number of 
earnest investigators increases so does more and more 
conclusive evidence of its usefulness accrue. New facts 
are constantly being presented for unbiased, truth-seek- 
ing minds to review, and if occasion offers, to use with 
advantage. Nobody can rightly claim to possess \ 
propef understanding* of astrology until they can cast 
and delineate the horoscope of birth, and erect and read 
a progressed chart. 

•The term ‘•horoscope” *is derived from the words, 
‘•hora,” an hour, and "scopft,” to view : a view of the < 
heavens for a certain hour, especially, an hour as meas- 
ured by the Sun. Formerly the word referred only to 
the Ascendant, or rising degree in a chart, but now its 
meaning is more general and has* referenced a # *whole 
figure 9 r .map of the heavens at birth time and is fre- 
quently referred to*as a nati\^ty. A horoscope is correct- 
ly drawn only when erected in accordance with the laws 
of Astronomy geocentrically applied* and such a shan't 
drawn by an astrologer is practically identical, from a 
mathematical standpoint, with one erected from.the saftie 
data in a§y astronomical observatory in *he world, but 
its aignifjpaitee is CQnceafed from all *exceptjthose who atre 
faifiijiar with the precepts of astrolegy* 

The pufpose of Astrology is not* to shodlder dff* re-* 
sensibilities to a*planet but,Vm the contrary, to learn Ry.< 
planetary indiaations*as they were affectipg* the Ea?rth* 
and its atmosphere at the time of'birth (i. e„ the haturc.

This book is arranged in five parts, the^advantage of 

this* division being obvious. Advanced students save’ 
time in being, able to turn at once to parts necessary fcr 
reference without searching through the whole book, as 
each part is CQmplete in itself. The subjects follow in 
logical sequence, enabling students to assimilate the 
essential principles and* methods, in the orderly manner 
nec^ssafy for practical ‘results, with the least expends 
ture ,of time and effort. 

New students should find the arrangement advantag- 
eous in that it conduces to concentration on one part of 
the subject and the mastery of it before proceeding to 
the next, thus making assimilation of each section easy 
and 'interesting. The five steps constitute a complete 
method of Practical, Modern Horoscope Casting and,, 
Delineating.

PREFACE 

* The ' word, astrology, is derived frqjn two Greek 
words, "astra,” ? star, and "legos/* logic or reason. Jt 
literally implies the doctrir.e and law as shown by the 
stars or planets. Astrology 1 * is the science which defines 
the action of celestial bodies upon animate and inanimate 
objects, and their reaction to such influences. It is the 
parent of astronomy. Originally, all who studied 
th;; sta/s wer^ astrologers. The antiquity of astrology is 
such as to place it among the very earliest records of hu- 
maft learning. Later, astrology and astronomy were one 
science, but the latter now treats only of distances, mag- 
nitudes, masses, compositions, motions, speed, etc., anc 
is founded upon observations made with various instru- 
ments ; therefore astronomy may be termed a purely ob- 
jective science, concerned as it is with the outer ex- 
pressions of other worlds, (with their form or body,) 
while astrology may be considered as subjective, dealing 
with the influence of ,the life within the form and its 
effect upon surrounding bodies, the Earth and its inhab- 
itants in particular and so may properly be termed, Th£ 
Study ol Life's tl Reactions to Planetary Vibrations. The 
making of a horoscopal figure is an astronomical process ; 
the reading of the chart is astrological. 

Astrological science is generally broadly divided into 
five great branches, namely: Medical Astrology, or 

-Astro- Pathology, is devoted to diagnosis, healing and 
prevention of physical and mental ailments by knowl- 
edge gained from a horoscope. HippocrateS,' who has 
been justly nam<ed the Father of Medicine, declared, "A 
physifian cannot safely administer medicine if he be un- 
acquainted with astrology/* which simply means that in 
th^ absence of knowledge of first principles for guidance, 
the application , pi remedies, however good thev may be, 
is #pt to be guesswork. Fortunately for the public at 
large, there are m^ny, of the new^chool of phvsiciansand 
sergeons v/ho^do urderstand astrology andwhe^are using 
this .knowledge with, gratifying* results.

Si. 


Asrtro-Meteorology is fhe art of predicting the* weather* 
*from planetary aspects. This branch is ‘again becoming 
a popular stujy and ha& been reduced to a ma‘rvelous % 
4*gree *of accuracy by & number* of ‘researchers. It is 
not at all associated with tfee methods used in common 
alfnanacs and by the Government Weather* Bureau. It 
is only a matter of time, however, when nations gener- 
ally will recognize this information and “ include * it in 
their present calculations, thus * making their system 
more practical, serviceable and efficient.* 

• Judicial Astrology relate^ to the forecasting of* the 
principal events which will come to pass in a country 
and conditions which will prevail in the land. For in- 
stance, the financial panic in the fall of* 1907 was pre- 
dicted by the progressed Moon in the U. S. Government's 
horoscope coming by progressed motion tc^an opposition 
of the radical and progressed Uranus, the Moon being 
in the house of money in th£ national chart. Students 
of St&te Astrology, $s this branch is sometimes called, 
not only foresaw but expected a financial panic. Here 
"is another instance concerning the use of Judicial Astrol- 
ogy: The Japanese were very proficient in thte braftch 
when they hjd official Court astrologers, and, in con- 
nection with the war against Russia (1904-5) while gen^ 
erals and statesmen everywhere were predicting success 
for the Russian arms, astrologers unanimously declared 
that Japan would be successful. The Japanese, them- 
selves, through their knowledge of astrMogy; never* 
sought^ a battle except on a day that Would be good for 
them witfi*t/ie resfcft'that tljey were astoundingly victor- 
ious in spite of superior forces. The Japanese govern- 
ment then used astrology as a necessary feature Jn the 
successful administration of its affairs, but soon after- 
ward abolished court astrology. It ts only a question 
of time yhen the fc United States and otfeer nations will 
find astrology indispensable for art orderly arrangement 
aitd b conduct of affairs in harmony %with natural laws. 

Rapid advancement is bain^madet in this branch Yif thb 
science, and through it the fcaflueac^of ati*intra-Meroq^> 
iS t planet (already ^named Vulcan) is tjeigig. felt *and u 
recognized. Astron&mers* have*»iot yet been successful

INTRODUCTION 


21 


# a»&troLogy 

Astrology Ifi The Science of Life’® Reactions 
To Planetary* Vibrations 

Astrology, as the interpreter of Nature, shows that 
the # world is conducted according to a well defined plan, 
tlytt everything is arranged with wonderful order, duly 
timed with marvelous precision and effected with unerr- 
ing accuracy. Nothing happens by chance ; there are no 
accidents in the divine plan ; and in reality there is no 
discord, for Nature recognizes no distinctions and works 
only for progress through a refining process which some* 
tjmes destroys only to reconstruct with improvement. 
As students advance in the study of astrology they real- 
ize that we are here for certain, definite, natural purposes, 
each in his place, according to an orderly or cosmic plan ; 
With opportunities to p'roduce certain results, but* it 
remains for us to determine the quality of those ^results 
by refining our reactions to planetary influence. 

• Thus? it is seen that although we are subject to the 
various seasonal changes and are affectcxl by planetary 
influences, yet we are ndt altogether and absolutely 
creatures of predestination or fate, # for it remains with 
us to improve or neglect our,, opportunities; to manifest 
the influence in a coarse or in a refined manner. 

Therefore, it behooves us to study Nature that, we may 
consciously co-operate wi§h her in* order to produce 
higher and finer results from the various causes set in 
motion through usr. 

• 

We do not change the law: we do # not hold Nature in 
abeyance nordiave it at our beck and call as* may suit 
ottr personal fancies, but we cas* improve our manifested 
response to it by 'improving oui* thinking, our haotts an % d 
Wtions. 

A 

w nether /ou have a «o-tfalled fortunate or unfortunate 

! >lanet for»your significator, gou will have opportunities 
or expression in yotfi**particular environment which no

pther individual will l&ve, and if we f wish to make the 
world a. better place to live in, we« must begin by refining 
our owr everyday expressions, or response to urges 
frCm within^ whic^ owe tSieirorigfh fo 'planetary vibra- 
tiocs. 

The study of astrology tshows us when fertain influ- 
ences are operative, but our own reactions, menially, 
physically and spiritually, determine to a large extent 
the quality of the results as affecting us and our immed- 
iate surroundings. Thus astrology becomes the mqst 
' important of all studies, and its precepts should he 
taught in every school.

THE ZODIAC 


The Z 9 <liac is«a circle of space surrounding tfie Eai^h. 
It -may be imagined as a <5elt in the* heavens abouMS 
t degrees wide in which the jfianets travel. It is the Sun's 
apparent path, called the ecligtic. The zodiacal circle is 
dividedjnto 1 twelve; parts, each part containing 30 de- 
gr^s oi opace Called* the Signs of the Zodiac. Thus a 
si^n a one-twelfth ^division of the zodiacal circle and 
is defined as contairfing 30 degrees of celestial longitude : 

* 12 signs each measuring 3Q degrees constitute the circle*, 
of the zodiac or 360 degrees. In this circle the planets 
travel each in its own orbit, one outlying beyond the.* 
o^her. The zodiac, then, is a circle divided into twelve 
signs through which the planets travel or transit from 
west to east going through one sign after another in 
their order from Aries to Pisces. Although the zodiac 
is generally referred to as a circle it is, in fact, elliptical. 
Note the illustration cyn page 8. 

Each sign possesses a certain specific influence of it? 
own. 

t The motion of the Earth* around the Sun once a year 
causes the Sun to appear to pass through one of the 12 
,signs each month? Its influence, according to location, 
determines not alone the seasons but the general nature 

1 and character of pers6ns born at that time. 

The planets also, as they travel iroumf ’the Zodiac, 
exert an influence according to their separate natures 
and ip accordance .with the quality of the aspects which 
they form.

The plmets are not arbitrarily°allocated to signs as 
« “rulers'*' but are in each, case sq designated by virtue* of 
similitudes. For* instance, although the Moon i$ but a 
satellite oS the Earthy it possesses powers ^sufficient 
dignify it by the name of \ planet. Its own inherit 
influence is found to be such that the Moon is termed the 
“ruleiif' of the, sign Cancer whxh is credited with influ- 
encej/thtft coincide with those of the Moon. Thus it is 
seei^that the word “ruler” although a convenient term, 
is a misnomer: *What is really implied is that the influ- 
ences of a certain planet harmonize or coincide with 
those of one or more of the signs which afford a freer 
medium for its expression.

The ascendant is always that point on the eastern 
horizon where the Sun^appears to rise in the looming. 
It is the point where the signs and degrees of»the zodiac 
appear t’o rise or ascend in their sequence. Prdjjerly, 
the “ascendant” is that particular sign &nd degree ’exact- 
ly on the eastern horizon in the* latitude of the birth 
place at the moment for which the chart is. cast. 

This apparent rising of the signs is due to the Earth's 
rotation on its axis at a rate of speed which causey one 
degree of the zodiac to appear to ascend to the eastern 
horizon every four minutes. As there ane 30 degrees to 
a sign, it requires approximately two hours for a com- 
plete sign to pass the horizon, to be followed in %his 
order by each of th* succeeding«sighs until in a complete 
day of 24 hours every degree of the zodiac will have been 
momentarily on the ascendant. By tha use pf Sidereal • 
Time, (into which terms birth time is* converted), 0 and 
"Tables of Houses, the e&act degree and sign passing the 
ascendant may be ascertained for any latitude. 

B By what has been said concerning the rising of a 
zodiacal degree every four minutes, the necessity of 
correct birth data becomes strikingly apparent: 

By careful study of tjie foregoing it will become 
evident that because Aries is the first sign of the zodiac 
it is by no iheans always on the first cusp of a chart* The 
signs are apparently constantly revolving, and at differ- 
ent times of the a day, different signs and degrees are on 
the ascendant and other house ct^sps, hence the necessity 
of knowing the «birti% time. The chapter on “Sinfereal 
Time” will. explain hew to find* the ascending sign in

All the signs of th* zodiac 1 are good; orie sign is 
equally a o good as c.nocher. 

The progressive student will do well to iemember that 
'destiny” properly m£ans whatever is possible unto ui, 
and "fate” neans the external circumstances which ap- 
pear in our pathway to be manipulated and eventually 
overcame through effort and ' nderstanding. "He who 
wouM slay dragons must first learn their habits.” To 
ovexome fate we must first know our fate. Astrology 
points the way to that desirable information. 

* Astrology is not a doctrine of fatalism. It provides 
us with an inventory of the working materials with 
which we were endowed at birth in the form of tenden- 
cies, mental capacity, physical endowments and abilities; 
but how we use or neglect to use the tools remains with- 
in ©ur own jurisdiction. "Wisdom puts an end to pain.” 
Through astrology we learn how to improve our work- 
manship on the wheel of life and consequently to improve 
our fate by complying, consciously with Nature's 4 laws 
instead of violating them. 

This system of astrology is known as the modernized 
Arabian Systefh, the oldest known, but revised an i 
adapted to present condition* and peoples. Correspond- 
ing to and harmonizing with astronomy, it is necessarily 
geocentric in its methods ("geo” meaning earth, and 
"centric” meaning center) ; in other words, recognizing 
t the Earth # as a center cf influence and observation. 

Placing the Earth in the center o{ a chart (in place 
of the Sun, see page 8) maizes it easy to visualize how, 
by the turning of the Earth on its axis from west to east, 
the* Signs appear tc ascend at the eastern 1 horizon and 
descend on the western. ° 

Astrology is ,a language of symbols, hence w) the first 
step toward it$ mastery is a th<tfough ? knowledge of its 
symbology. Before sttempting-to erect a horoscope, 
become perfectly familiar with the characteristics of*the 
planets, the" Atndenqes bestowed by the sighs* and the 
9 na|ure of the houses. TJien f 4earn which planets rule the

THE HOUSES -OF A .HOROSCOPE 

The twelver: houses of* the horoscope should hot be 
crnfusea with the twelve signs of the zodiaj, because 
to f a student the tWelve houses are divisions a map or

f * l 

The houses relate particularly to the material interests 
, and conditions, while the signs tell us more of the spirit-

and tendencies. * 

Inasmuch as parts of the human body fre concerned, 
the signs and houses are identical in influence, that is, 
the first zodiacal sign Aries rules the head and facets 
does also the first Irouse in a cflart. The sign Taurus 

• rules the throat as does the second house, and so on. 
Each house has a specific value and quility* o| its qwn 
and these characteristics* should be leaAied thoroughly 
by the student who hopes tp read a horoscope well. 

For instance: In addition to th$ influence already

as Angular, Succedent and Cadept. The angtes are the

7th and 4th housej, known respectively as the Ascendant, 
Midheaven ^(MC. — MediumSCoeli or Meridian), Descend- 
ant, and‘the # Nadir or lowest point. These are the most 
important places to ctAsider when judging a horoscope. 
When a planet Is in an angular house it is then termed 
“accidentally dignified,” if cadent, “accidentally debili- 
tated ” ‘

Fourth and Tenth Houses 
In Relation to Father and Mother^

Ninth house Influence

Canada Is the United 1 States ’a foreign country* and vice 
versa, inasmuch as they ar^ under difljprerfc f forms of government 
A Canadian, who wrshes to enter the civil service of* this country 
lr- obliged to take out naturalization papers just tis do English, 
German, French, Italian, Spanish, or any foreign borD person. 
Duty is charged on new gpods coming or going into u. S. or 
Canada, and Just as strictly" on the border as in the interior. 

Although Alaska for years past hafs belonged to the U. S., it 
strikes one forcibly that it must bea f r a ninfn .house r^ati^n to 
persons whose home is remote from it. •For instance, many have 
gone there and prospered, not suffering any particular discomfort 
from the cold, and other adverse elements, while a young man 
whom we know, fortunate in many respects, but with Saturn in 
ninth, went to Alaska and nearly froze to death. He suffered 
many other hardships besides. So the ninth house must represent 
foreignness in every sense of the word. 

The ninth house of a horoscope not only rultes long Journeys, 
foreign affairs, foreigners and foreign countries, hut also places 
remote from that of birth regardless of whether actually u».der 
anotfter flag, that is, to persons borp in the U. S., Alaska or the 
Philippines would be toreign” places. A person born in the U. 
S., near the border of Mexico, upon crossing the border, even but 
a short distunce, would technically be in a foreign country. As- 
trologically, the mere fact of his crossing over the llrte woultl not 
•entitle the act* to ninth hoifce consideration because the customs, 
#clfmate, etc., would all remain the same, and no radical change 
is apparent nor foreign elements to contend with. But, of cour&i 
Judgment must be Exercised on such questions as these, for cir- 
cumstances niter cases, for instance, had this hypothetical 
American formally cast off his allegiance to the U. ^ and adopted 
Mexico as his country and enterecW into active* interest in the 
affairs of that country, then It would be (i ninth house matter, 
for while distance did not make it a foreign affair, it was made 
so by the circumstances, conditions and the new elements and 
activities 'encountered.

Cardinal, Fixed and Common Signs 

The cardinal signs may be likened to the cardinal 
points of a compass, i. e., east, west, nqrth, south. 
Zodiacally expressed: Aries, east; Libra, west Cancer^ 
north; Capricorn, south. These signs, Aries, Cancer, 
Libra 'and Capricorn are palled cardinal signs, because 
of the change of seasons therein: Aries, spring; Cancer, 
summer; Libra, fall ; c C^apricoirn, winter. These chapges 
of season are due to the Sun's apparent change of declin- 
ation, or, in reality, to the obliquity 6i the Earth aepord- 

ing to its locatioirin its orbit. 

% 

Fixed signs mfiy be likened unto the huddle of a sea- 
son, th$re being threfe signs to* e^ch season. If.JJie 
cardinal sign* brings us ipfo ? f change of season then the 
fixed sign sees us firmly centered 6r set ip that seafson. 

* • * i ••• 

Common signs arc the^third or last months pt the foil* 
seasons. We have' become wfell acquainted with the

§ Perspns l?orn during the 'reign of cardinal signs Sre 
said to be versatile, adaptable jpd readily capable of ad-

J^hose who /ire born fn fixed signs are more set in their 
ways and more fixed in their views than those born in 
Cardinal or common signs. They are likely to have more 
continuity and be more difficult to move, change or 
sway. They do not so readily change their minds; are 
no£so quick to adjust and adapt themselves. 

Those bori* in common signs have more or less the 
attributes of both cardinal and fixed and can adapt 
themselves to the natures* of both those signs, conse- 
quently, to the natives*of the othgr signs, those of the 
common signs, seem to be mutable or unstable.

Nature and Correspondences 

' 4 


Thi influence of the Moon, Venus, Sun and Jupiter is considered 
good. The influence c* Mars, Saturn and Uranus is known as 
malefic. The influence of Pluto is not yet definitely determined. 
Mercury and Neptune are neutral, 1 . e., theft* influence is good 
when in good aspect to other planets hut is adverse* when evilly 
aspected. Jupiter is called* the “greater fortune” and Venus the 
“lesser fortune.” 

^ When a planet is at home (in its own sign,) exalted, 
dignified by position in the chart (in angles or being the 
planet most elevated or nearest the midheaven,) or well 
aspected by beneficent planets, it manifests its highest 
qualities, that is rf the native is inclined to react wisely 
or favorably. 

y When a ’planet is, out of dignity (out of home *sign, 
exaltation, ouUof angle, etc.,) and debilitated by unfavor- 
able sign position or aspects, especially by malefic plan- 
ets, tha lower siefe of jts nature is more likely to be prom- 
inently manifested. 

If a ]3\anet should recai^e a good aspect from ajberiefic 
and an sdVerse aspect froi£ malafic, the^lanjt in. ques- 
tion will manifest both sujes <pf its natiire — the higher 
and the lowrer — when influenced by directions and.tran 
sits, and in accordance with tho nature of such 'aspects.

The Epkemeris 

After the signs and planets^and their symbols have 
beeg learned, continue the investigation by procuring a 
geocentric Ephepieris for the year of birth. 

. In the Ephemeris tor the birth year on a line with the 
date of birth will be found figures expressing the “Sider- 
eal Time” for noon * on that day in hours and minutes. 
(In some Ephemerides the seconds are also quoted. Dis- 
regard the seconds until the rules have been learned.) 
Sidereal time is the Sun time when it is noon at any place 
on Earth. In \he Ephemeris, Sidereal time is usually ab- 
breviated S. T. Note the St T. in the following specimen 

An Ephemeris is a publication vfhich shows the S. T. 
at noon eagh <J a Y * n a certain year, and among other 
things, thfe sign and degree of the sign occupied by the 
planets at noon "each day at Greenwich (England). It ifi 
necessary therefore to refer t« an Ephemeris for the year 
in which birth took place, and in it reference is made to 
the birth month afid date. This will then show the 
zodiacal petitions of the planets on that day. There are 
<welve signs, # and thirty* degrees of celestial longitude to 
each sign. The pkinets are constantly moving frqm one 
degree to the next, passing through oite sign, and enter- 
ing the next sign, except when retrograde. (Apparently 
’moving in reverse order.) You wiU know when to con- 
sider a planet retrograde or direct in motion by the 
letters R or D in the planets' longitude columns of the 
Ephemeris. See* illustration of page ffom 1^10 Ephem- 
eris where you,*vill see that Saturn wan retrograde dur- 
ing the month of December. Note that ifs longitn<ft 
gr&hi^ly decreased^and that i retrogressed # from* Taurus 
to Aries.

Rctrogradation 

‘When a p^rnet appe«yrs to begirt to move backward in 
the ordehoi the signs it wk usually (but not always) tie- 
noted by. an K marked wi\r^ifs degrees in fhe Epheiperis. 
^Vhen it apparently turns c^ re St in^ motion it is usuLlty 
ifitiifated by* D at that t date. A planet, is** $aid to b% 
stationary when neither moving direct nor retrograde. 



65 


* PART I, THE NATAL CHART 

§ » 

* TJie pa\h or orbit of eVery planet is more of &n ellipse 
than a circle. No two planets perform a complete revo- 
lution in*the same period of tim’e. Owing tp the differ- 
ences in their spee’d there is continual change in th^ 
zodiac rfs vi^tved from the oarth, one jflanet overtaking 
<yid passing another, then apparently slowing, stopping 
and beginning to move backward. The apparent stop- 
ping is a # “stationary” position ; the apparent backward 
movement is termed “retrograde.” However, although 
eacji, planet m^vos more quickly at certain points of its 
orbit (the earth's morion varies between 59 and 61 min- 
iftes*of longitude per day), no planet actually becomes 
stationary or retrograde, only by appearance, caused by 
the combined motions of the planet and the earth and 
their positions relative to the Sun. (See illustration on 
page 57.) 

• 

When you find in the Ephemeris that the longitude of 
anf planet is increasing be'tween one day and the next 
you may know such placet is direct^ in motion. If long- 
itude decreases, the planet is retrograde. 

• 

We belfeve that influence of benefic planets is weakened^ 
when they are retrograde. The condition cTf retrograda- 
*ion is contrary or inharmonious to the regular direction 
of actual movement, in the zodiac and»is in that respect 
evil; hence, when malefic planets arc retrograde theij 
jnalefice is # ir^creased. Aspects, whether radical or pro- 
gressed* that are thrown to a retrograde planet rob the 
pjremittor of its efficacy, what it promises falling short 
in actual results. Should bofti the planets be. retrograde 
the things indicated by the aspect would be deficient or 
disappointing, hence retrogradarion is considered a 
debility. 

The effect of £ planet is usually '#ery strong w*ien it is 
stationary upon the ^lace of a •radical or progressed 
planet or in aspect to such. For*instance, Saturn is re- 
trograde foV 140 days, and stannary approximately five 
days ‘before and after # retrogr.*<]at ion. 

% Jupiter -is* retrograde 120 days afid is stationary 
proximately five days before -and after. 



50 


GENETHLIA.C4L ASTROLOGY * 


Mars is retrograde for°80 days*and is stationafy two or’ 
three dfcys before and after. 

Venys is retrograde 42 days and is stationary approx- 
imately two day$ befor^and after. 

f c> ^ 

Mercury is retrograde 24 days and is stationary ap- 
proximately one day before and after. (The Sun and 
Moon are never retrograde or stationary.) 

The favorable influence of a gbod aspect forme^d by a 
planet during retrogradation is considerably debilitated 
and falls short of its efficacy in the good results which 
would otherwise be indicated. 

The adverse influence of an adverse aspect formed by 
a planet during retrogradation is considerably accentu- 
ated. (Please see page 222.) f

Sidereal Time — How to Use It 
For P. M. Births 

» 

When a birth occurs in the p. in., add^the time of the 
event to the S. T. given in ,the Ephemeris for that date 
and„the answer will be the S. T. at birth. If the sum 
exceeds 24 hours (a full astrological day) subtract 24 
and use the remainder as the S. T. at birth ; This figures 
is then used as a starting point in the Tables^of Uguses 
,as shown in the next chapter. 

4 ' 

For A. M. Births 

, When a birth occurs in the a. m, (between midnight 
and noon), the method differs Rightly from fha^ of p. nv 
births. 

For an a. ni. Wrth subtract the time of the event from 
noon (12 m. or 11:60) and then subtract the remainder 
from the S/T. giver> tn the Ephemeris for that <lat£ and 
the answer is'rtie S. T. at birth. This figure is then used 
as a starting p^int in the Tables of Houses. If you 
should find the S. T.ias given in the Ephemeris too small 
to be subtracted 1 fron#, add 24 hours tcJnt, then do the 
s*ut>tracjing«and the remainder is' the S. T. at birth. f 

For an ’example, to ll^iftrate the different rules to 
follow, we will use the date December ^4th, 1910, lVotv 
City. • Birth at 11, a. m. mean local time (l^houar 
before noon). 



50 


# PART I, THE NATAL CHART 


• S. T. 1(J.49 at noon (zfe given in. the Ephemeris Dec. 
4th) % minus' 1 hour, birth before noon, (i. e., 11 a. m.,) 
gives S. T. IS. 49 rat birth* Find this figure (S. T, 15.49) 
in T. of H. of theJatitude for which the chart is to be- 
erected. 

^Jote: Had the birth time been 1 p. m. (or one hour 
after noon) we would simply ha>e added 1 hour to the 
given, S. T. for noon, and the sum would be used as the 
starting point in the T. o£H. which will Show what signs 
and fl£g#ees are' passing over cusps of houses in the hor- 
oscope at birth time in the latitude of the birth place. 

Tables of Houses — How To Use Them 

Have at hand a horoscope blank such as is used for 
the Example Chart further on in this book. Turn to the 
back # part of Raphael’s Ephemeris and find New York 
T. of H. as this birth occurs near New York City which 
is it* Latitude 40° 43' north.* In the S. T. columns look 
for the S. T. nearest that $vhich we have just worked out 
of the foregoing example, 15.49. Thf nearest S„ T. given 
In the tablesds bSA7. Take that as our starting point: 
In the ne^t naryw column, on the same line, we find 
the figures 29 and farther up the same column is the 
symbol of the zodiacal sign, Scorpio, and right above iv 
the figure 10. This means that the 29th degree of the 
sign, Scoipio, shouU? be placed on ttie cusp oi the tenth 
house in o»r chart. The other five narrow columns 

should b£ treated in the hame manner. 

• 

It will be noticed that thoi tables give the sigh and 
degree for only six houses in bur chart, but a*s we have 
already learned in a preceding chapter, which, signs must 
always oe opposite to each other we fill i$ the other six 
cusps.of the chart in their proper order, using the same 
number of degrees. In so doing the example chart 
should work out Jlike this : 

29* TO on Tenth cusp, opposite 8 on Fourth t 

21* / on ^leventh cusp, oppAjte 21* II on Fifjh? 

H* VJ on # Twelfth fcusp, opposite 11° 55 on Sixth? 

7 # 46' z: I?irst cu»p, opposite V 46' Si on Seventh. 

25* >€«o2 Second cusp, opposite 25** TO on Eftghth. 

• 8* 8 on Third cusp, opposite 3* TO on Ninth.

genethl/aoal astrology 


Be very careful t6 copy from the tables the correct 
$ign. Sometimes the sign shown a't the top o* a column 
completes *he 30 degrees comprising one sign and the 
sign next in order begins lower dbwn. In that case 
j eject the sign c at top and use the one ohowta nearest 
above the Sidereal Time at Birth. 

The degrees given may vary slightly if other than 
Raphael's Tables are used, due io slight difference in lat- 
itude, viz., from 40° 43' to 41V* 

Looking around our chart it will be noticed that the 
sign Aries and its opposite sign (Libra) have been left 
out. Place them in center of the houses where they would 
naturally fall in the regular order of the signs, i. e, be- 
tween second and third .cusps for Aries, and between 
eighth and ninth cusps for Libra. 

Oftentimes it will be found that one sign extends over 
two cusps and thu^ causes another sign to be crowded 
out; therefore, always look the chart over carefully and 
see if all the signs are there and in the'proper sequence. 
5igns thus left out and so inserted, as in th'e example, 
"are called “intercepted" signs. 

% 

If the example data called for birth in 40 degrees only, 
no minutes of latitude, there would be a slight difference.

tude makes in some cases, note the list of cusps follow-

interception 

Due partly to the latitude for which a horoscope is 
cast, as well as to inequality in the shape of the Earth, 
it sometimes appears that a sign is intercepted between 
the cusps of two houses yet not on either of them’. How- 
ever, if the conventional form of horoscope blank is re- 
placed with a chart on which the 12 signs and 360 de- 
grees are shown,* the house cusps marked in Ly the 
student as indicated by the T. of H., will readily show 
that interception is an appearance on'y when viewed 
from certain latitudes on the Earth. At the equator all 
the, signs rise in regular rotation, but as we proceed to 
south or north latitude an inequality in their rising 
occurs. In other words, there is no interception in the 
zodiac. It appears only in charts. 

It will b£ seen that aq intercepted sign is one which 
has all of its thirty degrees contained within the confines 
of one house of* a chart, whereas*ordinarily a sign has 
. some degrees above a cusp and the remainder below it. 
The fact that a sign is intercepted in a*hflrosjcopc dqes 
not seem to alter its influence. In # other words, inter- 
ception neither increases nor decreases the force of the 
tendencies bestowed by ihat sign. However, the influ- 
ences of \ house which is host to such entire sign may 
be “stepped up” sb*that planets therein may' seem to 
possess mor<? potency than if posited there when not 
intercepted. However, that this is more or less Conjec- 
ture is indicated bf tne fact that the*findings of astrol- 
ogers are not ifi agreement on ^this point of accelerated 
influence. There may*be^some occqJt influence associated 
with or* ipdccated by intercepted signs and in some cases 
it may manifest physically. Th£ older astrologers held 
jthat the most important part V>f a' house was its cusp. 


A blknk such as used iCjth the “Mechanicpl Aspectarian.” 

‘ c 1 ■ 




PART I, THE NA*TAL CHART 


tThe. stucfejit should^ remember ttfat if the T. of H. 
shows a sign covering twq cusps# the opposite sign of' 
the zodiac will lifc£wise do so and, consequently, some 
other sign .and its d^posite will b£ intercepted. , In the 
high nortlrern latitudes it is n&t uncommon to find two 
sig*s intercepted in *bne house and of course the two 
* opposite signs wjll be intercepted fn the opposite house. 

(See .Intercepted signs, Chart 3, p. 170, and note on p. 77.) 

Cofrectiorv to Sidereal Time 

To the earnest student who is desirous of being ac- 
curate in details connected with the proper construction 
of a chart, we offer a consideration which should be dealt 
with iu all cases when the birth time is presumed to be 
reasonably correct, and that is a correction between the 
mean focal time«of birth and S. T. at noon. That is, 
noting the difference in time: between birth time and 
noon, correct the Sidereal Time quoted for that birth (fay 
at the rate of 10 second^ fP>r every htur of such differ- 
ence. The "Table of Correction to S. T.” on page 64 
makes the of>eratio?i simple for parts of an hour. 

• « c 

S. T. increases from the first <jay of spring at the rate 

of approximately 4 minutes per day of 24 hours ; 2 min- 
utes in 12 hours; 1 minute in 6 hours, of about 10 sec- 
onds in 1 hour. 

• 

If birth occurred before *noon subtract the necessary 
correction from the S. T., as shown in fphemeris.* If 
birth was after noon, add to th&S. T. the correction of 
• 10 seconds for every hour after. 

• • 

Thus: For a birth, occurring 4:00 p. m. # mean local 
time De£. 4, 1910 — # 

16:49 # :41 S. T. aV noon tiiat date \ 

add. ;40 Correction fc>r 4 hours* (Birth occurred ,4 hrs/ 

aflei* npon.) 

16:4^:81 taansfcym 81’ seconds to 1 minute and 21 
6ecorfds, making the S. *T. ft>:5®:21 aft^r correction be- 
tween mfean afta S! T. To this weYiow add the 4 hours, 
making the S. T. at birth 20:50:21.

6= 1 second correction. 


This table will help in rftaking correction to S. T., as 
given in Ephemeris, for longitude of birth place, and 
also the correction between mean and S. T. 

Correction to Sidereal Time for Longitude of Birth Place 

Another factor necessary to consider in an effort 
towards accuracy is a correction to S. T. for the long- 
itude of any place in question. 

Change the longitude into lime by multiplying it by 
4 minutes, and divide the product by 60 minutes (to get 
hours and minutes) or ?j 0 seconds if less than 15 de- 
grees from Greenwich, and get minutes and seconds : 
the answer is the t'/me distance from Greenwich. For 
every hour dl this time allow 10 seconds, and for parts 
of an hour in proportion.

OENETHLI ACAL ASTROLOGY 


TTME CHANGES 
An Important Lesson For Students 

r Standard Time as use<J’in the United State's, Canada 
and Mexico, is the term employed to designate that all 
the clocks in each tim^ zone register the same time, re- 
gardless of whether a place is east or west of the center 
of a zone. Standard time is therefore an artificial method 
of time and for horoscopal work the standard time should 
always be changed to mean time. 

Mean time, sometimes called local mean time, mean 
Solar time, civil time, or true local time; all of which 
titles are intended to designate the real time of a place 
as distinguished from its standard or artificial clock time. 
The birth time given for horoscope wor4c should always 
be reduced to this mean time which is then changed 
intc Sidereal Time to determine the S. T. at birth. 

How to Convert Standard Time Into Meqn Local Tim" 
For Any Place in America 

On November 18, 188X at noon, a change was msfde 
from local to standard time, therefore, for all birth times 
and for all charts to be cast for any year since that date, 
in America,* calculation should be made to convert 
standard time back into mean local time, and the true 
time used as tftus fqund^ For instance, in Salem, Ore., 
standard; or city clock tbne, is approximately 12 minutes 
faster than mean, or true, time. To find what the differ- 
ence is between standard and mean time for £r\y other 
place in America, observe the following rule: 

America is divided into five “hour zones/ 1 each one 
15 degrees of longitude in width. Tire center of each 
rone is a dividing line. Places east of center need some- 
thing added to clock ime; places west, of ceiiterneed 
something subtracted from clock time. 


• Also for using the “Improved Perpetual Planetary Hour Book.” 



PART I, THB N'A^AL CHART 


m 


• Add four minutes # to» clock timd for every degree of 
longitude, a place may be situated east of the “center?' 
of a zone. The* rfesult is Mean Local Time from # which 
one hour /is to be hubtrScted if Daylight Saving time i$ 
in use. 

"Subtract four minutes from clock time for every degree 
of longitude a place may be situated west of the “center" 
of a time zone. The result is Mean Local Time from 
which one hour is to bet subtracted if Daylight Saving 
timt fs # in use. * 

Time Zone Divisions 

The first zone has “Inter-Colonial,” or “Atlantic" time 
as it is more generally called. It extends from 52 % de- 
greej west longitude to 6 7j£ degrees west longitude. 
(This space of 15 degrees includes Novia Scotia, the 
Bermudas, the JLesser Antilles and British Guiana.) The 
center of this zone is the 60th degree of west longitude. 

T&e second zone has “ Eastern” time. It extends from 
6 7 l / 2 degrees west longitifde to 82 degrees west longi- 
tude. The center of this division is 75 degrees west 
longitude. • 

The third zone has “Central"* time. It extends from* 
8JJ4 degrees west longitude tb 97 degrees west long-* 
itude. The center of this division is # 90 degrees west 
longitude. * 

The fourth |one has “Rocky Mountain” time. It ex- # 
tends frohi 9 7 x / 2 degrees west longitude to ll2 l / 2 degrees 
west longitude. The center of this division is 105 degrees 
west longitude. 

The fifth zone has “Pacific" time. It expends from 
112 ^ 2 *dtfjg:rees west longitude to 127^ degrees west long- 
itude. The center of this division is 120* degrees west 
longithde. 

Orwthe center ol a division, i. e., 6(7, 75 f 90, 105 and 120 
degrees west I6ngitud<n “standarg” time and “mean” 
tim* arj ev$n. But#as one proceeds east of west of*a 
cefiter # a difference between standard and trlic* time oc- 
ci\rs#at the* rate £>{ four minutes for each degree of long- 
itucTe, ^which piust be VIded tt> or su^tracted 9 from youa # 
clbck m ordkr to get true time. Then subtract one hour ' 
if Daylight Saving Time is in use*

Note: When it is' found by reference to a reliable 
atlas that the place of residence is very near the limit of 
a zone, i. efi, near 6 7 l / 2 , 82 1 / 2 , 9 7]/ 2} 112.% degrees west 
longitude, great care should be taken to ascertain what 
standard of time is in use ac that place, i. e., what zone 
time. The foregoing rules also apply to Canada. * 

No other corrections to clock time are necessary, ex- 
cept to deduct one hour from clock time when Daylight 
Saving time is used. The daily newspapers announce 
the dates for beginning and ending of the Daylight Sav- 
ing periods. Unfortunately all places do not begin and 
end on the same dates, nor do all places use it. Astro- 
logically speaking, we wish there were no such thing as 
Daylight Saving Time because it contains liability of 
serious errors. 1 

Examples 

No. 1 : Find the necessar-y correction between stand 
ard khd mean time for a birth at Salem, Oregon. 

Rule: Salem, Oregon, is at 123 degrees west long- 
itude. That city is, therefore, in the Pacific zone, and is 
located 3 degrees west of the center (u e., 120 degrees 
west longitude). Four minutes 'to each degree equals 
four times three degrees or 12 minutes. Being west of 
the center, subtract 12 minutes froi?? clock time for that 
place for data since Nov. 18, 1883. Thus: When it is 
10 o'clock standard time at Salem, the me^n or true time 
at that place is 12 minutes to 10 a. m., i. e.% 9:48 a. m. t 
and the correction to time should be used for astrological 
purposes ni Salem, Oregon. 

Example' No. 2. % Find the necessary correction be- 
tween standard and mean time for Boston, Mass. 

Process: Boston is on the 71st degree of west long- 
itude. That city is, -therefore, in the eastern zone. It is 
located 4 degrees east of the center (i* e., 75 degrees 
West Iqngitude,) and 4 rr\inutes for each decree of lpng- 
itude e<mal? 4 times 4*- degrees, or 16 rpinutes. 4 - Being 
east of ' the^center, add 16 minuted to all clock times for 
• that place, since 1883. Thus ; IP a time is given W 1 
o'clock at Boston, the mean time at that pla r cc is 1 rI6. Re 
sure to note whether I^ayKght 0 Saving time is in use. f

Montana in Mountain Time 

The entire statg of Montana was placed in standard Mountain 
time June 1, 1919, i£ if ruyng issued by the Interstate Commerce 
Commission. ' However the Zone Tim# Map sfcows by the red life 
just east of a ^portion of the western boundary that the railroads 
)ptt a small section tif Montana in the Pacific zone. For births 
in places where zone time changes ^re not definitely known it 
will be necessary to ascertain carefully what standard of time is 
used there. For this information write to the Railway Station 
Agent. 

lWs # is an important notice to horoscopal students, as it means 
that hereafter all charts made for places in Montana west of 
112% degrees of longitude must be corrected to Mountain time. 
Heretofore that portion of Montana was in the Pacific time zone 
The new ruling affects all towns in Montana between 112% and 
116 degrees west longitude. Henceforth, to convert Mountain 
clock time Into true mean (horoscopal) time for Butte subtract 
30 minutes from dock time. For Missoula, subtract 36 minutes. 
All Other towns#in this portion of Montana accordingly. Study 
the following rules and make note of this new time factor for 
future reference.

Time in Detroit 

i. 

The Detroit News gives the following statistics* regarding time 
4n Detroit, Michigan: 

A number of propositions regarding time Change were voted on 
In Detroit between 1905 and 1916. The first time was the spring 
election of 1905, when the voters favtfred central standard time 
by a large majority. 

In 1907 three propositions were voted on, Central standard. 
Eastern standard and Sun time.^ The biggest majority was In 
favor of Central standard. In the fall of 1908 a similar vote was 
takei^. In the spring of 1911 the proportion to change to Eastern 
standarS was defeated by just a bare majority. In August 1916 
the proposition to change to Eastern standard passed by a large 
majoslty, and Detroit has had Eastern standard ever since.

Daylight Saving 

Daylight 'Saving offers many advantages in the com- 
mercial world as well as to individuals, but intends to 
complicate; the 4l time factors" for students^ of astrology 
in that if the rule comes into general practice in th*; 
United States it becomes incumbent upon astrological 
practitioners and students to make allowances for that 
advanced hour and subtract it from all given Horary 
chart times as well as for the Uirth times of infants ; in 
addition to making the usual correction betweeiY mean 
and standard time as always, since noon November 18, 
1883, when Standard Time was adopted. 

In other words, it must be plainly understood whether 
any certain given time is Daylight Saving time or not; 
if so, subtract one hour from it, then make the usual cor- 
rection from standard to mean time, for all astrological 
purposes. 

i

6r slower than mean time.'

•Time. Changes 'Necessary in* Horoscope Work 

When a horSstope is to be erected for a^plac^ which 
is east q# the center of* a standard tim^ zone, count how 
many degrees of longitude ft is east, multiply that num- 
ber by four minutes and add the result to the birth time 
given by the clock ; then procetW to erect the chart with 
the true time thus found. (See Note concerning Day- 
light Saving time on p\ges 65 and 68.) 

• • • * 

If tfie place of birth is west of a center in one of these 
zones, count how many degrees it is west, multiply the 
number by four minutes, then subtract the amount from 
the time given and proceed to erect the chart in the usual 
manner. (See Note concerning Daylight Saving time on 
pages 65 and 68.) 

In other words, allow 1 minute for each of a degree 
of Jongitude from a center in any zone, adding to the 
time given if the place ij east, and subtracting frdin it if 
the place is west of the center, • Portland, Oregon, is 
* approximately $ degrees west of center in the Pacific 
zone; therefore^ 12 minutes should always be subtracted 
from the standard time to make mean local time. ' 

Special attention should be given to the rules in con- 
nection with Horary Astrology and Mundane Astrology 

and in the use of Planetary Hour Tables. , 

• • 

Iri calculating the nativity, however, the efforts toward 
accuracy should wot end here, for the horoscope should 
be corrected or ‘‘rectified” by "Arc of •Events" before an 
attempt is made at figuring fhe progressed chart. Very 
few. people know more than th? # approximate time of 
their birth, and for that reason extra fffort should be 
exerted to make the data as correct as possible before 
erecting a chart* 

V^hen very •particuljir work is* required, that is, when 
precise, scientific Results are, desired (dates and nature 
of events, ct<;.) the data must.be corrected#!!* A scientific 
rmyifier. * 

’Ope of the # mosf accurate and, reliable methods* Is 
given in )’ J art # IV in th« volume: A process of calcula- 
tions deducted Jrom prominent 'dates in the life*. These

Tjime S/andar&s ir^ Foreign Countries 

A few words regarding other standards of tftne will not be 
amiss right here. The careful student win remember to investi- 
gate the dates received f<ft births in foreign . countries before 
erecting the horoscope. Many nations have standards of time all 
their own -and, therefore, great cautlcwn should be exercised. 

In Russia, for instance, they lised the Julian calendar 
(until February 14th, 1918 when the Gregorian c&lendar 
was adopted), and in the nineteenth century their dates 
differed from ours (i. e., Gregorian, adopted in England 
in 17S2) by 12 days, and in this twentieth century by 13 
days. When it is September Sth by our calendar Jit is 
August 23rd by theirs. The Gregorian calendar, adopted 
February 14th, 1918, was superceded by /he Soviet* cal- 
endar October 1st, 1929. By it five days comprise each 
weekend six such weeks comprise each of the twelve 
months, leaving five (in leap )t 2 ar six) surplus days to 
be accounted special festivals. 


, Throughout England, Greenwich standard time is 
used (except* in Canterbujy and Ipswich, where local 
time is kept) and to find the mean time of any plact 
there add 4 minutes for every degree of longitude east 
erf Greenwich, and subtract 4 minutes for each degree 
of longitude west. # € 

Another Kind of Time } 

When* the railroad extended Jts line to X .... , and adopted 
Mountain time for its official purposes, the little town was thrown 
Into confusion. Since earliest times the townsfolk had used 
Central Standard time. , Folks went to church* by It, werew bap- 
tised, married ar^l buried by Central time, and the old sexton 
could not be induced to change his habits. * Now, in order not to 
miss being at the station to see the trains come in, they* were 
obliged to keep two clocks, one timed to Cdbtral, the other to 
Mountain. Soon thet citizens split into two fac/lons: "Mountain” 
and "Central” and there urns much arguing of the pros and cons. 
Unde Ezra listened but said nothing, and gfter considerable .cogi- 
tating he decided to use a tf pie just half way •between ^Central 
and Mountain, so os "not to liult any one's feelings.'* • 

tut it illustrates the*}?*

and cannot *ive definite Information on such matters, write to the

PART I, TOE NAtAL CHART 

NO(5N MARKS 

Noon Mark is t^emegn local time at birth-place whep 
it is nooif (12 m.) at Grecnjvich* England. . 

• Because Greenwifch, England, represented 0° 0' of ter- 
restrial longitude at the time of tfhilding the Observatory 
there, most Ephemerides are calculated from that point. 
This is very convenient *f or Britishers and in the early 
day£ t>£ Astrological History in the United States calcu- 
lations for correcting the planets' places for a chart were 
made by adding to or subtracting from Greenwich time. 

But for American students and those born other than 
at G«eenwich that cumbersome and unnecessary method 
should be substituted by one more up-to-date and in 
keeping with actual geographical conditions. 

The method of Noon Marks for such calculations is 
the logical process. 

* Noon M?rk is«the mean, local time at the birth place, 
wherf it is noon*at Greenwich, England. It is simply a 
time equivalent, or to state it f another way:' 

* 

Noon Mark is the mean, local time .corresponding to 
the time the planets *are inserted in the Ephemeris. Th<* 
ephemeral place of the planets mu^t be corrected for the 
amount of time one is bom before or after the Noon 
Mark. « Compare the birth time with the Noon ^ Mark 
and the difference between thefri will indicate the amount 
of correction necessary to the given longitude for each 

planet, before inserting them in the horoscope. 

% 

The following illustrations will show how to do so 
correctly. There) is nothing complicated aboUt this 
methbd ; on the, contrary, it is simpler end more logical 
than the old way, especially by using the “Table of Nopfi 
Maflcs”»on pages 78 %nd 79. 

Cporectinfe the* Planets’ places for the Time of Birth , 

•We* have' now finished .with the T. of H., so turn to ’ 
the Ephemeris for the example Mate of birth ’(Dec. 4,

G BNETHLf ACAL ASTROLOGY 


1910) and take a glance at the 0 planets' places, a ^ they 
$re given by sign and degree of longitude. ^ (Latitude 
and declination we will not use at present.) *The plane- 
tary locations are calculated fur each day noon in 
Greenwich, England. (See specimen of *an Ephemeris 
on page 54 for December, 1910.) < »* 

For persons born right in Ureenwich it is Only neces- 
sary to make a correction to the planets' places, as they 
are found in the Ephemeris fol noon* fo, r the amqujit of 
their motion in the time between 'birth and noony adding 
if born after noon and subtracting if before noon. - 

The Moon, being swiftest in motion, is the principal 
planet to correct. Its approximate motion is about ]/ 2 
degree of longitude per hour. (Its motion varies be- 
tween 30 and 37 l / 2 minutes of longitude per hour of 
time.) 4 

Bfit for births occurring in places other than Green- 
wich it is necessary to work ifi a slightly different man- 
ner to find the planets' correct places in the zodiac at 
the moment of birth. First find the time (called ,Noon 
4 Mark) at tl\e place of birth when it is nRion in Greenwich 
*md correct the planets' given ephemeral places accord- 
ing to their motion in the amount of time that birth oc- 
curred before or after the Noon*Mark of that place, 
"adding the motion if % born after the Noon AJark and sub- 
tracting if born before the No«n Mark 

« 

Noon Mark for Any € Place — How to Find It 

Here is # a simple rule for finding any Noon Mark: 
Refer to an atlas ancTfind the longitude df the biiWplace, 
multiply the longitude by 4 minutes, divide by 60, sub- 
tract tjie remainder from 11 :60 (12 m.) if it is west long- 
itude, and the ansvfler is the a. m. time (Noon Majjk) in 
Jthe place of birth wlftn it is rioi)n in Greenwich. 

• 

If the Jougitude is e^st multiply^it by # 4, *divide I>y 60 
and the answer is the p. In. (Noon Marik) time* in the 
» birth place when it is nc#>n in Greenwich. Whefl»*fhe 
sum canmft be divtded*by 60 the sum il6eff.is the No^n

* Fog example, w # e find tfiat New York City is in approx- 
imately 7\ degrees West longitude. 

74 x 4 jr .296 minlite^; 296 ~- % 60 = 4 hrs., 56-mins v 
Subtracting tljis amount from.noon (11 :60) leaves 7 K34 a. 
n\, as the Noon M^rk for New York City, which is the 
same as saying that the planets' places are shown in the 
Ephemeris for 7:04 a. m. in New York City instead of 
for Greenwich noon. 

Nbtfe; The “Table ofc Noon Marks" on pages 78 and 79 
obviates the necessity of the foregoing calculations. 

Our example native was born at 11 a. m., therefore, 
approximately 4 hours after the Noon Mark (7:04 a. m.) 
so dq,e allowance must be made for the planets' addition- 
al progress through the zodiac in the 4 hours. As the 
Moort’s motion#is approximately degree per hour, in 
4 hours after the Noon Mark it would move forward in 
the ‘zodiac about 2 degrees. Add this to the Moon's 
place as given in the Ephemeris for Dec. 4th, 1910; 
% Moon’s longitude, 10:02 in Capricorn, plus 2 degrees 
motion in 4 hourt> equals the Moon’s longitude, 12:02 in 
Capricorn, at moment of birth. 

, Note: If the person had been born 4 hours before tht 
Noon Mark (about 3 a. m.) the 2 degrees motion of the 
Moon would be subtracted. 

The other pdanets more so slowly that by beginners 
they need not be calculated down any finer than their 
places given for the day in the Ephemeris. HoweVer, if 
desired, it can be done by logarithms, but often the 
equation can be performed mentally.

Table of Noon Marks — How to Use 

' Noon Mark is simply the mean local time at birth place 
when it is noon (12 m,) at Greenwich, England. 

Rule: To find the Noon Mark look iif an atlas and 
note the longitude of birth place. Then consult the fol- 
lowing Table of Noon 1 Marks, find that degree in the 
column of longitude and the figures next to it show the 
time of Noon Mark. Look to *he left side of longitude 
column when birth place is east of Greenwich rncf the 
time given as p. m. For places west of Greenwich look 
on right side of longitude column and time given as a. m.

GENETHIAGAL ASTROLOGY 


Placing the Planets in a Chart 

’Remember that the zodiacal signs are 0 apparently rising 
from the eastern horizon (cusp u of first house) and as- 
cending over thfe top of the chart (called Midheaven) 
and setting in the west. 

(m 

The actual motion of the planets is, therefore, just the 
reverse, r. e., they are moving under the eastern horizon, 
on through the Nadir (cusp of fourth, hopse, the lowest 
point in the chart), up and around the map through" the 
western half, except when marked R in Ephemeris, 
which means retrograde. But apparently the planets are 
carried around with the signs. Right now we are con- 
cerned with the actual planetary motion. 

When placing the planets in a chart use the degree of 
the sign on the cusp of a house as a guide : When a 
planej has approached to within 8 degrees of the decree 
on the cusp its influence begin^td operate in that house. 
This should be indfcated by running a ray from the 
planet to the number of the house as %hown in our ex- 
ample charts. To insure getting the planefs properly 
inserted it i^ a good plan to imagine trie signs and de- 
grees on house cusps as £ast or stationary and that ?t 
birth the planets were moving toward, or have just 
passed them, as the case may be. 

A Mental Eqfuation 

To Find and Correct Pl^ets' Places for the Amount 
of Time Born Before or After Noon Mark. 

The Moon*s # motion varies between 12 and 15 degrees 
per day. The average daily motion is about 13 degrees. 
To findf its actual mfttion on any date, Subtract its given 
ephemeral place Sn on® day from % that of*the next, if Dorn 
after Noon Mark, but Subtract from day preceding birth- 
day if bbiyiJbefore Noo?. Mark. The an^wSr is*motion 
in one day. 

t 

c * Multiply^he degrees qf the answer by 60,*<udd the min- 
utes if th^re are any, divide th^ sum by 24, and you, have

the AJoon's motion per ljour. This*amount is tfien used 
to correct ^toon's givfcn eghemer^ place on date 9 f birtlj

When ^Vloon moves 13® ^>er day its speed is 32%' per hour

When Moon moves 15“ per day its speed is 37%' per hour 

The other planets' places are usually easy to correct 
by mental calculation similar to the above, but for more 
precise work it is advisable to correct the planets' places 
by logarithms.

QENETHLSACAL ASTROLOGY 


LOGARITHMS 

t ‘ 

Correcting Planeks’ Places fron}. Noon 'Mark 
to Their True Places £t Tiifte of Bit'th 

When very precise and accurate work is required the 
planets* correct places ‘should be found by means of log- 
arithms, a table of which may l je found in this book and 
in back of Ephemeris for any § r ear. Jhe proper rule by 
which to use them is as follows ( 

Add proportional logarithm of the planet’s daily mo- 
tion to logarithm of the amount of time born from Noon 
Mark (these two logs, must always be added) and the 
sum will be the log. of the motion required. » 

Add this derived motion, in terms of \ongitude, to the 
planet’s given ephemeral place if born after Noon Mark, 
but subtract if born before 5 Noon Mark, and the insult 
will be the planet’s true places at moment of birth. Re- 
verse the above process for retrograde planets. 

Example — Person born December /rd, 1907, 4 hours 
rand 30 minutes after Noon Mark: 

Moon 28:30 Scorpio ori Dec. 4 

Moon 14:11 Scorpio on Dec. 3 , 

14*19 Motion in 24 hrs. log* .2244 
Log. for 4:30 after N. Mk. .7270 

.9514 

0.9515 is nearest logarithm that can be found in table 
of logs, which shows* at top of column 2'degrees*; on side 
of table we And 41 minutes; Mooli therefore moves 2 
degree^ and 41 minutes in the 4 hours^and 30 minfites of 
time born after N6on Mark, and this amount must be 
|idded to Moons ephemeral pla^.e on bltth, thus: 

*Mooi\ 14:11 Scorpio 

*2141 motion in 4hrs. ar*d 30 mihs. on this date 


^oon 16:52 in Scorpio, correct piace v artjtne of birfh 
and is so entered in thjis hirth'chart. 




PjiRT I. THE NATAL CHA RT tt 

The motion of flepeune, Uranus and Saturn js some- 
times so slf*w that jt canndt be computed by logarithms/ 
but mental ^quatior* is sufficient. 

•How to Correct a Planet’s Place by Logarithms 

Rule: Add proportional logarithm of the planet’s 
daily motion (distance traveled between two days) to 
the logarithm of. amount df time born from Noon Mark 
(these tfro logs must aftvays be added) and the sum will 
be the log. of motion required. Note the degrees and 
minutes represented by this logarithm, then add this 
derived motion, in terms of longitude, to the planet’s 
given ephemeral place if born aftcT Noon Mark, but sub- 
tract if born before Noon Mark, and the result will be the 
planet's true place at moment or birth. Reverse this 
process for retrograde planets.

PARS, FORTUNE 
Bart of Fortune) 

The Part of Fortune is a zodiacal point which is equally distant 
from the Ascendant as {he Moon is from the Sun in longitude. It 
benefits the house .in which it Is located at birth. 

The place of Moon’s node and the ten planets for any date 
may be readily obtained by 'referring to an Ephemeris, but the 
place # of the Part .of Fortune* must be calculated from the horo- 
scope itself. It Is used ateo In Horary Astrology, and its place 
In either a natal or horary chart may be found as follows: 

Rule: Add the longitude of the Ascendant to the Moon’s longi- 
tude from which sum subtract the longitude of the Sun, and the 
remainder will be the place of the Part of Fortune. 

August 23, 1884, 0:46 A. M., Elkhart, III. The ‘'Horoscope 
Illustrated” (chart No. 3) shows the Ascendant to be Cancer 4* 
18', which equals 3 whole signs 4 degrees 18 minutes from the 
first point of the zodiac (0*00' T). 

Th^ Moon Is 29° 52' in Virgo,. or 5 whole signs 29 degrees 52 
minutes from the first point of the zodiac (0°00' T). * 

Add these two positions in # tvrms of longitude, and from the 
$um subtract the place of the Sun. The result will be longitude 
of the Part of Fortifce ©. 

Asc.tfZ 4° 18' 3.04:18 

+ Moon TTR 29° 52' # 5.29 :52 1 

8.33 :70 

Less Sun tie 0“ 29' 5.90 :29 

3.33:41 = Place of ©. 

Transpose the 33 degrees by writing them 1 sign and 3 degrees 
and the answer \fill read 4 signs 3 degrees 41 minutes from first 
point of zodiac (0°00'#T). 4.03:41 = SI 3° 41' as place of ©. 
Find that place in the chart and ypter the symbol. It \rill fall 
in the second house. If the work iias been correctly performed 
It will prove the rule, namely, that © will be distant from the 
Ascendant the same number of degrees wtych the Moon is distant 
from the Sun. Test it. The Moon Is 29° 23' frogi the Sun. The 
© is 29* 23' from the Ascendant. 

Be careful not to rpake an error when cqpnttng from flast point 
of Arges. 

For instance: 

Aries 18.0:15. 

Taurus 15° ls.1 whole sign and 15* degrees. 

Gemini 15f is 2 whole Signs ant! 35 degrees, 

Goffcer 15° is 3 fcliole %lgns#and f 15 degrees. „ 

In other WQrfis, sone whole sign froip th^ first potrft of Arles ft 
tne 1st point of Taurus, (Infhience of % Pars Fortune is described

' ZODIACAL ASPECTS 

* a I 

A most '^failing experience of* the excitement of sublunary 
natures, by the conjunction and aspects' of the placets, has In- 
structed and compelled my un\fjlling belief. — Kepler 

Aspect Defined 

An aspect is a certain angular distance between 
two points in the zodiac ; from one planet to another 
or from a planet to a point in the chart, as for instance, 
a house cusp. Certain distances produce effective angles 
which are appropriately named. Astrology uses 16 as- 
pects, including the Conjunction and Parallel. 

An aspect is the angle between two planets at some 
time and place in their established outfits, and *their 
angular relation to the earth, the influence of which is 
registered or received by tHose individuals who are re- 
ceptive or keyed to those particular vibrations, according 
to the impress or iihpulse given their being at the mo- 
ment of their birth by the planetary vibrations pr^vail- 
«ing at that time and place. 

It has been observed that students just beginning tWe 
study of astrology usually find it difficult to master the 
f subject of planetary aspects and orbs. Because this is 
a basic feature in horoscopy, it 4 should bo ffilly, compre- 
hended. That its importance may # be realized, extra 
effortfis here ma^ie to famjliarize students with each de- 
tail that each step shall be thoroughly understood and 
the aspects^ correctly recorded. 

It makes arf immense difference whether an aspect is 
applying or sepsyating, that is, forming or waning. 

Aspects are divided* into two glasses, ’harmonious" and 
inharmonious, according fo the nature of the actior^ or 
th*e responses, they produce. Harmonious Aspects may 
be thougfit # of as pacifying* or mollifying/ whjle the in- 
harmonious aspects may b^ considered as stimulating^ 
it rotating. *The stimulating effect of the ihharmoniouf 
aspects is often seen i? the horoscopes of persons, who 



PART I, THE NA TAL CH ART 


81 


have "ma<ie their ,mark" w 6 r achieved distinction 6 y vigor- 
ous efforts/ On the’ other hancV in the horoscopes o # f 
persons w?yi a preponderance of harmoniousraspects we 
find they h*ave attained success no* so much by the Vigor- 
ous prosecution of business efforts as by" apparent "good 
luck" or fortunate circumstances. They "get the breaks." 
For instance, the old homestead vshich the folks acquired 
from the Government at $2.00 per acre and on which 
they worked hard for years to clear and cultivate, is at 
last willed to a nephew who finds that a town has grown 
up aroflnd the old place and it is now, without labor on 
his part, worth a fortune when divided into city lots. 

Not every one with a preponderance of good aspects 
is "successful” in life; some may be too easily satisfied 
or contented. Nor does every one with a preponderance 
of inharmonious aspects achieve “success" through stim- 
ulating effort; they may be too easily irritated and de- 
velop obstacles in their path through enmities, injuries, 
etc. 

* So it wiU be ^en that aspects are the modifying in- 
fluences in a horoscope. Unless aspects are properly 
computed the necessary modifications canhot be read 
atid the delineations not properly made. The influence 
of a planet in a “hguse" is qualified by the aspects it 
beholds. 

Much *of the skill and art in reading horoscopes de- 
pends upon the correct recording of aspects and knowl- 
edge of their influence. The »rst requisite is*the ability 
to calculate and collect the facts about aspects. Although 
such bdbks as the "A to Z Horosc&pe Maker and Delin- 
eator" give the text of the influence of each planetary 
aspect, the saying of Ptolemy should be^observed ^"Judg- 
ment must be regulated by thyself a$ well as by the 
science . 1 

Consider 'th.e, different applications of the»si}nfte aspect 
in sg veraUsuch cases as a man in the penitentiary, or one 
lying ill in a hospital • a >t>utli off on his vacation ; a mats 
ki charge o 1 A flourishing business; a woman chiefly 3 
concerned with her family of* children.

,

benefic?

above the aspecting planet, or below it in # the chart? 

13 — Art either of tt^se planets in their aigns of dignity or 
debility?

biryi.

An Orderly Tabulation of "Aspects 

Always count the shortest way through the zodiac in 
the chart, either forward or b^cfk, to figure an aspect. 
When a benefic planet is found to b t e retrograde (marked 
R in the Ephemeris,) its power for good is weakened. 
When a malefic planet is retrograde its malignancy is 
magnified. 


When figuring the aspects for a horoscope always Start 
with the Moon. Being the swiftest in motion she can 
apply to and form aspects with all the *>ther planets. 
Then take Mercury, it being next in speed. Find the 
distan/e between it and all the remaining planets in tlTeir 
order and see if any^ aspects a ¥e formed and carefully 
tabulate them. Then* in the same manner take up the 
aspects of Venus, Sun, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, ‘Uranus ^nd 
Neptune. Pluto being slowest and lasfr in order, can 
apply to none. 


When checking \ip aspects do the«work in this order:

BLANSTARY CTRBS 

• a 

The orb Si a placet or # the orb pi an aspect is ifs sur- 
rounding circle of influence. •Eight degAes forward and 
back of a planet constitute its orb. This amount of dis- 
tance in longitude applies to allrfhe planets except the 
Sun and Moon. (See “Orb of Influence Explained.") The 
Sun's orb is 12 degrees «when applying (forming) to a 
major aspect, agd 17 degtees when leaving it (separat- 
ing) /hswing passed tht? point of aspect. This rule applies 
particularly to the Sun's conjunctions, as it is likely that 
its orb (in major aspects) is perceptible to the senses 
only 12 degrees before and after an exact aspect. 

The Moon's orb is 8 degrees when approaching a 
major* aspect, ar*d 12 degrees when leaving it. 

• 

Thfs rule applies particularly to the Moon's conjunc- 
tions, as it is likely that in # actual practice its orb of per- 
ceptible influence is 8 degrees applying to and separ- 
ating from ma*jor aspects. 

When the Moon, for instanee, is traveling toward 3 
plsfnet in question, the sextile (>k) begins its influence 
when Moon is 68 degrees distant and increases in power 
as Mooii draws up to the exact degree of aspect (60 de-

one.

cupy the same degree of longitude, beyond which the 
influence of the aspect decreases in power and ceases a* 

8 degrees past the place of Jupiter.

Orb off Influence Explained 

Students sometimes wondev how a planet's orb of 
influence became known, tha*t is, the range in *dfgrees 
where an aspect begins to manifest prior to the Actual or 
partile aspect. For instance: The orb of Jupiter is said 
to be 10 degrees; the orb of the influence of Mercury, 
Venus, Mars, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune is 8 degrees, 
while the orb of the Sun is 12 degrees; the ftjoon 8 
degrees. 

One.of the interesting ways in which the extent ot orbs 
is ascertained, is by watching in an Ephemeris, the ap- 
proach of two planets to a conjunction and noting by 
newspaper reports*the beginning of events which coin- 
cide with the nature of such conjunction.* Observations 
of such phenomena began long befor^ there were* news- 
papers; lohg before printing was invented, which is of 
* comparatively recent origin. In fact, the invention of 
writing was largely the result of Jhe need to adequately 
record celestial phenomena with relation to seasons for 
planting, hunting, fishing, warnings qf floods, famine 
or pestilence. 

• 

Probably th£ reason tjiat Jupiter has a larger orb than 
the other planets (excepting our satellite the Moon, and - 
the Sun) ‘is because it is by far the largest anfcmg those 
planets. 

Jupiter, the * giant planet,” is (excepting the Sun), the 
largest body i£ our f solar system. It j? more thati three 
times as large and*.abc$ut thrde times as massive as all 
Ihe other planets put together. Y^t, such io th* prepond- 
erating *nfass of our Sun that Jupiter 1$ less than one- 
thousandth part of our central luminary.

able that the great Mississippi River floods begah about

Aspects Applying or Separating, # 

Application: (a) When a planet direct in course ap- 
plies to an aspect with another planet or cusp; (b) whim 
a planet retrograde applies to an ^pect ; (c) when both 
t planets are retrograde and one overtakes the other. 

Separation: Moving away from the partile; a dimin- 
ution# of influence because of dissolution of the aspect. 
In Horary astrology it indicates events just past, or that 
the matter inquired after will not occur. 


The” conjunctif>n*knd parallel, strictly speaking, a^e not 
aspects but position#. However, for convenience they 
4$e usually referred fo a# aspects. ^ 

Platic aspects are those which Sl re not exact, but yrith- 
4 in orb either applying or separating. 

Kartilf aspects are trfose which are exact.

• Exjeptifig the exact opposition, #11 # aspects are either 
dexter or sihister* Thaf is, an asypecting planet is either 
at the rigfh f (dexter) of the left (sinister)/of .those 
planets with whicH if m^y be making aspects. In#other 
words, it is cither travelling away from, or travelling 
toward those plane # ts. In the foregoing diagram the 
Moon is travelling away from ajl those planets which 
are above the horizon ; the Moon is to the right of them 
and consequently forming; dexter aspects. The Moon is 
approaching all. the plantrts under the horizon, is to the 
left of°them and thenrfore forming sinister aspects. 

Dexter and sinister positions also have significance in 
that the influence of the rays of an aspector planet must 
necessarily be of somewhat different quality or effect 
when it is moving away (dexter) from the aspected 
planet, compared with when it is approaching (sinister) 
such planet. But this difference is not sufficiently per- 
ceptible to define its effect ih nativities. 

i The two words theretore designate relatively the kind 
or position.of an Aspect (whether approaching or leaving 
the Body of another planet) rather than describing differ^ 
ent influences. Sinister aspects are soirfewhat more 
powerful than dexter aspects. * 

The positions “dexter” and “sinister” of the aspecting 
planet a^e ri^Uparticularly significant in natal astrology. 
They are mentioned here simply as information and not 
as a feature to be stressed.

PARALLELS OF DECLINATION 

Parallel:' Equgl ‘distance fropi the celestial Equator, 
north or south ; or one stellar body nofrth and the other 
gouth. 

The declinations of Neptune, \jranus, Saturn, Jupiter, 
Mars* Venus and Mercpry are given at top part of page 
in Raphael’s Ephemeris, «but be careful not to use latitude 
whtc% is given there also, as it is not used in these 
calculations. 

Where declinations of planets are given for every 
third day, if your required date is one not shown, re- 
member to make the necessary correction by subtracting 
one declination from another on the two given dates 
between which your desired day falls. The result is 
motion in 3 days; if you need to go 1 day farthq|r, take 
one-third of this motion, or for 2 days take two-thirds 
of it and add or subtract it from^date preceding birth 
► date according to whether the declination was increasing 
or decreasing. Raphael's Ephemeris for later years gives 
decimation eve*y other day. In Part II of # this book ar£ 
two pages of Ephemerides showing the declination *of 
planets. 

In the Simplified Scientific Ephemeris the declinations 
are given dh eight hand page, and in the Astrologer’s 
Ephemeris at bottom of page, for alternate days. 

Beginners need not try to» reduce tfte declination ot 
the above named planets to the hour and minute of birth, 
as it fs%ufficiertt now to record it ft>r the # day only. 

Declination for the luminaries is giv/m for ev^ry day, 
in the Ephemeris? and their true declm<|tion at birth time 
should be ascertained yi the samt manner as obtaining 
longitude, except where deettnatton is decreasing be- 
tween <f tie day and fhe next when it should be^tYeated as 
though re^rogfade, i. e., add instead of subtract; or vice 

ver &, as the caSe requires. 

• 

• To find whether or not any planets are parallel, (P) ' 
make’ a note of the declination of each planet^ as given
