t 

in the Ephemeris for date of birch under that heaaing. 
Work out Moon’s declination to date and a^so to time 
of birth, and then comoare them ail. * If you find any 
planets within ofte degree cf each other, (hey are said 
to be parallel. N. or S, (north or south) that is, on*} 
north and the other soitfh, or both north or both south, 
makes no difference. The object of working on the dec- 
linations is to compare them one with another to ascer- 
tain if any are in parallel; i.e.; in the same degree or 
within one degree of being the same. Parallels so 'found 
< are recorded in the list of aspects, as illustrated later on, 
because the effect of a parallel is of similar nature to a 
conjunction. 


We are very much inclined to give the name “good” to those 
feelings and sensations which register in our n^nds as pleasant, 
that is, when sensory nerves receive a stimulus, which, when 
transmitted to the brain and there reacting in the attitude w k hlcb 
we call pleasure, the resulting motor discharges and the chemical 
activity coincident thereto produce wuat the world chooses to call 
“good feeling,” hence, planetary angles which produce this char- 
acter of results are known by the general term * of “good” or 
“favorable” aspects. 

On the othei* hand, the vibrations of planetary angles which 
give our sensory nerves a stimulus that causes an attitude of 
displeasure are called “adverse aspects,” because the unpleasanf 
mental attitude thus aroused produces motor discharges in the 
mind and body which register an unpleasant feeling. Planetary 
angles or aspects are one 'of the chief influences jvltich direct the 
metabollstlc processes of the body. Anabolistic action registers 
as pleasant and is produced by “favorable”' aspects; catabollstic 
action registers as unpleasant and is produced by “adverse” as- 
pects. Herefn lies the crux «f how planets affect people. We 
find planetary vibrations giving direction to metabolism (expand- 
ing, contractihg and otherwise changing the quality 61 ‘bodily 
fluids), the incoming stimuli producing chemical changes noticed 
as feelings which induce attitudes of nund, — the attitude determin- 
ing the course or character of our acts, which in turn alsd make 
up our environmenti 

tPlanetary vibrations act chiefly upofi the nervous system, which 
astfologically is governed by, responsive related to, the planet 
Mercury, r 't£e« Messenger of the Gods.” The nerv&us system Is 
the messenger of the mind and through the ml nd*a re transmitted 
the Intelligent impulses which know how to direct the actions of 
rhio yarious organs of tJie body* so as to bring abcgit appropriate 
<- responses in the organs selected an<J the functions involved.

OENBTHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


ANALYZING u PLANETARY ASPECTS 

The importance of planetary aspects capnot be over-emphasized. 
To assist earnest students to jive full consideration to the matter 
of aspects, we reprint the following chapter from “The Astrolo- 
ger's Searchlight" 

During the course of correspondence between Sir Isaac 
Newton and Edmund Halley the latter took occasion to 
speak disparagingly of the astrological influences of 
planets and their aspects, whereupon Newton rebuked 
Halley by saying, “I have studied these things; you have 
not.” 

In the Jan.-Feb.-March issue of "The Astrological 
Bulletina,” 1933 (out of print), I gave a brief but sig- 
nificant biography of the great Kepler Whose voluminous 
writings portray his vastr labors in the interests of both 
astrology and astronomy. Kepler very carefully pursued 
Genethliacal astrology and compiled a diary of the events 
in his life which coincided with the aspects both in ma- 
ture and time. Were there not tru\h in -astrolqgy and

knows how to utilize knowledge and is capable of large 
responsibilities with possibility of great achievement,

clerk he can accomplish more.

. Scientific Deductions 

A fundamental value of astrology is embraced in the 
fact that it, better than any other means, explains the 
operation of natural laws as registered through planetary 
vibrations, and proves its hypqthesis by the correspond- 
ing conditions and events which are everywhere evident 
and continually transpiring.* Through the study of as- 
trology in relation to celestial and physical correspond- 
ences, or reactions* to planetary vibrations, a trained 
studenf is enabled to make logical deductioms and to 
develop his skill to a point of accuracy where it enters 
the fealm of science. In fact, he may do sq with a degree 
of accuracy which compares favorably with the percent- 
age of deductions that are derived from other branches 
of science. .The sfudent may so intelligently develop his 
faculty for deduction that in many cases he can as un- 
erringly predict forthcoming conditions as £n astrpnomer 
predicts the time of the next eclipse. 

In the* development of that Ptol&naic * # ‘inference” 
through the exercise of good judgment in the use of 
aspects, it is necessary to consider then* in relation to 
planet, sign and hoifse. In other wprds,*an aspect is 
never ju'dged of itself alone, but t alw^ys in connection 
with thf planet, sign ar$fl house from which it emanates 1 
and to whiA it*is % projected. 

Modifying Influences

abrupt 'expression, .positive assertions, discontent, rest-

square is between planets whose adverse natures are 
similar to a square, such as Mars, Saturn or Uranus, the 
undesirable features will be accentuated, and still more 
so if the planets involved are in angular houses of the 
chart and in signs in which they are not dignifi&dt The 
foregoing would be greatly modified and would be much 
less malefic if the planets were Mars in Aries and Saturn 
in Capricorn (signs in which they are dignified) and if 
they were in the least expressive houses (cadent) of the 
chart. Here we see how an adverse aspect may*exhibit 
its most malign influence and how that same aspect may 
be greatly modified according as it was contacted by sign 
and* house and the nature *0! the planets involved..- 

1 

The same premises may be applied to the favorable 
aspects. If the good aspect is formed by benefic planets, 
from signs in which they are dignified andTrom angular 
t houses, it$ best and strongest influence will be expressed. 
But if those planets are in signs not agreeable to their 
nature, and im> cadent houses, the opportunities for the 
benign expression of the good aspect is greatly lessened. 

4 «

Not *Law Evasion 

The student of* astrology is not seeking to evade na- 
tural law, nor to avoid responsibilities ; «n the contrary, 
he is studying to analyze and understand them in order 
td acquit himself ihore creditably and satisfactorily in 
his own conscience and at the same time be in a position 
to point the way to others who are seeking light. Al- 
though not neglecting reasonable and necessary precau- 
tions hi the emergencies of life, he is able to appraise 
or evaluate conditions and circumstances at their trie 
worth from the standpoint of spiritual unfoldment and, 
consequently t< is frequently able to discern the good in 
seemingly adverse aspects. The mere interpretation of 
a horoscope is insufficient ; indeed it may prove discour- 
aging or hurtful unless astrology is understood to be the 
means of pointiftg out the problems of life to be encoun- 
tered^ and that planetary influences should be manipu- 
lated in a hopeful and intelligent manner. In connection 
with astrology the study *of metaphysics will assist in 
developing those # attitudes which realize joy through 
visioij of eternal verities by controlling and directing , 
the reactions tcf planetary aspects, thus transforming 
adversity into blessings.

in Part III, f entitled, "Planetary *Aspects.” Also the 
chapter in Part? V, entitled, "Astrology — Is It Science 
or Religion?”

^ delineate a horoscope correctly?

STRENGTH OF, PLANET IN ANY CHART 
How*to Determine It 

A planet is at its best 'vsfhen located in its own home 
sipi, as Saturn in Capricorn, or Mercury in Gemini 6r 
Virgo. It is next best when located in the sign of its 
Exaltation, as Sun in Aries, or Moon in Taurus. It is 
not so strong when in the sj&n of its Detriment, as 
Venus in Scorpio or Aries, ft i| most •weakly ipeated 
when in the sign of its Fall, as Mars in Cancer, or Jupiter 
in Capricorn. 

When a planet is neither in its Home Sign, Exaltation, 
Detriment or Fall, its strength is judged accordiyg to 
its position by house in the horoscope, whether Angular, 
Succedent or Cadent. If Angular, its strength is the 
same as though in home or Exalted sign; if in a Succe- 
dent house it is the same as though in sign of Detriment ; 
if in a Cadent house it is the aame as though in sign of 
Fall. 

m The good or evil qualities of a planet # are accentuated 
by the aspects it may receive from other planets. 

• * f 

Jupiter, for instance, in its own sign, angular and well 
aspected, may bring the native great good fortune, when- 
ever well aspected by favorable “directions’/ or transits. 
But if in its fall, cadent and adversely aspected, it has 
little 9 r no power for good and the native may suffer lack 
of favorable opportunities (at needed times) and dis- 
credit and loss when Jupiter is evilly aspected by 
Erections. • ^

S. T. at birth. 

If birth oqcurred in P. M., simply add the birth time to 
the S. # T. of the desired date. * 

i — With the sum thus gained enter an appropriate P. 
of H. (for latitude nearest that of the t>irth place) and 
locate the S. T. whicfh corresponds nearest to that re- 
quired. Fill in the signs and degrees as given therein. # 
Fill in the remaining six cusps of the chart with the 
opposite signs and degrees. 

4 — Find the Noon Mark for fche place of birth. 

5 — Find how much time birth occurred befole or after 
the Noon Mark. 

6 — Find how fai; the Moon would Snivel in zodiacal 

longitude at the jrate of approximately one-half degree 
per hour, in the Tength of time between birth and Noon*

Thus we see that the planetary aspects, of themselves, are 

neither good nor adverse, they are Simply operation, differentiated 
principally according t<f the angles formed and the character of 
their product. It must be remembered that jve are not conscious 
of all our bodily reactions to stimuli, nor is every stimulus , trans- 
formed into conscious thought ; In fact, most of our bodily reac- 
tions are performed unconscigusly. 

Solar, lunar and planetary quivers supply the cells, through the 
media of the sensory nerves, their motility; the inherent and 
•acquired knowledge of the cells endows them with the ability to 
manifest their intelligence and so we have a Uvifjg organism or 
body through which and by means of which we can &press life. 

Different planetary aspects -or angles provide different sensory 
stimuli according as their ybrations differ in wave, Intensity, 
frequency and duration; or whether they are direct, Indirect, 
curved, straight, long y short vibrations. Iqpluding the, aspects 
which Kepler discovered, astrology uses sixteen planetary aspects 
or angles. Some are termed favorable atod some unfavorable, ac- 
cording to whetheclheir effects give us pleasure or displeasure. 
The aspects are Jlso named according to their difference in 
strength of influence.

HOROSCOPE DELINEATION 
• » 1 
The Knack of Doing It Correctly 

\t has been observed that many who learn to erect a 
horoscope are “all at sea” when it cpmes to delineating it. 
For that reason I am here giving my own method used 
effectively for many years. 

Th£v*ecret of horoscope delineation lies in the prepara- 
tion. That is, before commencing to delineate a chart 
make careful preparation by compiling a table of notes 
for guidance. , The more complete the memoranda, the 
simpler becomes the work of delineation. In the table 
of memoranda outline the elements to be considered and 
make notes with each element to be featured. The 
orderly table of Elements, the notes you make in connec- 
tion lyith them, together with references to “Delinea- 
tions For Horoscopes” in^Part III, will enable you to 
modify or adjust the readings to suit^he particular horo- 
^tope under consideration. Once more let me impress 
the student With the fact that the secret of success in 
delineation lies largely in the preparation* In other 
wcyds, make your table of Tnemoranda outline the* 
features to be considered.

house it rules in the chart. 

In makhig relerence to the chapter of “Delineations 
For Horoscopes” remember to keep your notes to the 
subject of finances.

manner as outlined fof finances. It will be* noticed that 
just as finances relate to the second house of a chart so 
are the other features related to the hotkjes in sequence.

on the various features of a horoscope, let us take for 
instance the subject of finances. Read the rule already 
given, then use the following chart for reference* and 
compile memoranda relating to the financial prospects.

or nature of planets, houses, signs and aspects, and wh© 
follows the preceding outline, should become an excellent 
horoscope reader.

Saturn conjunction withf second'cusp (same as Saturrf

of the earth, i^s Saturn ^is conjunction with ruler of

would also be a source of loss. As the fifth house rules 
love affairs there may also be monetary loss through 
such or through lawsuits (Moon opposing Saturn from 
seventh house) resulting from fifth house matters. 

Neptune in second house: Neptune is in adverse 
aspect with Jupiter posited in the eleventh house, which 
indicates loss through fraud, plots, schemes or treaohery, 
the failure of banks or bursting of speculative bubbles. 
Danger of loss through advice of friends or by entrusting 
money to theiji. ganger of loss through lawsuits con- 
cerning business enterprises. Jupiter ruler of seventh, f 
Neptune ruler of tenth house: Business partnerships 
hatf best be avoided, also financial transactions with 
friends.

of third house, indicating that money may be gained by 
obscure and patient endeavor through writings, science, 
large institutions and matters relating to the sea.

of professions or 'government appointment^ but should 
the native essay to rurf for political office his opponents

adverse aspect tef Neptune* Taken alone, Neptune in Leo. 
in second Jiouse denotes gain by turning his skill to mat*

Makl Your Own Chart 

Make your own horoscope^ (or a study chart you may 
prefer to use instead of your own data), embracing and 
showing all the conditions referred to in the first part 
of this lesson by the numbers 1 to 9, in chapter on 
“Making the Horoscope." Then study it as outlined in 
10 to 16, finally making a Memoranda as Shown for each 
of the features, to be followed by a complete delineation. 

Take plenty of time on this study. Work out one point 
at a^time and do it thoroughly and completely. When 
delineating your own chart do not read it simpl> from 
what you know of yourself, but hold fast to the princi- 
ples and procedure* already outlined. 

In the preceding chapters you acquiree! information 
,of the various elements, constituting a horoscope. You 
learned of the tendencies bestowed by the signs, of how 
the planets bestir those tendencies into characteristic 
action, and how their indications are modified accord- 
ing to the house occupied, by dignity o/ debility, and 
especially by their aspects. You learned what matters 
are ‘ embraced 4 in each o ( f the twelve houses of a chart; 
how the zodiacal sign^ add their special influence to 
house and planet. In the Table of Memoranda you were 
shown how to correlate these elements practically, pre- 
paratory to* delineating a chart. If you find it difficult 
to compile thA notes in the Table of Memoranda for 
each element to be considered (the* notes which are to 
guide you in bringing put the features of the chart and 
to help ^you stress Ur modify certain points as you study 
the v&no’is readings in “Delineations For Horoscopes”), 
the difficulty encountered is evidence ihat at this stage 
of your study where yop should possess a fund ol infor- 
' nation of the elements before-mentioned, your trouble 
is due to deficiency in that respect. 



PART I, THE N> # TAL CHART 


m 


Fortunately, this difficulty dai\ tfe overcome. Turn 
back to the prficed^ng* chapter^ and study everything 
related to Jhe influences and indications employed in 
horoscope readiifg, As before mentioned. 

9 The process of erecting a chart is not difficult, neither 
is the reading when approached by the method provided 
in this chapter. It may be properly stated that the secret 
of correct horoscope delineation lies in the compilation 
of memoranda # for referearce and guidance. 

No instructor or book can give the student the ability 
to synthesize, but the proper procedure can be outlined 
to develop this needed faculty. The art of synthesis is 
like good judgment or intuition : it cannot be handed 
from one to another; it is a growth within the person, 
an expansion or extension of knowledge, as a result of 
careful study &f authentic texts, assimilation of the in- 
formation thus acquired and application of the pr^iciples 
thus learned. 

i But if the fundamentals have b^en correctly assimil- 
ated; if the *stucfent has paid the necessary attention to 
the Initial steps of study and thereby thoroyghly learned 
die characteristics of the planets, their dignities and 
debilities; the tendencies and properties of signs and 
houses; the quality «nd influence of aspects; and if the 
various elerpents of the chart h^ve been properly coh 
lated so .they Can be readily analyzed by means of the 
Table of Memoranda, the student should be able to com- 
bine these various elements with logical judgment* in the 
form of a delineation embracing the salient features of 
the horoscope. 

In the next chaptef will be found a natal chart with the 
principal calculations for its erection forked out in se- 
quence to serve is an example of what has been taught 
jn previous chapters concerning fhe making of a natjd 
chajt, 

The calculations are followed by a delineation which 
is'iiftended to show how *he reading may be^condensect 
instead of tfeating each feature, separately as was t>ut- . 
figed in this, chapter.

Note: To find daily motion of planet in a nativity 
where birth is after Noon Mark, always subtract planet's 
place on birthday from that of the next day. If birth 
occurred before Noon Mark subtract longitude of day 
previous from that of birthday. This rule applies only 
to the nativity, *for in a progressed chart we must always 
subtract place on birthday from that of the next day in 
advance, regardless v>f whether born before or after Noon 
Mark.

We have now illustrated the method of delineating a 
chart and shown in their logical order the points gener- 
ally of most interest. In ordinary practice the “physical 
description” may be omitted. However, to. make a reg- 

* ular study of a chart we would proceed to look up the 
iofluence of all the aspects, all the planets by sign, all 
the planets by house, and the rulers of all the houses as 
they are posited'-in the various houses of the chart. Study 

• of the nativity does not end here as transiting planets, 
(shown in current year Ephemeris) are traversing 
through different houses in the natal chart and constant- 
ly forming aspects with ylanets in the radix thus bring- 
ing up new events, new conditions, new states of being, 
and a’S the student watches these he will realize, at times 
with awe, the stupendous working order of the laws of 
nature. 

Further research is made by means of a yearly chart 
or Progressed Horoscope in which the monthly aspects 
of the Moon indicate the trend of affairs, and mutual 
aspects ^ejween the planets designate the 'quality of the 
year as a whole, whether the lessotl to l ’be learned will 
be through pleasure or , limitations. 'These points are 
tuny covered in chapters on Aspects, Orbsf, Transits and 
The Progressed Horoscope. 1

ADDITIONAL AID TO HOROSCOPE READING 

In relation td the, “Example for Compiling Memoranda 
in Preparation for R^adin^a Horoscope” t fie topics listed

forming correct judgment of fhe horoscope. 

House Cusps 

This is a matter that cannot be confined to set rules, 
as the “cusp” or dividing line between the houses is a 
mundane thing; in reality there are no dividing lines in 
the heavens. In fact, it should be treated as a psycho- 
logical influence and has a close relation to the entire 
chart. 

In the ancient texts on astrblogy it was generally ad- 
mitted that when the Sunjiad reached 28 degrees of a 
sign its influence was mainly in the ®ext sign. By way 
oT example, if the Sun is in 28 degrees of Virgo we use 
Mercury as the ruling planet, although the characteristics 
of Libra ^yould be very noticeable in that per&m's rnakq^ 
up* 

Judging the Mentality 

The mental rulers are the Sun, Moon and Mercury, 
although all of the planets have an influence on the njind, 
especially the ruler of the Asceifldant, rulers of third and 
ninth houses. Also Saturn and*Uranus if in aspect to 
the mental ruler^. Read their delineations according 
to the aspects they may # behold.

Here is a simple but accdWte rule which needs for its

ble and difficulty. If the aspect is a good one, the result? 
are likely to be favorable.

Difference In degree of development or understanding deter- 
mines, to a large extent, the difference In human response to 
planetary aspects. One who is ignorant of Natural Law will 
blindly follow his own imperfect conceptions of his feelings, urges 
and conditions, and then rant about his “bad luck.” Another 
individual, under the same planetary influence, who understands 
the workings of Cosmic Vibrations, is apt to be much less disturb- 
ed and a great deal more fortunate. • The one follows "fate”; the 
other anticipates it and profits to the degree of his knowledge in ( 
the refinement of his response to planetary vibrations. In other 
words, Ignorance impels people to do many regrettable things 
which the application of wisdom transcends. 

No one should feel discouraged because they find a number ctf 
adverse aspects In their horoscope. They are In every horoscope, 
and for a purpose. They remind us that ttoere is work to be done, 
battles to be fought and w # on, difficulties to be overcome — and that 
In victory over self lies sweet reward. In othenwflrds, weakness 
and faults denoted by adverse aspects should not be accepted as 
a handicap, but rather regarded as a challenge to develop latent 
powers and faculties. 

By usinf adverse aspects, instead of being antisea by tnem, man 
may win his*battle of life through sheer merit of persAtent, de- 
termined effort, encouraged by the wlsdojn of the ages, astrology. 
The horoscope of many successful men and women show that they 
won success, not because of good aspects, but because they over- 
came and utilized tlie so-called “adverse” aspects. 

Jhe aspects are perfect, but man’£ responses *are mostly poor 
or imperfect, and the pc#)r responses to the aspects sqoII oppor- 
tunities fbr perfect happiness; and so ne brings* hi ifiself into 
conflict, carter than into harmony with Nature# and does so to 
his owjff detriment. *

PART II, THE PROQgtegSED CHART , IBP 


THE PROGRESSED fiOROSCOPE ‘ 
Theory and Technic 

The Progressed Horoscope is a chart made for any 
year after Birth, in which one m^y read the conditions 
of that period, according to astrology. 

f 

The horoscope of birth and the progressed chart may 
be lifoened unto 'a boolp of information. The index tells 
what*the volume contains and the chapters give the de- 
tails. The birth chart is like an index, the progressed 
chart like a chapter; and as a book contains only that 
which was indexed, so from a progressed chart should 
be read only that which is indicated at birth. 

From the location of the planets in the zodiac at birth, 
their aspects and the positions in the horoscope, «n in- 
fluent is exerted throughout the whole life. This fact 
can easily be discerned by watching the transit of a 
planet over natal positions for over^he cusp of a house 
in the natal .chart}, although the planet itself may long 
since have moved away from the original place. 

• 

The reason for the permanent influence of the planets 
is, as one writer says^ "Because these influences are in- 
herent in the very structure of the physical body. All 
atoms and mofcecy les of the physical 'plane, and all mater- 
ial combinations, whether solid, liquid, gas or ether, are 
classified in terms df planets gnd zodiacal signs. 1 The 
physical body has been built up of these combinations, 
and it forms a highly complex whole, which exactly 
corresponds in composition to the sfellar positions (lo- 
cations and aspects) fit birth, and responds to their 
vibrations. The horoscope, therefore, is registered in the 
body itself, and lasts as long as life endures.” (See chap- 
ter in Part V entitled, "Change •of Residence.”) 

Astfhe Jbirtji map may be considered permanent be- 
cause it represents the structure and compositidh«of the 
bodjj (the physical vehicle and individuality),* so mfejvi a 
• syste/h of "progression* b<? considered valid because*it 
represents the clfanges taking place in the living being 
within the limits.imposed at birth. # 



160 


GENETHLlXCAL ASTROLOGY 


The twelve zodiacal figns apparently rise and set dur- 
ing the twenty-four hours of each day. The planets 
continually traveling through the zodiac change their 
relation one to- another and also to the places held at 
birth. For instance, if the Sun were in the first degree 
of a sign at birth and Jupiter in the' twentieth degree of 
the same sign, the Suri would arrive at the place held by 
Jupiter in the nineteenth dad- after birth. 

With regard to the conjunction used 'as an example: 
Jupiter being a benefic and the conjunction a good one, 
the effects would be very favorable but if Jupiter had 
been heavily afflicted at birth no great good results 
would occur. (See "Progressed Horoscope Aspects" in 
Part III.) 

The changes among the signs and planets after birth 
are classed under two heads : 

a 

First : The rotation of the earth on its axis apparent- 
ly causes the signs t» move at the rate of approximately 
1 degree every 4 minutes, so in the course of 24 hours 
the whole zgdiac of 360 degrees has crossed over the hor- 
izon of every point on the earth. In those 24 hours the 
earth has also moved forward in its orbit approximately 
one degree and «by so doing it consumes approximately 
4 minutes of time more than 24 hours. Reference to the 
S. T. column in an ^phemeris shows that ‘Sidereal Time 
advances about 4 minutes per day in accordance with the 

earth’s daily orbital progress of one degree. 

• 

The ’Midheaven progresses uniformly, but this is not 
true of the qfher Hbuse cusps due mainly to inequality 
in the shape of the earth, except &t the equator. There- 
fore, each day Jaunted forward in the Ephemeris to re- 
present one year of life changes the ‘Midheaven one de- 
gree each day. The? ottoer cusps change in their proper 
sequence as indicate by the Tables of Houses. 

The*original places of the planets in .the natal chart 
an^nanged by this motion, # but not* their longitudinal 
places. For instaace^ if* a planet were, in conjunction 
with the degree on the cusp«of the first house at birth, 
every fgur minutes (ever^ year) that degree and planet

will appear t6 be carried up one dfejjree into the 'twelfth 
house and ifs distance from the horizon and meridian 
would change. The planet just pientioped would be

conjunction, etc., with what was the degree constituting 
the Midheaven (meridian or ci'sp of tenth house) at birth. 

t 

The second change which takes place is caused by the 
planets' actual movement in the zodiac. In this way a 
planet may move away from its location in the zodiac 
at birth and form new aspects to other planets; also to 
its original place in the radix. The measure of time for 
a chart made by this seeming movement is one day for 
each year of life, i. e., the aspects formed during the first 
day indicate the events of the first twelve months of life ; 
the next day represents the next 12 months, and so on. 
The elements taken into account by "direction" ar3 the 
places of Sun, Moon, placets and house cusps. The 
Ascendant and Midheaven are of moje importance than 
tfle other cusps. Asi y of these may progress to an aspect 
of any other, or to an aspect of its own place in the birth 
chart. 

\&e use the word "position" for place in the chart, and 
the word “location" foi* place in zodiac. 

The word "rfcdwc" means root or beginning, and since 
after birth # the signs and planets all move away from 
the places occupied irf the birth .chart, and because they 
all move at different rates, they .form new aspocts, not 
only to thg various fixed places in the birth horoscope, 
but also among themselves as they advance. % Therefore, 
a distinction must be mtide between any planet or cusp 
as it exists in the map for birth, and the«same one as it 
is placed after haviftg moved away from the place at 
birth. • # * ' % , 

j ,

in the birth chaijt, and the term "progressed” Indicates 
the places arfd condition} in the progressed chart.

First, directions from progressed planets or cusps to

Second, directions from progressed planets or cu<,ps 
to progressed {Janets or cusps. 

The motion of the Moon is approximately 13 degrees 
per day (per year by progression), consequently it will 
require about 2 )/i years to progress through one sign of 
the zodiac. Tints in approximately 28 years it will pro-

it originally held at birth. The Moon’s aspects formed

Illustrating* the Process 'of 
‘Casting the Progressed Chart 

Progressed Birthday , # Sidereal <Time And Signs 

' to erect a ‘'progressed” chart, refer to an Ephemeris 
for the date and year of birth, and *count each day after 
birth as one year in life, i. e., a chart made for the first 
day after birth would show (by planets' positions, as- 
pects, # etc # .) the conditi<yis to operate between one year 
and two years of age; a chart cast for the second day 
after birth would show the conditions for the twelve 
months following the second anniversary, and so oo 
through any number of years. 

If you wish to erect a chart for the twentieth year of 
a person born on the first day of the month, count 
twenty days after birthday, and it will take you t<* the 
twenty*first day of that month. A chart made in the 
usual way for the time of bfcrth will show the conditions 
for the twelve months following. 

Example 

4:3(? a. m., August 17, 1909, present birthday.

which is equal to 33 (Uys after birth. Thirty-three days 
added to birthday (Aug. 17) briftgs us to Sept. 19, 1876, 
as the progressed birthday for tvhich a chart is then 
iqade in the usual ^ay. 

After counting forward in the Ephemeri^ the required 
number of days (for the age desired), youthen make the 
calculations to S. T? for this date in exactly the same 
manner as when*rpaking the birfh horoscope, i. e., you 
find thg S. T- of birth Recording to ht>w much time the’

• houses ii\to a circular blank* chart.

Latitude and Lorvgitude of the Progressed Chart 

* t 

ft , 

Tiie progressed chart is always erected for the latitude 
of birth place. The* progressed chirt is a projection of 
the birth chart. The latter is based upon zodiacal condi- 
tions affecting that particular latitude (latitude of"the 
birth place) which*' are “cast” in the individual born 
there at that time, and throughout the life there is an 
Intimate relation to the conditions so cast. See chapter 
on “Change of Residence” in Pjirt V. 

Placing the Planets 

Having placed the signs and degrees in the chart from 
the Table of Houses, turn to the Ephemeris and copy the 
planets' places into the horoscope, first taking care to get 
their correct locations according to the time of birth, by 
how, much time birth occurred before or after the noon 
mark of birth place. 

The goon’s ftlonthly Motion 

« 

Before proceeding to figure out *the various aspects 
between the planets, find the motion of the Moon on the 
Progressed birth date by counting how many degrees 
and minutes of longitude it will travel between its given 
place on this 'day and its given# place on the next day. 
Then change this a, mount of Moon’s motion from degrees 
and minutes to all minutes by multiplying, the degrees 
by 60 and adding the minutes to this sum. Dividing 
the total by 12 shov/s fhe rate at which the Moon will 
move, forward in the zodiac through the chart in each of 
the twelve months during this progressed ye*ir. Adding 
this a mou nr! of Moon's monthly motfon twelve times t6 
the place of Moon at birth hour on this progressed birth- 
day will shovC in what degree the Moon will be each 
month during the corning twelve months. 

* The average motion of the Moon is about thirteen de- 
grees pt r year, which is equal ^to 1“ 5 r longitude per 
rn^rth by progression. In locating the degree occupied 
-by the Moon each mpnth,. begin ybur count fvr>;n the 
actual birth rfirtnth* and date, because the progressed 
birthday represents the real 1 birth date. In the foregoing-

These are termed Lunar Aspects and they are very im- 
portant. By working in the foregoing manner on? may

It makes no difference in the nature of an aspect

that is, the square will always be adverse, the sextile 
always good, etc. It should be remembered that tfie orb 
of aspect by progression is bdt two degrees. 

It is considered that the Sun, Moon, Mars, Jupiter, 
Saturn and, Uranus, in particular, passing in aspect by 
direction to the places of the planets at birth, or passing 
their own radical places, or passing progressed places 
of the planets, are very important and bring about events, 
especially if the planets were in an aspect of similar 
quality at birth. Mercury, .Venus, Ndptune and Pluto 
are important also, but to a lesser extent.

PROGRESSING THE* CHART 

It is presumed that the student now understands how 
to erect and read a natal* chart (tfce radij) so we turn to 
Chart No. 3 which is the radix of the progressed chart 
we are about to make. The latter is illustrated in Chart 
■ No. 4. It is advisable to read this* process over carefully 
and then with pencil and paper work the entire example 
out, two or three times if necessary, until your work 
corresponds with the illustration. By doing this it will 
be fftund that the wcfrk is not at all difficult. Anyone 
with ordinary education and persistence can learn to 
perform it correctly. 

Progressed Birthday — How to Find 

August 23, 1910= year for which a chart is desired. 
August 23, 1884=birth date. 

26 years, yhis is equivalent to 26 days 
after birthday; 26 days added to # the birthday equals 
^September 18, 1884, and a chart made as though the 
native were*born on this date at 0:45 :46 a. m., will repre- 
sent his 26th year from August 23, 1910, tc? August ^23, 
1911. 

Note: When counting the number of" days after birth- 
day, remember not to count the day of birth as one year, 
for the child is riot a year old until 12 months after birth. 
A chart made for tbe date and time of birth shows the 
conditions for the child for thte ensuing twelve months. 
A chart made for one day aftef birth represents the life 
between the first an< ^ second anniversaries. Likewise a 
chart made for 26 days after birthday represents the life 
between the 26th and 27th birthdays. ^ 


The table on pafee 692 is very handy foj finding the date repre- 
senting the* progressed yjpar. For instance, in above example 26 
days after ’the Tiirth date (August 23) is September algtli.

Aspects by Direction in the Progressed Chart 

The next step 1§ to find and tabulate the Moon’s aspects month 
by union tfi. It can be dtf>ne easily and quickly if you use an 
uspectariau blank and disc. 

In a blank aspectarian (a circular ^hart containing the 
twelve signs 'and the 360 degrees marked therein) 
the planets from the radix and the progressed charts 
against the proper sign and degree. Attach the cardboard 
disc, turn the arrow to Moon's progressed place on first 
month of prog, year and make note of what aspects, if 
any, tire pointed out. Move arrow forward one degree 
(representing the Moon's* motion in one month) and 
^again note the aspects, and so o* for the remaining 
months of the yesfr. 

Place the arrow against each of the progressed planets, 
ir^ their order of speed, and note any aspects which may 
be forming within two degrees to either radical or pro- 
gressed planets. Aspects just past do not count. Allow 
but two degrees* orb applying for *a major aspect ; one 

degree orb for a minor aspect. 

• 

It is well to insert the radical places in black and the 
progressed in red for distinction. Also mark the As- 
cendant ind M. JC, of the radix and* progressed charts. 

•

INFLUENCE OF THE MOON'S NODES 


The motion is retrograd^at rate of about three minutes 
of longitude per day. The Nodes ^y-e of some value in 
Natal astrology a»d useful in the Horary branch. For 
the benefit of those who might wish to investigate the 
subject for themselves, we give what is considered tfyeir 
influence. (See also page 47.) 

The Moon's North Node passing the place of or form- 
ing a major aspect with the radical place of Jupiter, 
Venus, Sun, Mooli or Mercury is said to indicate benefits 
to the native. Passing in sextile or trine to the radical 
Neptune, Uranus, Saturn and*Mars is also said to be 
beneficial. Its square and opposition to the benefics, 
conjunction, square and opposition ty the malefics, por- 
tend evil according to tjie houses occupied by it and the 
nature of the planet afflicted. The Mood's South Node 
crossing over any planet is evil for things ruled by the 
house occupied ancl the planet) thws afflicted. As the 
Nodes doubtless 'have perceptible influence, these same 
testimonies n?ust hold true also in relation to tbe pro- 
gressed horoscope. Refer to chapter under thltf Reading 
in Pfitf III-

* PARALLELS’ OF DECLINATION 

m * 


Tha next step is to work up tt\e decimations to nnd 
whether any parallels % are formtd during this progressed 
year. 

Declination of planets may be found at top of page in 
Raphael's Ephemeris and is given for every second or 
third day, except Sun and Moon, which are given for 
each day on lower part of left hand page. In the Amer- 
ican Ephemeris (Simplified) the Mediations are given 
on right hand page. The Ephemeris usually gives the 
latitude of planets also but the calculations for this pro- 
**gtV&*e d horoscope work do not require latitude. 

To find whether or not progressed Moon will come to 
a parallel declination to its own radical place in declin- 
ation, # or to the radical or progressed declinations of the 
other planets during the progressed year, proceed as 
follows : 

When two or more planets have tfae same degree of 
declination they are parallel ; whether north’ or south or 
onj north a*id the other south is immaterial, for if they 
arc equal distance from the celestial equator they are 
parallel and the # eflfect is the same as though the planets 
were in conjunction. 

Parallels may be found not only between .planets as 
they were located at birth, but also as they may progress 
and come to the degree t>f another planet's declination 
in the ratfix or in the progressed chart. 

As the Moon is fikely to move # several degrees in dec- 
lination any da§' (equal to a year in progression), she is 
liable to form ^fcveral parallels in the course of such a 
period. The influence pf a parallef is likely to begin 
when within one decree of being complete, but is most 
af>t to operate when the parallel becomes e # xaoJ. 

The^algulation and tabulation of ^ecKnations for the 
progressed thart are identical in method to those the 
radix but we go’ i step further when the Moon is con- 
cerned.* The Moon's declinafion is treated similarly # to

187 


its progress in lopgitude* month by* month in oWer to 
ascertain whether it will come Into parallel with the. 
declination of any planet in either tfie natal or progressed 
charts during that yeaV. *When it does ccjme into a par- 
allel the effects are similar to a conjunction between the 
same planets and should be delineated in the same terms 
as a conjunction. 

To find Moon's declination each month in the pro- 
gressed year, proceed as follows : Refer to Ephemeris 
for desired date* and snake note of Moon's declination 
for that day and the day following. Subtract one from 
the other to find the distance traveled, reduce this 
amount to all. minutes by multiplying by 60, divide the 
result by 12 to find the motion per month, then adcfthisT 
amount twelve times to Moon's Declination at birth time 
on progressed birthday if the declination is increasing 
in size of degrees, as per Ephemeris, but subtract this 
amount if the declination in Ephemeris is decreasing. 

When the motion of M®on in declination is less than 
pne degree for any year (as will sometimes be the case) 
multiply the *giv<?n minutes by 60, thus reducing the 
motion to seconds. Divide the seconds by 12 and we 
have the number of seconds of declination Moon moves 
per month. Divide the seconds by 60 to transform the 
figure to minutes anc^ seconds of motion in declination 
and then add or subtract this amount from Moon's place 
in declination? apt birth time on progressed birthday, 
according *to whether Moon is increasing or decreasing 
in declination. 

After having made the column of figures showing 
Moon's cfegree af declination each month, compare its 
declination each month with the degrees ft f declination 
of other planets in both the radix and # # the progressed 
charts to ascertain # in which months the parallel aspects 
will be formed^ that year. It Is qtiite possible that in 
some years theri will be no parallels in operation. / 

When the* Ephemeris shows that declir«a4^n will 
change from north to south or vice versa, hftween the 
two* dates, add (instead of*subtracting) to find the daily 
(yearly) motiorf. Then from it£ place on progressed 
birthday subtract the monthly motion until it is^reduced

for remainder of the r year. 

To Find Declinatiop of the Cusps for Either Radix 
or Progressed Horoscopes 

The cusp of a house (sign and degree) has the same 
declination as the Sun when it is in that sign and degree. 

Use the Ephemeris for the progressed year, i.‘e., if you 
are making a chart for the 26th birthday which falls in 
1910, use the 1910 Ephemeris for reference regarding the 
J5un’j declination of any particular degree in the zodiac. 

The progressed chart has 25° 57' of Cancer on the 
Ascendant. Took in the 1910 Ephemeris and find when 
the Sun will be in that sign and degree, which is July 
19th, •and its declination is 20:59 North. This is the 
declination for the cusp of the Ascendant in thfc pro- 
gressed chart. Put the samfc figures on the opposite 
cusp, i. e. ; cusp of seventh house, and so on in like man 
ner with all the other cusps. The fifst, tenth, seventh 
and fourth # cusps are most important in both radix and 
progressive charts. 

The declinations of cusps are pot to be used except 
when the time of birth is known to be absolutely correct 
or proved so by a reliable method of rectification. Every 
four minutes of time change the cusps one" degree in 
longitude and consequently the declination changes also. 

When .you have found all the declinations and made a 
list of the? parallels mark them in the list of aspects be- 
side the month in which they faril due. After this has 
been accomplished give a delineation of the elements.

Declinations of House Cusps

of a house has the same declination as the Sun would 
have in that same sign and degree.

to find declination of cusps in the progressed chrrt,

this case use the 1910 Ephemeris, as the 26th birthday 
occurs in that year.* When any planet has declination 
equal to that of a cusp it is parallel therewith*and affects 
it with an influence similar to a conjunction. 

Unless*Tables of Houses for the exact latitude of birth 
place wese used ui erecting the char£, and birth time 
known to be* exact, it is useless and perhaps misleading 
also to attempts use cusp declinations. For this reason, 
and also others? it is extremely difficult to get the dec- 
linations absolutely texact and hence to state just when 
an event would occpr, indicated by an ericoming parallel, 
except, to state it in terms of a certain njonth ot year. 
However**/ a parallel usually takes about a year to com- 
plete ( excepting those of Moon) and wliile'the parallel 
is on it influence# matters tonnected with that house.

cusps, nor a progressed major planet with its radical 
place* 

A planet is in influence of parallel with another planet 
Vs soon as within ,one degree, regarcfless of whether both 
are north of south or one north and the other south. 

% When a planet is parallel with a cusp and also its Op- 
posite cusp, delineate the parallel in terms of the house 
which is in same direction (N. or S.) flf declination as 
the planet. 

Declinations must be within one degree of being equal 
in order to be parallel.

or with a«cusp, gamine the £pheme*is and your figures 
carefully to see whether the parallel is foitning, or has

which are applying or formir/g. Observe whether a 
planet's declination is increasing or ^decreasing in ordef 
to determine* if it is*approaching or separating from * 
parallel.

GENBTHfrIACAL ASTROLOGY 


When the decliriatym is decreasing between date of 
progressed birthday, and the next day, treat it as you 
would a retrograde planet. 

It is not absolutely necessary to work out exactly what 
day or date the parallel falls 'due, ^because its influence 
extends over an uncertain period, varying according to 
its motion in declination, and it may begin to exert a 
perceptible effect several weeks before birthday to sev- 
eral weeks after. Some declinations change so slowly 
that when a parallel begins, by orb, it may be many 
days (years by progress) before it becomes partite and 
therefore show up*in as many years’ progressed charts. 

Parallels seem to affect conditions which the native 
will find it necessary to meet, and these conditions are 
accompanied by events, more or less radical, according 
to the native’s plane of progress or state of consciousness. 
For instance, Moon parallel Mars would produce a fever- 
ish, passionate, rash disposition in an undeveloped type 
living on the purelr animal plane, which would lead to 
accidents and quarrels, while to an' advanced, refined 
being this same parallel would produce added energy 
resulting in constructive activity. In the one case the 
influence is apparently evil, in the other the effects *are 
good, the difference being not iry the planets but in the 
state of understanding of the being through which the 
influences operate. In other words, the sort of reactions 
which an individual makes to an aspect largely deter- 
mines the results of thec.influence. * Some people make a 
bad aspect worse by the way they think and act. Others 
greatly jnodify an adverse aspect by their, intelligent 
response to (planetary influences. *

TRANSITS ' 

The transit of jl planjt is its passage by ephemeral 
motion, in the current year as shdwn by*the Ephemeris, 
oyer some important place in the chart, either radix or 
progressed ; or its aspect to such points. 

In a progressed chart the effect of a transit over (con- 
junction) the degree of Midheaven, Ascendant or any 
planet, is more effective than an aspect thereto. 

In a nativity, transits by either conjunction or aspect 
are noticeable, the major aspects more particularly than 
the minor. 

There is little difference between the effect of a transit 
and the same aspect by direction, the difference being 
rather in time of duration than difference in influence, 
although, because aspects by direction last longer than 
those by transit, the latter may seem different. For in- 
stance, Saturn conjunction cusp of fllh house by direction 
might last elver a year, during which time, unless offset 
by other testimonies, it would likely produce physical 
disability, obstructions, limitations and restricted action, 
which might result in serious illness; jvhile the transit 
of Saturn over sixth fcusp or over planet in sixth house 
might cause awsevere cold or an attack of "flu,* but com- 
paratively .speaking, its effects would soon pass. If the 
nativity indicated much ill health, the native would likely 
suffer frequent spells of indisposition during Jhe whole 
period of its passage through the sixth, which is the 
house of •sicknes*, and the person would feel* especially 
indisposed on the day« when the Mooh {as shown in 
current Ephemeris) passed through thi^Tiouse and over 
Saturn therein. 

To study the effect of transits, lnarn the effect of as- 
pects* by direction, but modify the delineatiops because 
of the shorter, duration, and never predict s&nething 
fron? a trarfsit which is not promised by somf configura- 
tion at birth. If a person has § good horoscope for 
health, finance qr marriage, there is no use predicting 
trouble * because of a malefic, transiting through first. 




202 


OKNKTHI/JACAL ASTROLOGY 


second or seventh ' hqpses, because nothing more than 
slight Annoyances are likely to occur. 

When looking up transits, the declinations of the 
transitor should not be overlooked, for when it compares 
with the declination of any planet or cusp in the horo- 
scope it constitutes a parallel and’ has an effect like a 
conjunction c 

Duration of Influence 

The duration of the effects of transits depends upon 
two things. 

First, note whi<?h planet is doing the transiting, for 
each planet moves through the zodiac at its own rate of 
motion, the Moon being swiftest and Pluto slowest. 
Their order of speed is: Moon, Mercury, Venus, Sun, 
Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune, Pluto. 

Second consideration : Does the transitor tun? retro- 
grade before it gets out of c\\b of aspect with the trans- 
ited? Frequently ji transiting planet reaches a certain 
point, turns retrograde and recrossec that point; then, 
turning direct in motion, crosses it for the third time 
tlvas affecting that place or planet much longer than 
usual. Look in the current Ephemeris and count how 
many days it requires the transjtor in question to tra- 
verse 8 degrees preceding the exact aspect. After the 
aspect is complete, 'the effect quickly ^diminishes, unless 
the transitor becomes retrograde and goes back over the 
aspect. It takes Neptune several years to form and com- 
plete an^ aspect, while Venus or Mercury would cover 
thesame amount of longitude in a few days. During a 
period of gpod directions* the potency of good transits 
is enhanced apd the adverse trahsits diminished in influ- 
ence; and vice* versa. 

A progressed or* directional aspect is technically due 

* {o operate when the aspect is exactly complete, ♦ but the 
•transit of a planet over the place*of a directional* aspect 
which^s still forming (within a degree or so) may excite 
it into action prematurely. To illuctrate, suppqs^ that 
Saturn by its progressed rAotion is close to forming a

Future Conditions at a Glance 

In every month the transits of W, V, *?, U, & should 
be noted to see if any of them cross over the planets in 
the radix, or form major aspects to them. Or if they 
cross over or form major aspects to some planets Ih the 
progreSsed chart which are themselves in aspect, as in 
that case the transit sets trte progressed aspect into ac- 
tivity during the period of the transit. But generally 
speaking, transits through the radix are the most impor- 
tant. 

fhe transit of the Sun through the radix is important. 
The good and adverse* aspects which it •forms to radix 
planets indicate the favorable and, unfavorable Solar 
periods in ejtch yeAr. The Sun moves forward about one 
degree each day, hence, it is twelve days applying to an 
aspect. Do not seriously consider the minor aspects. 

The transits of Mercury and Venus are not very im- 
portant unless thfey are rulirig planets. They concern 
the minor details of life, when considered as transits, 
yet in the lives of people who are concerned mostly with 
ordinary matters th£se two are quite^ important. In the 
lives of those who are concerned with larger things,* 
business and public # affairs, the lirger planets are^ 
more important. The transit of the natnte*s«%niling* 
planets (one.rulmg birth month and the one which rules 
the ak&nding sign at birth) are veryjmportant. The 
aspects formed by rulers in transit, ‘which coincide with 
assets they held'at birth, are very powerful compared

with other aspects fthey mky form. For instance, if 
. Mars Ss a ruling planet and was in opposition to Jupiter 
at birth, then the adverse transits of Mars to the radix 
Jupiter wou^d be v^ry strong, white its good aspects to 
Jupiter would bring but little benefit. 

Look in a curre it Ephemeris and take note of the 
various planetary positions, then compare their ephem- 
eral places with radix planets to see if any aspects are 
formed. Note the date in Ephemeris when the transiting 
planet begins the aspect (allowing an orb of eight de- 
grees applying) and the date when the aspect is com- 
plete (exact). This will show the length of the period 
of the influence. 

The influence of transits should be judged according 
to their radical indications, i. e., if a planet is in adverse 
aspect to another at birth its adverse aspects to that 
planet by transit are of more significance than its good 
aspects. In other words, its adverse aspects being in 
accord with the c/iginal indication would stir up corre- 
sponding conditions; whereas its good aspects being 
contrary Jo the original indications would not have the 
jiower to produce any particular or noticeable advan- 
tages. To illustrate an Adverse Transit over a good 
Progressed Aspect, let us consider Venus progressed 
sextile Sun progressed and an adverse fransit would be 
the coming of Saturn to a conjunction Venus, but Saturn 
would at the same time be in sextile to Sun, which is a 
good aspect. This transit would have the effect of de- 
laying for the time being the influence of the good aspect 
between Venus .^and Sun, as set down in the regular 
canons of astrology and wouldo produce disappointments 
in matters ruled by the house then occupied by this 
transiting Safurn. The good aspect of Sun standing by 
would prevent any vefy serious results from the transit 
of Saturn over Venus. The good aspect of Saturn to 
"the progressed Sun would tend *o benefit matters ruled 
by the nouse occupied by Sun. On the other hand, a 

{ food transit over a good direction would accentuate the 
avorable vibrations 6f thfe direction and it would be 
especially noticeable on the days whe c h the Moon also by 
transit formed good aspects to this combination. *

Aspects by .transit which coincide with the opginal 
(radical) indications will aot director upon the native 
and in his affairs. Aspects which do not coincide will 
act indirectly : not uf>on the native and his* own affairs 
but more upon other people whom he is contacting dur- 
ing such transit. All "aspects operate in some manner, 
but whether directly on the native br on others about 
him depends upon the original (radical) indications be- 
tween the two planets in question. If in adverse aspect 
at birth the good aspect )>y transit is not likely to bring 
the native any particular benefit but he will see others 
benefiting in a manner coinciding with the nature of this 
aspect as related to its transiting house and sign. For 
example: If the aspecting planet is transiting through 
seventh house in the sign, Scorpio, forming a good as- 
pect to the radical Moon with which it was in adverse 
aspect at birth, it might benefit his partner or his wife. 
If he happened to be engaged in a lawsuit at that time 
it woula benefit the opponent and if the Moon at birth 
ruled the third house and w^is posited in the eleventh, 
thc?n that good Jtran^siJ would also benefit his brothers, 
sisters, neighbors and his friends. On the other hand, 
if that good transit concerned planets that ha<J been in 
gooc^aspect at birth then the native would benefit direct 
ly through the matters and people mentioned above and 
might also be in a position to help them. *

As Venus never is more than 48 degrees distant from 
the Sun, and Mercuvy never more than 28 degrees, their 
period is similar to that of {he Sup, namely, one year. 
However, difce to Stationary and retrograde positions 
these periods are only approximate. For accurate horo- 
scope work the transiting aspects of planets to points in 
.a chart require reference to an Ephemeris. * 

Usually, in compiling a list of transits, Venus, Mer- 
cury and Moon are omitted when dealing with major 
influences as they are chiefly indicative of favorable and 
unfavorable days lt ,while the more ponderous and slower 
mojdng planets mark the periods. However, all transits 
are important and delineation of their effects should be 
made in terms of the directions in operation. Transits 
aid in determining the dates when a given direction (pro- 
gressed aspect) is most likely to become operative. 
Tratfsits to the Part of Fortune and the Moon'^ Nodes 
should also be observed, especially the conjunction. 

How to Find and Use Transits 

, An easy way to find transits and record them is as 
follows : 

From the birth chart make a table for permanent use, 
showing for each planet the degree of the zodiac which 
is in exact aspect of conjunction, sextile, square, trine 
and opposition. Then look in current Ephemeris and 
note when any planet v^ill pass through the degrees you 
have recorded. In that way you will know the dates 
when your radix*, is affecjted by trapsiting planets and 
thus be enabled to prepare yourself according to whether 
the aspect fortned is good or adverse. 

For example: Suppose at birth' you had Mercury in 
Aries 26 degrees, the sextile to that is pemini 26 degrees, 
Aquafius 26 degrees; the squares is Cancer 26 degrees, 
Capricorn 26 degrees ; the trine is Leo 26 degrees, Sagit- 
tarius 26^degrees; the opposition is Libra 26 degrees.

days should be ^voided for important affairs. ,

A Transit Illustrated 

Transits over the places of planets in a natal chart or 
forming aspects with them are very effective and like- 
wise so in the progressed chart. 

Transits to the place of planets which are forming a 
progressed aspect are very important. To illustrate this 
latter point, turn to Example Horoscope No. 4 and note 
that th^ progressed Mars is forming a trine to the radical 
Ascendant. Read the delineation of this aspect (page 
184) which will be effective • from August 23, 1910 to 
August 23, 1911. However, during the year other pro T 
gressed aspects also operate, some of ^rhich are of con- 
trary nature, consequently, the effect of the progressed 
Mafs trine Ascendant radix will be most noticeable when 
the aspect is supported or accentuated by transits of 
similar nature, that is, good aspects. 

To calculate such supporting transits- make note of the 
signs and degrees which favorably aspect the place of

FAVORABLE DAYS 
In Relation to Ycgir Own Horoscope 
How to Find Them 

Favorable and unfavorable days are indicated by the 
dates when Moon (as shown by her place in current 
Ephemeris) crosses over or aspects important places in 
the nativity. * 

The Moon's aspects are the most important factor of 
all in determining the daily events in one's life. Observ- 
ing students will soon learn how to apply these to their 
own horoscope, as the Lunar transits are all of monthly 
occurrence. 

Look in the current Ephemeris at the Moon's lcmgi- 
tude on*the first of the month. Find that place in your 
own Radix chart, then note* what aspects it will form 
to^your Radix planets during the da^ considering that 
it will move forwarcf in the zodiac about thirteen degrees 
in the next twenty-four hours. 

Tfte Moon's place as listed in the Ephemeris is its 
position at the time of /ioon mark of tho place where 
you now reside. After noon mark, the Moon will ad- 
vance at the ratfc of* about one degree? every two hours. 

Noon Mark is the mean time for which the planets' 

locations are inserted for each dafy in the Ephen\eris at 

some particular place. It is the equivalent to noon in 

Greenwich. For instance, on April 6 , 1934, the Ephemeris 

shows Venus in 0 # 06' of Pisces at noon, Gr^erfwich. At 

120* W. long, that is the position of Venus # at 4:00 a. m. 

In other words, the floon Mark for places situated at 

120 # W. long, is 4 a. m. on any date.* (Noon Mark is 

defined in Part I.J 
• 

The Moon transiting a trine or sextile with thfe*place 
of the Sun in. your Radix is generally an indication of 
one of'ttie best days in the month in 3 business way, 
provided at the satpe time ttye Moor t does not form an 
adverse aspect with some other planet, and particyflarly 
with malefic planets in the radix. •

The^ Moon conjunction with youi' Sun. is good also, 
providing the Sun «at birth' was unafflicted. The con- 
junction is good for business, ( but not as good physically, 
as it sometimes tends to lower the vitality. When the 
Moon is forming adverse aspects to your Natal Sun, r the 
day is adverse for new undertakings or for dealings with 
people of high position, and is especially adverse for 
asking favors. 

The Moon in good aspect to, your Jupiter is next in 
power to her good aspect with the place of the Sun. 
Including the conjunction and parallel, the good aspects 
to Jupiter are considered among the best which occur 
monthly. These aspects favor general business and 
social activity. 

The adverse aspects to Jupiter are not to be consider- 
ed a‘s very malign, yet they incline to misjudgrpent, ex- 
cess, extremes, extravagance or over-estimation in busi- 
ness calculations or decisions. 

«> 

The Moon’s good aspects to Venus are next in power. 
T.hese, as.' with Jupiter, include the conjunction and 
parallel. On the days when this aspect occurs, it mukes 
one feel happier, more optimistic, more sociable and 
more inclined to entertainment *than usual. Very good 
for visits and dealings with the feminine sex. The ad- 
verse aspects to Venus are not very important, but still 
they disfavor the thing's just mentioned. 

The Moon transiting an adverse aspect with your 
natal Neptune, ISranus, Saturn or Murs are all unfavor- 
able indications, the worst orfe being the aspect with 
Saturn. No pew business should be undertaken when 
the Moon is in adverse aspect with the place of Saturn 
at birth, if it can Be avoided, for its tendency is to bring 
disappointments, • loss, hindrance, delays, vexations, 
anxiety* The good aspects to 'these planets tend to 
benefits through the things which they ruje. 

The Moon’s* aspect to Mercury or to its own place in 
the Radix, is not very important. flowever,, wheij as-' 
per.tinjj Mercury, it inclines the mind to excessive mental.

activity, and pei^aps causes the' native to be given more

In other words, on a day when the transiting Moon 
favorably aspects a planet fs a good time for matters 
or things indicated by that planet; on the other hand,

is afflicted by the Moon. As stated before, the most

transiting.

i% forming favorable aspects. Or at the time when there

Moon’s Transit Through the Houses of a Nativity 

The advantage of knowing Moon’s place ^nd aspects, 
as tfinnected witfi your nativity, lies in thfe fact that it 
has much dominion over the geaeril ‘daily affairs. Its 
influence is important because it goes entirely around 
your chart each month, through each house, and. forms

every aspect to all the planets. Look in current Ephem-

and effects will be in operation, gives ,us the opportunity 
to decide a course of action in keeping with the indica- 
tions. 

The house through which the transiting Moon is pass- 
ing in the Radix is important, as ft helps to color the 
‘aspect with the nature of things ruled by that house. 
For instance, if the Moon is passing through your second 
house ai:d forming a good aspect to your natal Mercury, 
that would indicate a good day for dealing financially in 
matters connected with literature, advertising, printers, 
publishers; for visits, neighbors, kindred, etc. Usually 
when tfye Moon passes through the sixth house of the 
horoscope, the native does not feel quite up to his ♦nor- 
mal physical stamina. Should the Moon meet with heavy 
afflictions when passing through the sixth house, natur- 
ally it would indicate sickness or physical indisposition 
of the kind indicated by the planets aspected. 

If the Moon is making a good aspect in your Radix* 
but on the same vlay is making adverse aspects in the 
zodiac, the good influence is weakened, and vice versa.

baily Transits 

By daily transits we mean those transits which occur 
each day in the zodiac among the planets themselves 
without reference or application to the chart of any in- 
dividual, although t in a general sense they may be so 
correlated. • 


* An Ephemeris and the “Moon Sign Book'* record the Moon's 
daily position in the zodiac. t It requires no great among? of 
horoscopal knowledge to be able to tell by these means just 
what house the Moon may be transiting in the individual natal 
chart and the principal aspects formed, and thus keep in touch 
with the changing influences, to great advantage in numerous 
ways. Observations of this nature provide the best proofs of 
Lunar influence, for, as the Moon goes from one house to another 
and form£ the various aspects, events will be seen to occur and 
feelings to change correspondingly? 

Vfe have here been speaking of the transifing Moon as applied 
to the natal chart, and, were it not for certain modifications, the 
same conditions, events, etc., would reoccur with monthly regular- 
ity, except that the date of the phenomena would differ. TlSe 
moditfbation is that of the Moon's daily "aspects" to planets in 
the zodiac which establish the general nature gf the day and 
those horoscopes which correspond to that general condition are 
the ones' which most strikingly feel the effects of Lunar Influence 
for that day. And*hereln lies the value of noroscopal knowledge 
in that respect,* for it will be readily seen that if the general 
conditions are excellent on a certain date, but an individual's 
horoscopal conditions very adverse, tile native need no£ expect 
much of either testimony; whereas, without being able to note 
the contrary testimonies, that Is, if the native based his judgment 
of the day upon the one set of Influences without coiuidering the 
other set he might at some tifde, sooner or later, 1^ disappointed 
In results expected.

* SOLAR * PERIODS' ' 

Good* and Adverse 

As the Sun in its apparent daily motion transits 
through the zodiac it movds away from the place it held 
on your birthday, passing through one sign each month 
and at the end of a year returns to place of birthday. 

In the sixth mopth from your birthday the Sun comes 
into opposition of your zodiacal sign of birth and its own 
place in your nativity, and tends to produce a trying 
time generally. Not a good time to m^ke any important 
move or ask favors. In the third and ninth months it 
forms a square to your sign which is also an unfavorable 
period. 

Your Adverse Solar Periods Each Year 

(Approximately) 

From 78 to 95 days after birthday. 

From 168 .to 185 days after*birthday. 

From 258 to 275 days after birthday. 

T^iis seventeen day period, which occurs three tinifcs 
each year, is not a good time to make important moves 
or ask favors. The "averse” days in th£se periods are 
apt to* be more 50 than usual, while tjie “good" days are 
less favorable. Orf your “Astrological Wall Calendar" 
make a list of these periods for use with your good and 
adverse days. 

Your Go$d Solar Periods Each Year 

(Approximately) 

From 48 tf> 65 days after birtfiday. 

From 108 to 125 days a'fter* birthday. 

From 228 to 245 days after J>j rthda y- 

From # 28§ to *305 days after birthday. 

In these four good # periods aim to extend your # interests 
and advance your business.® Your good. dayS in these 
good periods are ap t to be more favorable than at other 
1 times, while your adverse (fays are usually less severe.

PART II, <THE PROGRESSED CHART 


INFLUENCE OF PA^S, FORTUNES 

Rule for ’finding place ’of Part of Fortune is given in 
Part I, and its influence in the twelve houses is described 
in Part III. 

Apparently it has no influence on the character or 
health, but is said to benefit any house in which it may 
be posited at birth by gain in things ruled by such house, 
subject to the quality of aspects it receives. 

Benefic planets by progressed motion or by transit* 
passing over or favorably aspecting the radical Part of 
Fortune are a favorable influence. 

Malefic planets in like manner passing over or adverse- 
ly aspecting it foreshadow loss. 

* . 

As, shown by the method of computing its place, its 
position depends partly <ya location of Moon, and as the 
Moon in the progressed'chart is constantly moving for- 
*ward and travels# about thirteen degrees during the year, 
it follows that the place of Pars Fortunae in progressed 
chart w r ould likewise be constantly changing. Due to- 
this there is likelihood of error in placing it, so it may be 
omitted, if preferred^; in fact, like the Moon's Nodes, 
many astrologers do not use it at all, except in Horary 
astrology. We gjnention these pAints in order to aid 
those who desire to make practical research into the 
matter. In our own experience we have found these 
factors important and deserving of serious attention.

7 1 

E/JLIPSES 

Are Eclipses Effective Ordy in Natal Charts, 
or Do Theji Also Affect the Progressed Chart? 

Eclipses affect that chart most in which they form the 
strongest aspects. Generally speaking, the natal chart 
is more responsive to New Moons, eclipses and transits 
than the progressed horoscope. For instance, if an 
eclipse occurred in such sign and degree that it formed 
' no aspects in either natal or progressed charts, its influ- 
ence would be felt through its house position in the natal 
chart rr^rc than by its house position in the progressed 
horoscope. 

A New Moon, an eclipse, or a transit may cause an 
aspect forming in the progressed chart to be “set off” 
prematurely if they come into a like aspect with that 
formed by the planets. However, there is really nothing 
premature in Nature. If the eclipse, New Moon, or 
transiting planet should form an aspect contrary to a 
progressed aspect in process of forming (coming to com- 
pletien) it would tend to retard the latter or modify its 
influence during the time such transit, New Moon cr 
eclipse would bq effectively in operation. 


A chapter (ft 14 pages entitled* “Eclipses/* will be fo^nd In 
“Powerful Planets.’** In the same book a chapter of 20 pages is 
devoted to “Comets,” which gives< dates of che appearance of 
famous cipaets and the effects of their visitation. 



PART II, THE PROGRESSED CHART 


225 


RECAPITULAtlbN 

The Progressed Horoscope is a chart made fof any 
year after birth to J show {he natuie of conditions which 
will affect the life during # the twelve months of that year. 

Each day after birth is equal to one year in progres- 
sion. 

In the birth year Ephemeris count forward as many 
days after birthday as, you need to equal the year de- 
sired. Having found the desired date for progressed 
year, cast a chart as though you werj born oil that day, 
using birth time and same T. of H. used in makjgg the 
natal chart. 

Correct the planets' places by logarithms, using their 
longitude on day for which chart is made and next day 
in advance, regardless of whether birth occurred before 
or aftey Noon Mark. 

List the aspects, remembering to reject all those which 
h?ve passed the point of completion, and allow two de- 
grees for orb .of aspects between planets ; one degree for 
the minor aspects. 

Calculate the declinations and record all parallels. Al- 
low 1 degree for orb. 

Record the important transits for the year of progres- 
sion, using EphemCris for that year instead of birth year 
Ephemeris. Allow 8 a degrees for orb of transit aspects. 

List all the influences, favorable and unfavorable, by 
aspect, parallel, transit and Moon's monthly position in 
longitude. * 

Delineate each influence separately in terms ot position, 
location and dignity of the planets concerited, and weigh 
their strength and pdwer. 

State the outstanding influences, wither favorable oj; * 
unfavorable.* 

Synthesize # the.horoscope by condensing your.analyses 
into aepneise judgment of the year as a whold. Do not 
predict anything from the progressed Chart that is not 
indicated in the natal chart. Remember— "The •Stars 
indftie; they do not qompel.”

Wilson's Dictionary of Astrology, “In which the var- 
ious systems and opinions of the most approved authors 
are correctly defined" (1885, p. 356), quotes an example 
in the nativity of Charles V, Emperor of Germany, by 
i the renowned astrologer, Placidus. Herein Placidus 
demonstrates the ^importance of the influence of the 
following, in the "day for a year" progression: 

Progressed planets forming aspects among themselves. 

Progressed planets forming aspects to planets in Na- 
tivity^ (Sun is classed as a planet.) 

• 

Progressed Moon from itr monthly position forming 
aspects to both nafal and progressed planets. 

Progressed declination of Moon and planets forming 
parallels among themselves and with planets in the radix. 

Transits as affecting radix and progressed positions. 

Declinations of transiting planets forming parallels to 
radix and progressed declinations. 

Lunations as affecting radix and progressed charts, 
both in longitude and declination. 

Placidus did not outline them in this order but in his 
example every one of the foregoing was pointed out, 
while Wilson adds the general opinion that the pro- 
gressed planets should be observed as they aspect the 
places of the angles in the radix (cusps of 1st, 10th, 7th 
‘and 4th houses). , ‘ 

# 1 . - , * j 

Placidus used these progressed features in connection 
with another method of directing '(primary) said 
that unless one Qf these prbgressed directions occurred 
at the same time, th£ primary direction would be of little 
or no Importance. 



.PA RT II, THE PROGRESSED pHART , , 227 

Which gives rise to the belief that it is these pro- 
gressed influences, considered and applied in the m&nner 
enumerated and tafVight, that are, rafter ail, of primary 
importance. 

The late Alan Leo, famous English astrologer, said, 
"This method of progressing the horoscope not only 
includes the Primary and Secondary Directional Systems 
within itself, but also adds new factors of vital interest.” 

This method in olden days was called "secondary,” 
but it has beei\ so improved by modern researches* that 
it has assumed a place of primary importance, and is 
now generally termed The Progressed Horoscope. 

Here let me give students a friendly warning not to 
be in the least disturbed by two statements in a recent 
(1931) astrological treatise: (1) "Progressed planets do 
not aspect each other,” and (z) "Like progressed planets, 
transiting planets aspect the radical planets of the natal 
chart.” 

The second statement means that transiting planets 
do not aspect progressed planets. But every experienced 
astrologer knows otherwise.* 

"Progressed planets do not aspect each other?” (1) It 
is unthinkable! To oruit the influences of the progressed 
aspects one with another in the progressed chart, would 
be omitting valuable information which should be in 
possession of the client by me^ins of which he m ay plan 
accordingly before it is time to act. Imagine how you 
would feel if you paid an astrologer to m^ke your pro- 
gressed horoscope ajjd he omitted all mention of the 
progressed Moon's aspects to all the* other progressed 
planets, progressed Ascendant and tMidheaven, pro-< 

• The rules and examples given in "Practical Astrqjogy For 
Everybody” or the “A to Z Horoscope., Maker,” wbeft put into 
practice, will be convincing proof of the ppwer and influence of 
both motions referred to, and wilt place the student in a position 
to sur that anyone who says otherwise reveals their lgck of 
fLCtual experience in horoecopy, or a disregard for complete com- 
putations and accurate analysis. 



228 Q ENETHL IACAL ASTROLOGY 

\i 

gressed, Part of Fortune, progressed* declinations, and 
the mutual aspects • among the 'planets on progressed 
birthday. (2) Picture the further deficiency in the Pro- 
gressed Horoscope if# the Traflsit aspects to Progressed 
Planetary Aspects were omitted! You would feel that 
you had received but half a delineation. Without these 
features the Progressed Horoscope delineation would 
be misleading because incomplete, thus failing to indi- 
cate many important conditions which would eventuate 
in that year, and therefore not Jtruly 'representative of 
the values which astrology has to offer when rightly 
practiced. 

Another Method of Bringing Out “Directions” 

If your nativity is very “radical” (such as having the majority 
of the planets in angles or cardinal signs,) then the directions of 
Midheaven and Ascendant to radix of these planets are very 
important. 

To get this direction you simply move the cusp of the tenth 
house forward one degree for each year, but leave the planets 
as they were at blrth f , 

t 

In a “radical” nativity the transits of planet* over the radix 
ar£ very important 

If the nativity is not radical, the progressed cusps to the<pro- 
gressed planets' f places are most important, and the transits of 
ruling planets over these progressed places. 

For instance : If yotf wish to direct cusp? to radical planets for 
twentieth year, move the tenth radix cusp forward 20 , then And 
this new M. C. in Table of Houses for the same latitude as birth 
place, and arrange other cusps as shown therein from that M. C. 
Insert pfanets in the same tigns and degrees of longitude as at 
birth tthe house positions of some will be changed,) and note if 
any planets are creasing or inspecting cuspp of- 1st,' 10th, 7th or 
4th house particularly, and judge accordingly. To illustrate : We 
will suppose tnat Uranus was 20* beyond fourth cusp at birth. 
By moving tentti cusp forward 20* (one degree for each year) 
which automatically move* the fourth cwp likewise, it will bring 
the fourth cusp to a Conjunction with the radical Uranus, indicat- 
tag a sudden chang^ of residence or demise 6f a parent
