from it. Find this last result in figures of R. A. in Tables 
of Right Ascension and- note what zodiacal sign and de- 
gree of longitude jit represents ; then enter an appropriate 
Table of Houses with that sign and degree on cusp of 
tenth house and fill in the other cusp^ of the chart from 
the Tables of Houses in the usua! manner. 

When your work is complete and the rectified chart 
made by either of the above processes* the “Meridian 
Distance" of the planet must be equal to the A. E.,-* re- 
gardless of whether the planet was east or west of the 
lower meridian. 

"Meridian Distance" is the distance between the R. A. 
of the meridian (R. A. M.) and the R. A. of the planet. 
Subtract one from the other and if the remainder is equal 
to the A. E. the work has been done correctly. Do not 
direct to the lower meridian with any aspect but the 
conjunction. 


Rectification by ASPECTS to the Midheaven 
• 

If you find, by the position of the planet in the approx- 
imate chart, that the event was not caused by a conjunc- 
tion to one of the meridians, but by an aspect, count the 
number of degrees the .planet is from the M.-C., make 
note of this figure and call it the meridian distance, or 
M. D. 

Note: Count the nurtiber of degrees the chosen planet 
is from the M. C., rpake a note of the figure and call it 
the M. D. ; next jot down the figure of the A. E., then 
refer to. the Table of Right Ascensions and find the R. 
A. of the planet which rules the house of the matter in 
the approximate chart and jot it down with the other 
two* *You now have the necessary elements with which 

to begin tjie operation. 

• 

Rule for Planet East : If the chosen planet is east of 
the M. C., (between second and tenth cusps on left hand 
side of^hart) Subtract the A. Er from the M. D. (When 
the A. E. canpot be subtracted from the M. D. the rule 
mtSst.be wQdjped by conjtviction instead of by aspect.)

Then take the dqgfees of an aspect nearest to this num- 
ber, (when this figure comes very c(ose to a tie between 
two aspects, try the rule to both aspects and decide on 
the one which nearest answers to the requirements of 
the case) and add them to the A. E. Then subtract the 
sum from the R. A. of th^ planet. The answer will be 
the R. A. M. Now turn to the Table of Right Ascensions 
anfi find what sign and degree of the zodiac this derived 
R. A. M. represents. 

When once you know what sign and degree are to go 
on the Midheaven, the rest of the # work is quite easy. 
Just enter a Table of Houses for the proper latitude, find 
that desired sign and degree on®the cusp of tenth house 
and then fill in the other cusps of the chart from the 
Table of Houses in the usual manner.

Planet West of Midhfcaven 

When the chosen planet of the event is west of the 
M. C. (between tenth and sixth cusps on right hand 
side of chart) the process of working the rule is the re- 
verse to the method just given for a planet east. 

Rule: Find the R. A. of the planet; find the M. D. of 
the planet; find the A. E. Add the A. E. to the M. D., 
choose the aspfect nearest this figure, subtract the A. E. 
from this chosen aspect and add the remainder to the R. " 
A. of the planet. The answer will be the R. A. St. If 
the suii comes to m^re than 360, subtract that circle 
from it and use the remainder as the R. A. M. 

•

figure and find what sign and degree of the* zodiac it 
represents, then find that sign and degree on qusp of 
tenth house in appropriate Table qf Houses and from 
that point in®the tables cast the chart in the usual tyanner.

Experiment with scores of known birth times proves 
(as in one example given herein) that Uranus and Nep- ' 
tune rule Aquarius and Pisces, respectively, and* that 
Satum;and Jupiter do not, as some authors would have 
us believe. Investigation shows, also, th§t the tredecile, 
aspect. (108 degrees) though much neglected, is impor- 
tant and shcyuld be observed not alone in this work, but 
also in directions. Likewise the other minor aspects.

The R.*A. of a planet varies slightly in accordance with thq 
planets latitude, for instance, Page 513 glve&$ R- A. 201* 16'. 
If the latitude of Venus on that date was 1 21 K the equivalent 
R. A. would he 201* 45'. For simplicity in these examples the 
planets' R. A. is given without, or regardless qf, their latitude. 
The tables of R. A. on pages 518—21 quote R. A. "without latitude. 
Midheaven degrees have no latitude. Those who delfre to work 
these rules with greater precision will find in the book “Recti fica- 
* tien of The Horoscope," by DeLuce, tables of R. A. for latitudes 
.1* to 6* N. or S.

Explanation 

# The first column on the* 16ft designStes the degrees of 
zodiacal signs. On the same line.with the zodiacal degree 
in the six columns, will be found figures of sidereal time. 
Directly underneath the sidereal time are the figures of 
corresponding Right Ascension, or Arc, as it is sometimes 
called. The last figure in the R. A. refers to tenths of 
a minute, i. 6 seconds of R. A. A plzyiet in 2 degrees 
of the* sign Taurus would .have R. A. 29.49 ;04. When 
2 degrees of the sign Taurus are culminating on M. C 
the S. T. is 1.59:18.

Explanation 

• 

The first column. on tly^Jeft designates the degrees of 
Zodiacal signs. On the same line with the zodiacal de- 
gree, in the six columns, wilf be found the figures of 
sidereal time. Directly underneath the sidereal time .are 
the figures of* corresponding Right Ascension, or Arc, as # 
it is sometimes called. The last figure in the R. A. refers 
to tenths of a minute, i. e., 6 seconds of R. A. A planet 
in 2°. of. the* sign, Scorpio, would have R. # A. 209.49:04. 
When V of the sign, Scorpio, culminate on M. £. the 
S. T. is 13.59:18.

(Continued from page, 517.^) 

When in an approximate chart you find a planet 
posited in the hQuse ruling the event, it i§ better to take 
that planet as the ruler of the event rather than the ruler 
of the sign on the cusp, providing* of 1 course that the 
planet ruling the sign is posited in somfc other house. If 
the correction is to be made by event of death and yot\ 
find Saturn in the eighth house, take it as the planet to 
work by rather than the planet which pales the house of 
the person. In the case of event of sickness, and Safcurn 
is found in the house of sickness, take it rather than the 
planet which rules tht house denoting the person. If 
several planets are in the •house ruling the ihatter, take 
the one which best describes the matter or the one hav- 
ing strongest dignity therein, if the ruler of the sign 
itself is not there. 

Remember that the time derived from either of these 
processes is mean local time, i. e., real or Sun time, and 
not standard or clock time. There is a difference from 
one to thirty minutes between mean and standard time 
hrthe various hour zones in this country, as can readily 
be seen by reference to a Zone Time Map. 

The aspects that can be used are the vigintile, 18 de- 
grees ; quindecile, 24 degrees ; semi-sextile, 30 degrees ; 
decile, 36 degrees; serrfi-square, 45 degrees; textile, 60 
degrees; quirftile, 72 degrees; square, 90 degrees; trede- 
cile, 108 degrees; trine, 120 degrees; sesquiquadrate, 135 
degrees; bi-quintile, 144^ degrees; quincunx, 150 degrees; 
and the ’conjunction. The opposition asoect is^not em- 
ployed. The aspect* are always direttea to M. C., but 
conjunctions may be; used on Either M. C. or Nadir.

How to Interpolate a Correction to the 
Ascenddnt Longitude Given in a Table of Houses 

The ascendant is to be corrected an amount calculated 
by the rule of proportionals. In this case the Table of 
Houses gjves the ascendapt as SI 4*51'; the correction 
to it in proportion to 16' subtracted from the given M. C. 
would be 12' 36", which, when subtracted from the given 
T. H. asc. SI 4 # 51' = SI 4° 38' 24" as the corrected ascend- 
ant for Latitude 40° North.

'Correction to Latitude of T. of H. 1 

Exacting students will have noticed that, because the Tables of 
Houses give values only to the even degree, # it almost always 
becomes necessar^, in using them, for an astrologer to interpolate 
fer the odd minuted of latitude for birth place. 

We have heretofore, for simplicity and convenience, used ex- 
actly 40 degrees of latitude, but as before said, the exactly even 
degrees of latitude are rarely found in any case; therefore an 
interpolation for the 'odd minutes of latitude should Ite instituted. 
SuppcAe the latitude had been 40° 28' instead of exactly 40°. 
Then we would take the nearest tabular value of 40° and find out 
how much the ascendant increases at 41** of latitude. In this case 
the difference Is 29'. (Using Simplified Tables of Houses.) You 
merely multiply the excess minutes of latitude and tabular 
differences (of longitude) together and divide by 60,* the quotient 
being the extra minutes and seconds required, thus: 28 multiplied 
£y 29 equals 812, divided by 60=13' 32" to be addedt to the 
* Ascendant previously corrected to 4* 38' 24", which gives 4* 51' 56" 
of Leo ascending at time of birth, in 40° 28' of latitude. 

• (When the multiplication provides a figure less than 60 It is the 
answer in desired number of seconds for correction.) 
t (It was added in this case because the ascendant in> desired 
latitude is greater or advanced ; that is, the ascendant degrees In 
next latitude (41°) are larger. But had it been less, the cor- 
rection would be subtra?ted.) 

Unknown Birth Times 

One way to discover a person's Ascendant* at birth: 

Rule: Take note of the time when a person # asks for 
a horoscope of himself, refer to "Tfie Improved Perpetual 
Planetary Hour Book” and notice which planet rules the 
hour in which the question Is asked, fa) It usually rules 
tbe sign ascending at their*birth; (bj it is in a sign on 
birth day which should ascend, at birth time ; (c) or it 
rules the sign which at birth was on cusp of house of 
matter inquired about, as the sixth hoqse in question ’of 
sickness. 

For instance, a lady inquired about sickness, 1 nursday, 
August 3rd, 1922, at 2.05 p. m., in north la|itude 34 de- 
grees. Reference to the Hour Book shows this time to 
be ruled by the planet Jupiter, hence, Sagittarius (ruled 



528 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


by Jupiter) should be on the cusp of her fiatal sixth house. 
This is correct because in this case her biith time was 
known and she did have the 20th degree of Sagittarius 
on sixth cusp at'birth. It is interesting tc note that two- 
thirds of the planetary hour of Jupiter fyad passed at the 
time of the question, and at birth two-thirds of the sign 
Sagittarius had passed the cusp of the sixth house. 

The use of this plan in birth time rectification requires 
that the house ruling the question be>correctly chosen. 

When the planet ruling the hour is ruler of two zodia- 
cal signs, a little study will show which of the two signs 
to choose as they give decidedly different descriptions. 
Of course, 'this rule holds good only when the individual 
calls or asks in accordance with their own uninfluenced 
impressions; if they had a Planetary Hour Book and 
chose an hour from that in which to call, this rule could 
not then be used. If the request is by mail, take the time * 
you read the letter but preferably the time when the 
letter was written if it has been stated by the writer. 

Another Rule which may often be applied: Take the 
planet ip a man's horoscope which the Moon first aspects 
after birth. That planet will almost invariably rule the 
sign rising in the wife’s horoscope at her birth. To make 
a chart for her take about the middle of the sign answer- 
ing her description, which is ruled by this planet, and 
you will have a very close approximate chart with which 
to apply the rectification rules in order to derive the 

exact minute of birth. 

< 

In a*woman's horoscope take the planet whioh the Sun 
first aspects after birth, in the same way, and it will 
usually rule the husband's rising sign. If married more 
than once take the next planef aspected, as above, and sc 
on according to the number of unions. 

When none of the foregoing methods are possible we 
j>rocjed as follows*: 

Process when Birth Time is Unknown 

£ 

When thi hour of birth is unknown we must work to 
get the right zodiacal sign on the Ascendant. One way 
tp do this i» to refer to. “Description of the Ascend- 



PABT IV. RECTIFICATION 


ant" in Pa’rt III. Study each sign carefully and select 
the one which* be^t describes the native. Erect an ap- 
proximate chart with about the ^middle of that sign 
ascending. Take the ruler of the house *which governs 
the event, or the persbrf, and use it to work out the rules 
relating to “Arcs*of Events" method given in preceding 
pages. ♦ 

^Another method is to cast a chart for noon on birth 
date, study it in connection with the kind pf events 
furnished by the native as to sickness, accidents, etc.; 
turn tne chart around and put each sign successively on 
the Ascendant, noticing each time ifcthe position of chart 
then gives testimonies by planetary house positiop which 
tally with the nature of events as known. 

Perhaps two or more signs ascending seem to fulfill 
the requirements, in which case further study of sign 
m characteristics is necessary to determine which best ful- 
fills all conditions. Naturally, there is a chance here to 
err in judgment by attributing wrong virtues or influ- 
ences to planets in the various houses, but a correct 
knowledge of the influences of each planet, together witlu 
the ability to combine the house and planet influences 
will do much to obviate the likelihood of mistakes. 

For instance, Saturn ascending would signify a deep 
and sensitive nature introspectively trying to fathom the 
mystery of Lffe.and improve naturaMqualities ; attempting 
to ascend above the many limitations, restrictions and 
delays encountered on the way. Uranus and Mars in 
conjunction in the ninth house woyld signify* accidents 
to legs in region of ankles or thighs, which would agree 
with limitations also signified by SatuCn in the first, as 
before mentioned. These twp planets ip the ninth would 
also give radical experiences and injury in lands foreign 
o that of birth ; would endow the native with great zeal 
in the studies of the higher mind, astrology, etc., and 
would lead himp far from the orthodox, conventional re- 
ligions. The sign Gemini on the Midheaven w©ifld?be- 
stow Mercurial proclivities and also incline to business 
life. 

The instance just mentioned is actual. Saturn •de- 
scribes the native's disposition Mercury describes his #

business) Mars and Uranus in ninth house describe his 
scientific and religious tendencies and' also the accident 
which maimed his limbs in a foreign land/ The ^data is 
as follows * 


Illustration for Erecting Chart 
When Birth Time is Unknown 

Male, born at Greenwich, England, April 5th, 1863. 
Time of*birth unknown. Event: Long journey from 
England to America on September 5th, 1883. Make a 
chart for noon on th^ birth date as before explained; turn 
chart around from sign to sign and you will find that 
when Virgo 15° rises it •will nearly place the planets as 
desired, i. e., Saturn ascending, Mars and Uranus in 
ninth, but Cancer will be on the tenth cusp and we want 
G / emini there to correctly describe his business. (Mercur- 
ial) However, if you inspect the Table of Houses at tb** 
point of Virgo 15° ascending, you will see that a chart 
regularly made at this point with Gemini 11* on M. C., 
would put Mars and Uranus in the tenth .house and we 
\vant them in the ninth. Mars and Uranus are in the 
17th and 18th degrees of Gemini and to get them in the 
ninth house we must make the tenth cusp about 24° II. 
Now look in the Table of Houses at the place "where 
Gemini 24° io <*n cusp of tenth. Note the S. T. there 
given is 5.33:51, and from it subtract the S. T. at"noon 
on birthday, 0.53 :10, # which equals 4.40 :4L, time of birth 
p. m. 

If this answer had* been more than 12 hours, we would 
subtraot 12 hours from it*Tmd the result is a c . m. time of 
birth. 

Now make a chart for the time thus derived and c'all 
it the estimate chart. r Then the approximate time of 
binth must be further rectified by the reguiar iules as 
given in the first chapter of Part IV. As the event is 
thaVo^f r. long journey, we look to the ruler of the ninth 
house, which is Venus. Its longitude at the approximate 
'time is about Taurus 12° 57' and the right ascepsion of 
this is 40* 50'.

GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


FOR THE STUDENT’S NOTE ?OOK 


An Astrologies' day begins at midnight and is counted 
from the first to the twenty-fourth hour. 

* 

A Planetary-Hour day begins^ at sunrise. 

An Astronomical day begins at midnight and is ( 
counted from the first to the twenty-fourth hour. 

A Civil day begins at midnight and is counted from 
the first to the twelfth hour, and repeats for twelve 
hours. 

A Nautical day is twenty.-four consecutive hours, reck- 
oned from noon to noon. 

A Calendar month varies from 28 to 31 days. 

A Sidereal or Lunar month is 27 days, 7 hours, 43 
minutes, 11 and one-half seconds. 

A Synodical month is 29 days, 12 hours, 44 minutes, 
2.8 seconds. 

Solar year, or the transition from one Vernal 
Equinox to another, consists of 365.24244 solar days or 
365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes and 49.536 seconds. 

A Julian year ^365 days ; a Gregof ian year is 365.2425 
days. 

Every year, the nuriiber of which is divisible by four 
without a remainder, is a leap year, except the last of 
the Centrry, which is a leap year only when divisible by 
four hqndrefd without n remainder. 

The mOnth of February, 1886, was in f one respect the 
most remarkable in the world's history. It had no full 
moon. * January had two full moons, and so had March, 
but February had none. This had not occurred since 
the creation of the world, and it will not occur again, 
according to the computation of astronofner^, .for 
f 2,5QO$Op years. 

At the fame of a New Moon (5 60) the Pafrt of For- 
tune (©) is always on the ascendant of a chart made for 
that time. 1 At Full Moon ( 8Q) the Part of Fortune is 
always on the descendant of a chart made for time of 
Full Moon. *

ASTROLOGY 
Is It Science or Religion? 

O me astrology is both. I love the scientific 
features connected with the making of horo- 
scopes based upon the rules and findings of 
astronomy. I revere the “voice” (vibrations) 
of the* planets. They are God's Messengers 
and Speak in His language; they give us His “word.” 
Astrology is also a philosophy as^ it explains many of 
the paradoxes of life. It is something you can live by 
as regards daily conduct, ana with the stars *as your 
guide your thoughts must be attracted upward with 
greater reverence and inspiration. It turns one from the 
sordid to the sublime; from the world of mistakes and 
false appearances to spiritual reality. 

“The Stars Incline; They Do Not Compel.” 

Ignorance impels us to do many things which wisdom 
transcends. The Bible tells us, “Ye shall kngw the 
Truth, and the Truth shall set you free,” but man will 
not t>e “free” so long as he permits himself to be in the 
bondage of fear and dread of material appearances. Not 
until he begins to realize the Truth concerning the 
realities of life and its unseen influences can he begin 
to loosen the fetters one by one and exercisfe his divine 
attributes. 

Those v$|ho are seeking relief fro*m the troubles, strain 
and strife of life»will find renewed courage andf inspira- 
tion for higher development, as David did, through his 
v^ery significant and enlig*hfening Psalm. 

We suffer mostly from ourselves and mainly through 
incorrect err imperfect expression of planetary vibrations. 
Each ray (aspect) has a mission, a Message, ^divine 
purpose, but oh! how far short do we fall in 
pretation or expression, and yet each ^aspect is potent 
with magnificent possibilities. 

There are no evil aspects; there are only adverse 
•reactions. Misinterpretation of ^he meaning and purport* 




596 


GENETHLIACAL ASTRO'LOGY 

j, 

of planetary aspects or entire ignorance <*f them, is the 
cause of most of our*misery and troubles. 

The true pufpose of astrology js to* bring us into 
conscious relation with great kingdoms: the kingdom 
of the Heavens and the kingdom ot Gpd within. They 
are not separate ; they are one, a universe. ^ 

By lifting up our eyes to the “heavenly hills/' (the 
stars, plartets and the signs), a revelation of their sig- 
nificance appears. The thrill of new knowledge enfolds 
us in loving embrace* when we realize the magnificence 
of those wonderful words of the XIXth Ps^lm : 

“The heavens declare the glory of God ; and the firma- 
ment sheweth His handiwork." (Solar, planetary and 
starry systems arranged and motivated under divine 
direction for the benefit of all life.) 

Astrology is the connecting link between the human 
and the divine. It is a spiritual science couched in finite 
words for the understanding of mortals who need to 
'learn the lessons of this sphere through the experiences 
of daily life. 

There are No Evil Planets — There are No Evil Aspects 

This is the ringing 'message of astrology Which leads 
one at last to realize that God in love leads us through 
every experience for our good progress. Every aspect 
is a blessirtg in disguise. Wise indeed are they who 
bless every incident in their life, even those Which have 
the appearance of evil, for they are good # servants leading 
us to higher understanding, greater appreciation and 
better' conditions. Therefore, watch your aspects anil 
see how much benefit you can in reality derive from 
them. 

Th^fcffort to make practical response to the urges, 
generated by solar, lunar and planetary r^ys is the most 
important .effort/ of daily life. Upon our ability to har- 
monize with' the desirable cr constructive vibrations, 
and our power to transcend or transform those of un- 
desirable nature, mainly .depend happiness and success. * 


687 


PABfr V t ADDITIONAL STUDIES 

The determining point in attainment is the place 
where we* are required to act upon the urges generated 
within. The various daily planetary aspects provide 
changes in tfte deticiitely arranged elements of the body ; 
the chemical changes which result correspond to the 
nature or quaKty of ait aspect and transmit to the mind 
urges, attitudes, moods, desires, aversions, etc. There- 
fore it is readily seen that the reaction which we give 
to these urges largely determines the quality and nature 
of <he resulting experience. 

“Life is What W£ Make It” 

This old astrological aphorism assumes its right mean- 
ing when it is associated with those operations of Nature 
which animate our being and give opportunity for the 
expression of life. It calls attention to that highly sig- 
nificant and important point in our daily life where we 
are called upon to give conscious response or outward 
expression to the animations generated through plane- 
tary-chemical activities. 

Joy, success, knowledge, attainment and every other 
experience are largely the result of the nature and 
quality of the reactions we make to planetary rays 
which daily, hourly, constantly impinge our being and 
impart tfhe. power of thought a*hd action. 

An All Wise Creator has provided the vehicle, the ele- 
ments and the power necessary for our Spirit to mani- 
fest life'and has also given us a great measure of choice 
as to our conduct. Hence, we are responsible to a large 
extent for our present conditions. Nature has provided 
us with a personal workshop, tools and material, most 
of which we have used unwisely. Who would not do 
much better if we had the opportunity to live oqr life 
again ? 1 

However, our faults and failures are not usually Wilful 
or deliberate inversions and we can* bujld? better when 
working in conscious 4 harmony with Natural Laws 
Astrology points the way.

UNIVERSE MAKERS « 

A few years ago a well-known editorial writer publish- 
ed a list of notables whom he termed “Universe-Makers/* 
because of their outstanding achievements. Among 
them were the names of six history-makers known to the 
astrological ly-minded as great astrologers. As the per- 
centage of astrologers in the list of Great Ones pointed 
significantly to the far-reaching, constructive influence 
of astrology’s power in the making of worth-white his- 
tory, we give a few highlights from their lives. The six 
mentioned are Aristotle, Copernicus, Galilei, Kepler, 
Ptolemy and Pythagoras. 1 ' He might well have included 

Hippocrates (460 B. C.) Generally referred to as the 
“Father of Medicine,” disciple of Plato and Aristotle, 
author of many treatises. The Encyclopedia Brittanies* 
says of him, “One of his great merits is that he was the * 
first to .disassociate medicine from priestcraft, and to 
direct attention to the natural history of disease. . . We 
find in his works evidence of the acuteness of his observ- 
ations, by the manner in which the occurrence of critical 
days in disease is enunciated.” (The critical days in 
disease are enunciated by the Mood’s phases and aspects 
with other planets, in relation to the patient's horoscope.) 
Hippocrates is quoted as saying, “A physician cannot 
safely administer phySic (medicine) if he b$ unacquainted 
with first principles.” The “first principles” were those 
of natural law, especially those exerted by the Moon and 
planets up6n the progress of disease. 

Pythagoras, whb lived 60Q years B. C., was fully con- 
versant with astrology. H$ advocated the idea of the 
earth 'being spherical and deduced that all the other 
zodiacal bodies were globular.

SYMBOLISM 

This chapter is reprinted from a paper on “Symbolism” by 
Mrs. Mildred Ky]e, originally published in the October, 191? 
Issue of “The Astrological ' Bulletins.” 

I Astrology may rightfully be termed a language of 
symbols, hence the first essential step is to become per- 
fectly familiar with its symbology, otherwise it is impos- 
sible fo read a horoscope after having made it? 

Symbolism may be called a common language. By 
this is meant that certain concrete forms are employed 
to represent abstract ideas, which, presented to the view 
of one versed in the form, convey to the mind of that 
person a definite idea; just as numbers in arithmetic each 
embody some idea, but if the number be expressed in 
frords the spoken word will differ according to the lang- 
uage used. 

It matters not in what age we live or what language 
we speak, symbols tell the same story: It is the lan- 
guage of the initiates, and through such means one ini- 
tiate ^speaks to another though long cycles of time may 
separate them. 

O The /circle is one of the oldest symbols in the 
world, and if' taken alone, signifies the incomprehensible 
unity that underlies all manifestation. If applied to the 
whole vast universe, it stands for the Absolu,te/God un- 
manifested, all inclusive, without beginning or end. In 
another sense, it ■ represents our Solar Logos, 'or God, 
and the enclosing circle shows the extent of His limita- 
tion as differentiated front the Cosmos, but He* is all 
inclusive within that limitation ; He, himself, being with- 
out b$ ginning or end. 

When the circle is used in astrology it strafes, the 
Sun, whioh is the physical symbol^ our 
or God manifested in our solar system. . When it is usdd 
for man • it symbolizes the immortal spirit ^or the inner 
celestial man, who is ‘God's representative on earth ; al- 
ways it symbolizes that whfcn, like itself, has neither 



542 


QENETHLIACAL tVSTRQLOQY 


beginning’ nor en\i. It is a symbol of 4 life in contradis- 
tinction to form ; it has no limits either in* ^pace or time 
and enters into no relations, yet the circumference sug- 
gests limitation, so we read this^ message into it : The 
One Eternal Life is manifesting *it§elf in various ways 
through one limiting avenue of form. 

Through the symbol of numbers, this will be naugltt 
(o) ; in that of light and color it will be darkness ; and 
in that of*sound and music it will be silence. 

O The circle without the dot represents life or spirit 
unmanifested; with the dot it shows manifestation. All 
life begins to manifest fro c tn this central point. Wherever 
we see that point we know that it is a nucleus of life 
which will manifest as soon as it is given the proper con- 
ditions, as for instance, in the egg and all cellular forma- 
tion. 

® Let us draw a line through the circle, which gives 
us two half-circles. We will examine this symbol and, 
learn the story it holds for us. When used ki an abstract 
. sense it signifies that the Great Life is manifesting the 
two polarities of spirit matter, not separated in two ex- 
tremes, of spirit on one hand and matter on the either, 
but held together in one, a duality With unity underlying 
it; that duality is*the root of all the dual forces we rpeet 
in life, and we meet i^ everywhere ; there is r 4 pt a thing 
without its, opposite in all this manifested universe. 
When used in terms of consciousness it is the subjective 
and objective mind, or the consciousness turned within 
toward *the spirit and fumed outward toward {he world, 
but wherf we are using the objective mind we are only 
using one-half of our real consciousness, the rest is sub- 
jective, .or latent fof the time 'being. 

When used in terms of’ astrology, it is the Moon, and 
represents imperfection, because of its limitation. 1 We 
( musjt remember that the circle symbolizes the whole 
’ half circle bnly half of the real man* and that 

hstit the material, side of him, or that which belongs to 
form. Or agjin ft tells us that only half of the -spiritual 
man can express himself thrdugh form, and the dual 
nature of map has its root here in the Moon's fortes. % 





PART V, ADDITIONAL STUDIES 543 

When the conjunction of the Sun anc/ Moon. takes place, 
it symbolizes the union of the individual and personal 
man; the higher and lower self. * The opposition of the 
two luminaries, symbolized by the full^Moon, represents 
the personality illanfinated by the Sun, or Spirit, and 
throwing its borrowed light upon the Earth, or our 
physical consciousness. . 

* © Let us draw another line through the circle at 
right angles to the first, and we have a cross within the 
circle. This is & very old symbol ; its origih is lost in 
the # night of time, and like all other symbols has many 
meanings. It implies complete rganifestation and inces- 
sant activity, for it cannot be formed until, the dual 
forces have become fully polarized, active and passive, 
positive and negative, each acting upon the other and 
each reacted upon by the other. It is the unfolding of 
the evolutionary scheme of things. First we have the 
two halves, then the four quarters, which form the root 
of all quadrate things on Earth, such as the four seasons, 
four corners of the Earth, the four elements, etc. As- 
tronomically, it is the symbol of our Earth; and it sym- 
bolizes differentiation, activity in matter, the material 
body and material masses, being astrologically the sym- 
bol-of Pars Fortunze (The Part of Fortune). 

-f When the circle has been removed and only the 
four arms are left, it would signify that the spiritual side 
had been** forgotten and only th£ material consciousness 
was manifesting itself. From this symbol has been 
evolved the well known Christian cross, which did not 
originate with Christianity, but as old as # the language 
of symbSls. The symbol of the cross represents spirit 
and matter wfdded together in art indissoluble union 
throughout the ages of tlje life of a # Universe, and none 
•can wrench them apart. The vertical line represents 
spirit, while the horizontal lirfe symbolizes matter. The 
spiritual «forces are said to play through the spinal # col- 
umn, and as* man stands upright he <is the vertical* line 
and to that extent spiritual. The animal's spi^Aia "hori- 
zontal, a f nd that fact relates him to matter and raids 
him # to the yibration of the animal kingdom.. Man is not 
yet wholly spiritual his* bodies are composed of 4 matter 
so he is under the necessity of spending at least one-third 



544 


OBNBTHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


of his time in the horizontal position when asleep, while 
his spirit slips away to its own plane. 

\4 

Astrologically, the vertical line of the cross symbol- 
izes the Sun, while the horizontal line represents the 
Moon, or, again, life and form. It is the great loom of 
life on which all the fabrics of form are woven; all the 
garments with which the spirit of man is clothed are 
fashioned and framed by the operation of these dual* 
forces. Tha Universal Mind is the Great Weaver, love 
and life are its threads. 

The symbol of the cioss dates back to the period of 
the third race or the Lemu*-ian period, when the human 
soul fell Into generation, or when the division of the 
sexes took place. It was then that the divine in man 
became crucified, for as long as matter (which is the 
horizontal line of the cross) lives on the borrowed light 
of the spirit, the spirit is held to the plane of generation 
and can only use its creative powers in a material way, 
while it might, if freed from the limitations of matter, 
create like the Christ by the power of its own divine will. 
To this extent the cross symbolizes generation. 

From the foregoing we see that the symbols for “all 
the planets contain, one or more of three elements : the 
circle which denotes spirit, the cross which denotes mat- 
ter, and the half-circle - which denotes the intellectual 
aspect of the soul or mind. By this knowledge we can 
determine from the symbol of each planet whether the 
forces which ' emanate from it be spiritual, mental or 
material: for the planets themselves are centers ,> of great 
Cosmic forces which* enter into the composition of every 
earthly as well as eyery celesfial object, and the inter- 
mingling* of these forces in tfie° combination of man's 
various bodies — physical, emotional and mental — decides 
the temperament of the personal man. If they are har- 
monibus we have a» pleasing personality; if they are in- 
Harm Qflj jfttts, we have Ahe reverse. The symbol of the 
plas^twin also tell us which one of our bodies is 
affected for good ofr ill.

Mercury

Mars

out nature, entering into the composition of the material 
and emotional bodies, but not the mental, as we see no

Mars force is* predominant in the animal kingdom and 
in the merely animal man, and is not directly connected 
with mind, but with sensations Its work with man is 
to spur hirg to action, and finally knowledge comes as 
the result of his activities.

Uranus

ASTROLOGICAL VOCA6ULARY 

"Kfow forcible are right words!” — Job 6, 25. 

Zodiaaal Personality and Key Words 

Adventurous Aries says, “I am.” 

Thrifty Taurus says, “I have." 

Diversified Gemini says, "I think." 

Careful Cancer says, "I feel." 

Lordly Leo says, # T will." 

Vigilant Virgo says, ‘T t analyze." 

Loving Libra says, "I balance." 

Strenuous Scorpio ^ays, "I desire." 

Sportive Sagittarius says, "I see." 

Cautious Capricorn says, 4 T use." 

Altruistic* Aquarius says, "I know." 

Poetic Pisces says, "I believe." 

It is interesting to note that in everyday affairs we 
unconsciously employ astrological terms, as all words 
are originally the attempt to communicate in definite 
terms human urges or reactions to planetary influences. 

* 

Tfiis desire and rteed for a means of communication 
to convey thought and feeling in a mor£ permanent form 
than that of sound (the spoken yord), led to the first 
crude picture language which gradually cwvolved into 
alphabetical order and subsequent segregation of words. 

Today we have a language offering a wifie range of 
choice for4itness of expression, and students of astrology 
have the added Advantage pf being aide to select those 
terms which serve to int^rgret our reactions to celestial 
phenomena. % 

Realizing the importance of such selection, we have 
carefully gathered a number of words ipto the following 
vocabulary, chosen for their relation .in some fof nl il^Jthe 
planets, of to signs which they rule. Only the mo>t 
commonly used words have been allocated, therefore the 
list is by ho means complete and subject to amplification. 
The words arc arranged alphabetically for convenience 
•and the sagacious student will.jind among *these "kfy"*

PERTINENT POINTS 
Geocentric Astrology 

Geocentric Astrology is the oldest known system of 
astro-science, being # the <?ne originally used by the an- 
<yents. • 

When Copernicus discovered that the Sun was the 
center of our solaf system, many who did not imderstand 
the iflrinciples presumed his discovery would “explode” 
astrology, and those who voiced frhis idea but revealed 
their lack of correct information. As the masses^are not 
generally familiar with the principles of astrology, that 
incident has been incorrectly used by its opponents in 
an effort to discredit the science. 

The wisdom of ancient “wise” men was such that we 
find to this day all of the astrologers of renown using 
the same geocentric methods, but with many improve- 
ments, which conduce to greater accuracy through the 
invention and use of various tables. 


Ptolemy, (Claudius Ptolemoeus, Alexandria, Egypt, 
second century A. D.) who is termed the “Father of 
Jiodern Astrology,” was a noted mathematician so high- 
ly skilled that many of his ideag are incorporated in 
present day astronomy. He made diligent research into 
ancient writings on astrology and then compiled a cele- 
brated work entitled, “The Tetrabiblos, or OuaMre-par- 
tite,” being four books on the influence of stars and 
planets. 


In *his gre^t work, “Th# Almagest/* Ptolemy contrib- 
uted to science the theory of # the movements of the 
planets (geocentric.) His illustrated circles gave to the 
Arabians £reat stimulus for further interest in {his 
science and it Is said they brought its predictive side up 
to the highest art. The Arabians were noted for their 
wonderful learning, their observatories,^ inventions and 
the development of trigonometry. They ’substituted our 
present numeral system fdr the old ponderous Roman 
.numerals. 



566 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


# 

JL 


In the*19th century, Napier, a noted scientist, invented 
the tables of logarithms to facilitate, fyis astrological 
research*. Only a fe^yv years ago Joseph G. Dalton, an 
astronomer at one time connected with the Naval Ob- 
servatory at Washington, D. C., arranged a set of tables 
called, “The Spherical Basis of Astrology,” (being a 
Table of Houses for places fyetwefcn 22 and 56 degrees 
north latitude) as a contribution to the science of astrol- 
ogy for geocentric use, — that of finding the relation of 
the earth in its axial rotation to the ascendant, mid- 
heaven, £nd other points in the zodiac, which not only 
makes it possible to appoint the signs and degrees around 
the Earth as they actually are at any certain moment, 
but al$o makes it possible to place planets in a chart in 
their true zodiacal relation to the Earth. 

Geocentric Astrology bases its calculations on plane- 
tary aspects or upon the distance between planets i * 
connection with their angular relation to the Earth. The 
angles (aspects) formed with Sun as center (heliocentric) 
are altogether different from those with Earth as focus- 
ing point. We live on the Earth, — not on .the Sun. The 
angle formed upon the Earth by two planets 60 zodiacal 
degrees apart is altogether different from angles made 
by planets 90 or 120 degrees apart, each angle producing 
a difference in vibration. Thus rome angular aspects 
are benign, cohtsive or uniting in their nature, such as 
the sextile aspect of 60 zodiacal degrees, whi\e the square 
or right aggie aspecf is disruptive or disintegrative. 

Thus it is seen that Geocentric Astrology considers 
these impirtant principles : The relation of the planets 
to eachr other; the angles formed upon the Eaith by those 
relationships ; th£ relation Qf the EartH' by axial rotation 
to pojnts of the zodiac ancj ^o planets, producing their 
actual “house” positions in charts madef for different 

latitudes. 

• 

' Copernicus did not “explode” astrology. Man; dis- 
covering something which he did not know before, does 
not change any law of Natuie. The laws of Nature are 
'eternal ajmd reidain unchanged by man’s changing ideas. 
Whether rfne thinks the Sun njoves around the P Earth, 
or fhinks the Earth revolves around the Sun, does not 



567 


PART V. ADDITIONAL STUDIES 
1 

# • 

change* tfye influence *of the Sun *upoA the Ear,th, and it 
is planetary influence shed upon the Earth with which 
astrology is,n!o^t concerned. Therefore, no “explosion" 
occurred, except in the minds of tllose who were unduly 
perturbed by being unaware of these fac'js. 

Heliocentric Astrology was the outcome of Copernic- 
us' calculations; A i. e., observations of the planets' rela- 
tion to the Sun. And Helio (Sun) centric (center) 
methods are entirely correct for those beings who may 
reside upon tho Spn. However, there are son^e features 
of astrological research which may properly and bene- 
ficially use helio-centric positions, such as Astro-Meteor- 
ology. 

• * 

Geo (earth) centric calculations have been found to be 
more applicable for the inhabitants of the Earth, and as 
before stated all astrologers of renown, both ancient and 
modern, favor the geocentric system in the erection of 
charts for interpreting planetary influence in relation to 
human life and its affairs. That is, for convenience they 
place the Earth in the center of a chart, and consider the 
Sun as though traveling in the Earth’s orbit. 

As in every four minutes of time the Earth tunns one 
degree, making a complete rotation of 360 degrees in 
twenty-four hours, and as each degree rising on the 
Ascendant or eastern horizon, gives a different influence, 
it is' readily seen why persons differ so, although thou- 
sands may' be born on the same diy. This difference is 
further multiplied by the twelve signs of # the zodiac, 
being traversed by the Earth during the year, and each 
day rotating on its axis, while at the sarffe time the 
planets ar$ also moving in their orbits, changing aspects 
between themselves and the; Earth. 

L A ‘chart of* the heavens 1 g^eocentricafly made for* a cer- 
tain moment, for any certain place, will show the par- 
ticular influences in operation which will affect the indiv- 
idual "born* at {hat time and place. 

If two individuals are tyyn at the same time dad place, 
they will have similar characteristics, tastes and exper- 
iences, differentiated according to their different environ 
ment and heredity, and their stage of evolution. 


568 


GENBTHLIACAL ^ASTROLOGY 


A historical case in. Europe is cited^of a prince and a 
peasant who wer$ born at the same moment in the same 
lecality. r They looked very much alike, Vere ill of a 
similar malady r at the same time, were jnarried on the 
same date, and when the prince ascended the throne the 
peasant opened a shop. They die<J on* the same day. 

By taking geocentric motion into account, difference 
in terrestrial latitude makes a difference in rising de- 
grees, hence, two horoscopes made for. the same time and 
date, but* for different latitudes, would show difference 
in the individuals. The importance of geocentric methods 
is inestimable where'it is necessary to take into account 
all of the various phenomena connected with the move- 
ments of the planets and their relation to the Earth, 
including its own axial rotation. 

Some have tried mixing heliocentric planetary posi- 
tions with geocentric methods of casting a chart, bui < 
helio (sun) centric positions are planets in their relation 
to the Sun, consequently, such method is not advisable. 

Astrology from the time of the ancients to the present 
is scientific in its principles. Various attempts have been 
made to omit the scientific element and devise means of 
character delineation without the use of much figuring, 
but they are only partly successful. It is obvious tfcat to 
secure all information possible concerning tbe planetary 
influences on which all life is dependent, it is necessary 
to use ^11 available means to interpret those influences, 
hence, the*Earth being the vital center of influence for 
its inhabitants, it is essential to notice its angular rela- 
tion to planets, (by means of aspects) r.s well as its own 
rotation whereby we find its relation to points of the 
zodiad for any location at arfy' given time. * 

however, with present day facilities in t,he form of 
astrological schpols, classes, textbooks, porrespondence 
courses ^nd magazines, it is no longer a tedious or diffi- 
cult matter to learn to cast -and delineate a horoscope, 
and the knowledge acquired through the study of such 
a chart is tff inestimable value.

* Horary Astrology * 

One branch of astrology called "Horary 0 relates to 
the word “horfis” meaning hour. It is a^system of divin- 
ation based upon a chart cast for a certain hour and min- 
ute, pertaining to a thought, question or event. 

\ 

9 

Horary Astrology bears out wonderfully the idea of 
celestial correspondence to mundane affairs, exemplify- 
ing the sympathy existing between the operations of 
mind* and planetary vibrations. Through the positions 
and aspects of planets chemical changes occur in the 
human organism which have,, an important be^ing on 
metabolism, and to a great extent determine the anabol- 
istic and catabolistic activities. These subjective states 
lead people to think of and ask questions of importance 
at times when the planets bear definite relation to the 
origin, nature and termination of the matters involved. 

In Horary Astrology very much depends upon whether 
an aspect is applying or separating; whether planets are 
angular, succedent, cadent, dexter, sinister, etc. A num- 
ber of considerations not usually employed in» Natal 
Astrology are used in Horary, such as collection of light, 
combustion, disposal of, frustration, reception, refrana- 
tion, translation of light, void of cour^fe, etc. 

i 

To become proficient in Horary Astrology one should 
first become very familiar with Natal Astrology, espec- 
ially as regards the casting of horoscopes, lo<jal time cor- 
rections, the nature of signs, plants, houses and aspects. 

# * 

This branch of astrology* is useful for answering spe- 
cific .questions which arise as the result of sickness, acci- 
dents, journeys, news, reading^ letter, receiving a pro- 
position and other matters which give a desire for infor- 
mation regarding the outcome. 

Several ^textbooks are available for the study of this 
subject.

* 'Plariets in Business * 

The Commercial world is divided into *nine general 
divisions, each filled by a planet in the fallowing order: 

r 

Oil and fishing industry represented^ by Neptune. 

t 

Railroads, aerial and electric industries by Uranus. f 

Mining, farming and cement work by Saturn. 

, » 

Manufacturing, building and munitions by Mar^. 

Religious, legal an^ financial affairs by Jupiter. 

Government represented by the Sun. 

Entertainment art and social functions by Venus. 

Schools, intellectual affairs and pyblishing by Mercury. 

Common employments represented by the Moon. 

The influence of Vulcan and Pluto is not sufficiently 
known to be classified in this manner. 

Each division is subject to nine sub-divisions which 
in turn are ruled by the nine planets. Take the yil in- 
dustry for example: The first division is ruled by Nep- 
tune itself, and the first impression is that this industry 
is a “trust/' a vast, irpxicate, complicated scheme where- 
by the people represented by every othei planet down 
the scale are frequently deceived and robbed, and the 
power to ^o this is gained through secret intrigues, 
lobbying interests anti other underhand and put-of-sight 
method^. This represents Neptune's vnftivorable influ- 
ence; more correctly speakfeig, a gross interpretation or 
manifestation of its influence, t The influence of a planjjt 
remains the same but oyr expression of it is modified in 
various ways, that is, the awakened soul expresses (acts) 
for* the good of all, while the unawakened (tho^e .on a 
purely.material fdage) express the influence in selfishness 
regardless of the welfare of osiers. 

The seCottd division of the industry under considera- 
tion *is ruled by Uranus, the* enlightener. (Oil is used 




PART V, ADDITIONAL STUDIES 


571 


for light and power.) ~ This planet governs the transpor- 
tation facilities connected therewith. ? The next division 
is rul^d by Saturn and represents thfe oil well „ diggers, 
drillers, pipemen, etc. Jupiter represents the numerous 
legal departments, Jh$ financiers and Cashiers. Mars 
represents the agents, contractors and the construction 
departments. Sun governs the various directors and 
lugh officials. Venus, being the octave of Neptune, in 
this case expresses some of the latter's subtleness by 
extending and maintaining the influence and prosperity 
of the industry through social intercourse at'% banquets 
and efther such functions given for the purpose of influ- 
encing various dignitaries in power* to gain desired ends. 
Venus, therefore, rules the social and entertaining ele- 
ments connected with the ente?prise. Mercury rules the 
clerical forces, the advertising and the press agents 
which it maintains. The Moon rules the teamsters and 
other common employees and also the masses who buy 
land consume the product. 

Thus each of the general divisions first named is sus- 
ceptible to subdivision, and, although in this example 
we have brought out the faults rather than the virtues 
of this particular industry, it is by no means wjthout 
virtues, for while there is room for vast improvement 
in its methods and conduct of the business yet it pro- 
vides employment and is a means of livelihood for many 
thousands of men and women. 

As yet this industry is expressing the influence of 
Neptune in a very low manner but all these phases grad- 
ually evolve to a finer or more perfect order in* proportion 
to the development or unfoldment of all the in^viduals 
connected therewith: the employer, employee and con- 
sumer. 

% i' 

Of course, the foregoing allocations of planetary ruler- 
ships in business are general considerations. When we 
say, for instance, that the Moon rules liquids, we mean 
that it has dominion over the neutral or basic -humor 
which, speaking physiologically, acts as a ^medium 
whereby certain substances can act aijd react, or by 
which they may be carried to various parts of the body 
to be dissolved, resolved or expulsed.

The Moon is said to rule milk. Milk has a watery 
basis (as many \nilkmen know !) represented by ; it 
has fatly constituents, U ; caseine and extractive -matter, 
O; and salts, Therefore, when we^say “the Moon 
rules milk," it is a general statement referring to its 
fluidic quality, and not to the spe^fic forms constituting 
milk. 

In the elementary study of astrology general terms 
are popular but when approaching the ccience of astrol- 
ogy the ‘constituents, or elements, are specialized % by the 
planetary, or sign, rulership. 

Therefore, let us, as students of Nature (as interpreted 
by astrology) endeavor to learn everything possible re- 
garding the influence of the planets, and to express those 
influences in the finest or highest manner of our under- 
standing, thus setting a standard r by our example that 
others will be anxious to follow. ' * 

* Decans * 

In decent years the word decan (dekan in ancient term- 
inology) has been applied to a one-third division (ten 
degrees) of the^ zodiacal signs buf research shows that 
originally decans, and faces as they were also called, had 
reference to the constellations. 

Albumazer (Abu Masher) a noted Arabian physician 
and astVoqomer who lived about one thousand years ago. 
and whose writings tiave been commented <jn as of the 
highest authority, by such men as Abfn Ezra, refers to 
“the Decans and their houses (signs), according to 
Babylonians, Egyptians and«Rersians,” saying, 

“Here follow the Decans, which the Arabs call 'faces.' 
They are three to each sign of the Way (zodiac).” 

*Th£sfr Decans of Faces, belonging to any one partic- 
ular sign, could easily be distinguished when they came 
along the meridian coincident with or clo t se along with 
the # sign to which they belonged. (They do not now 
coincide.) 




578 


PART V/ ADDITIO NAL STUDIES 

The “terms” 'as/given by Ptolemy a yp the special div- 
isions of the signs; the “faces” (pictures) and “iecans” 
(side pieces) ar^ the constellations, thirty^six in number. 
To illustrate the*importknce given the decans or constel- 
lations by the ancient*;, I have referred to many of them 
in the twelve chapters on “The Zodiacal Signs,” in latter 
pages of Part V. 

However, in modern astrology the word Decan or 
Decanate now has reference to the one-third division of 
each sign. The original significance and beauty behind 
the meaning of the divisions of the*constellations is al- 
most lost, although all classical mythology and «every 
religion are based upon their indications. But so far 
have modern creeds departed from the parent meanings 
that they actually deny their source when they deny 
astrology 

A decanate now consists of ten degrees or one-third 
of a sign, hence there are three decans in each sign, 
thirty-six in the zodiac. Two methods have been in use 
for allocating the planetary ruler of each decanate. 

The Oriental system names the ruler of decanates in 
th^ order of the triplictties thus : First decanate of Aries 
ruled by Mars, second by Sun, third by # Jupiter. This 
seems *to be tjje popular method. TJje principle concern- 
ing decanates is that while a planet rules its*sign as a 
whole and especially the first decanate (as Mars rules 
Aries and also the first decanate of Aries), the other two 
divisions of the sign each expresses a sub-influence of 
the nature of the planets which rule the^remaining signs 
of the same triplicity. To illustrate: in the case of Aries, 
the Sun because ruling theeiext sign of*the fierv triplic- 
ity, Leo, and Jupiter because it rples the following sign 
of the fiery triplicity, Sagittarius, are decanate co-rulers.

The foregoing is the modern adaptation but one an- 
cient system allocated the rulers of decans not by triplic-* 
ity sign-rulers, but in the same order as the planetary 
hours thus: Aries — Mars, Sun, Venus; Taurus — Mercury, 
Moon, Saturn; Gemini — Jupiter, Mars, Sun; and so on 
in this order through the twelve signs. The term “face” 
is sometimes incorrectly used to designate decan. 

c 

The use of the decanates in natal astrology is applic- 
able principally to the ascending sign. By noting which 
decanate is on the ffrst cusp, gives an ide#" of how the 
native is particularly expressing that sign, or rather, what 
particular part of the sign he is expressing; this being 
mainly, interpreted in terms of the planet which rules 
that decanate. For instance, if Leo is ascending the gen- 
eral significator of the pei^on is the Sun. If the 22nd 
degree of Leo is* on the fir^t; cusp the personality is in 
general described by the Sun, with Mars ^s a sub-infki- 
ence, thus adding to thd Leo-Sun tendencies more action, 
fefree, desire and execution. In other words^ in this case

Progressed Decanates 

In the progressed horoscope decanates crossing the 
ascendant with the passing of years produce noticeable 
changes in the temperament and characteristics of the 
native. The year in which the degree beginning a new 
decanate comes to the ascendant usually marks the be- 
ginning of new moods, inclinations, desires and interests, 
according to the nature of the planet ruling it and in 
accordance with the ppsition and aspects of that planet. 

Influence of Decanates 

A very good idea of the influence of the decan may be 
obtained by referring to a delineation of the planets by 
signs and reading the planet which rules that decan as 
though it were in that sigh. For instance,^ if the pro- 
gressed ascendant is Y* r &° 25th degree, it is in the Taur-

It is not the purpose of this work to enter into a de-

various symbols of signs, stars and constellations, but 
simply tocshow that what was written in the Scriptures, 
as well as in allegory and mythology, is likewise written 
in the skies, illustratiag the axiom, “As above, so below,” 
and shewing that the Bible contains much of astrological 
significance. 

<■ 

Whatever is written in the stars above will be enacted 
in events below. The references given in this book do 
not come near to exhausting the subject; they are but- 
a few of the many that could be cited and are offered 
with the hope that other researchers will complete the 
chain of connecting links between celestial indications 
and their terrestrial correspondences. The Old Testa- 
ment was written long before the birth of Christ and its 
references to the signs are often indicative of what was 
yet to come; while the New Testament refers to what 
did eventually happen to fulfill prophecy, in accordance 
with those signs.

Venus and Neptune 
9 V 

Venus, ruler of the signs, Taurus and Libra, some- 
times called “the love pair,” calls forth response from the 
finer attributes of*the being, inclining to beauty, har- 
mony, Sociability, gaiety and popular music . 1 She gives 
appreciation or.ability for art, for developing fine pictures 
and producing beautiful music ^through skillful' touch. 
Vibrations of Venus are affiliated with the sense of feel- 
ing. 



PART V, ADDITIONAL STUDIES 


577 


Neptune is termed the octave of Vt?nus. It reveres 
beauty in its broader, deeper or uniwertfal aspect, "in con- 
tradistinction to , personal appearances. Neptunian peo- 
ple are Bohemian rathef than just sociable. They love 
the weird more thun popular music. Venus may be said 
to represent the cultivated musician ; Neptune the in- 
spiced. Venus produces a realization of the nature of 
objects by physical contact because it rules the sense of 
touch’, by which its qualities may be observed. Neptune 
can produce sensations without contact, by rrfeans of 
inner or psychic feeling. Neptune rules the psychometric 
faculty by which otherwise unknovfti qualities may be 
described through the sense of fueling, ruled by Venus. 

Venus and Neptune are natural complements. Venus 
is “exalted” in Neptune's sign, Pisces. It may be noted 
that from time immemorial a great number of people 
have observed Friday (Venus day) as “fish” day. Stud- 
ents who use the “Improved Ferpetual Planetary Hour 
Book” are aware that Neptunian events almost invariably 
occur in an hour ruled by Venus. 

Between the personal interpretation of love, as sym- 
bolized^by Venus, and the universal love of Neptune, 
Jupiter, “hail fellow $ell met,” or “the # pillar of the 
church,” stands as a bridge over which the love-seeking 
Venusian must pass into an understanding of divine love. 
It is significant that, until the discovery of Neptune, 
Jupiter was assigned the rulership of both Sagittarius 
and Pisces.

NJercury and Uranus 

» V 

In the natural zodiac Mercury is ruler of the mental 
signs, Gemini, and Virgo. 

Mercury rules ordinary mental processes through 
which one acquires knowledge by observation, percep- 
tion, reading, writing, speaking, listening.' 

* » j 

# # » 

# Uranus is termed the octave of Mercury. It rules a 
mental sign, also, Aquarius. It governs that-' phase oL

mind which is not dependent upon the ordinary percep- 
tive processes. Mercury rules those 'things which de- 
scribe a picture or 'place, but Uranus rules the" things 
which take y<ru there. Mercury reprerents knowledg 
we acquire through present conditions; Uranus repre- 
sents accumulated wisdom gained through previous ex- 
periences manifesting as i'ntuition.,, Mercury is the 
learner; Uranus is the knower. They are natural com- 
plements of which Uranus is the higher octave. Mercury 
rules sight; Uranus rules clairvoyance. * 

Students who use^the “Planetary Hour Book” regular- 
ly are aware that events of Uranian nature occur almost 
invarfably in the planetary hours ruled by Mercury.

In Exodus 4, 14-16, the relationship between Mercury

flective qualitias of Mercury, through the profound, per- 
severing, reasoning processes of Saturn to the lightning- 
like flashes of Urani'an intuition. 


Stars and Planets * 


* i 

The word planet means “wanderer,” that is, a body 
which travels (transits) ;n< a particular orbit *in the 
zodiac within a certain period of time, its speed being 
known, calculations are made astronomically (see 
Ephemeris) which show the date when it wili transit 
through the Various signs and degreed of the zodiac; 
also \jrhen it will 'be in conjunction or other aspect with 
other 'planetary bodies. The calculated positions and 
aspects «afe u$ed by astrologers to deduqe tjie influence' 
of. such configurations in, accordance with the accented 
rules of astrology. 



PAitT V. • ADDITIONAL STUWES 


579 


To a casual observer the principal difference between 
a star and a planet is in the fact tl^at/the star gives a 
twinkling light* while a planet shines with a steady glow. 
Through a telescope the* planet appears as a disc. The 
light shed from a#pl|met is largely a reflection of that 
received from the Sun. 

Stars are often referred to as “fixed” because they do 
not traverse the signs of the zodiac as do the “wander- 
ers.” Some of ttfe largest stars are thought to £e great 
suns possessing their own systems. 

Natal astrology is more concerned with planets than 
with stars. Planets travel in the, zodiac which is a* belt 
of space on both sides of the ecliptic witjiin which are 
the orbits of the planets. In other words no stars are 
located within the boundaries of the zodiac. The stars 
are out in the constellations beyond the zodiacal circle. 
The boundary of the zodiac, as at present known, ex- 
tends to approximately eight degrees beyond the orbit 
of Pluto. 

Signs and Constellations 

The constellations have the same names as the signs: 
Aries, Taurus, etc., and at one time the signs and con- 
stellations of the same pame were side bf side, but due 
to a continual shift the signs and constellations do not 
now coincide. The “shift” is at the rite of approximate- 
ly fifty seconds of longitude per year, or one degree in 
about Seventy-two years. That is to say, ouj Vernal 
equinoctial point, Aries 0 degrees 0 •minutes, moves in 
backward order so Jhat it passes the constellation Pisces 
29th degree to 28th degree, and so on. At present (1944) 
the 0 decrees 0 minutes of tjie sign Aries is at about 10 
decrees of the constellation Pisces, astronomers assert. 
In 10 degrees more the beginning of the sign Aries wilj 
reach the re3r of the constellation Aquarius. At the* 
rate of approximately one degree in 72 yeaVs it will qpn-# 
tact Aquarius in about 720 %ears. 

• As the # orb of the Sun is 12* the equinoctial^ point at 
10* X indicates* that the Aquarian Age began *to da\^n 
# (by orb of influence) about ISO years ago, or at the time 
of* our Declaration of Independence for the freedom ofc 



580 


GENETHLIACAt ASTgOLOQY 


the nation. Note that the words, * independence and 
freedom, belongyo Aquarius.* 

However, far as the student is concerned in' the 
making of a horoscope, he need dot concern himself about 
the forementioned movement of t,hf v.hole solar system 
through the constellations, as that factor does not enter 
into the calculations for the birth cha'rt of an individual 
in which are considered mainly the movements of the 
bodies iji the zodiac, although it is accounted for in the 
influences attributed to the signs, as* shown in an article 
entitled, “Signs and Constellations” in the book, * rower- 
ful Planets.” 

The, Fixed Stars 

The zodiac 'may be pictured as comprising our solar 
system; stars are objects of other systems. Stars are so 
remote from the earth that thek* distance is best ex- 
pressed by the term "light-years” to state the number pf 
years which light, traveling at the rate of 186,000 miles 
a second, would require to reach our globe. Planets are 
so close, relatively, that their distance is but a few light- 
minutes. For instance, the Sun is approximately 
93,000,000 miles away but its light-distance is approxi- 
mately only five minutes. 

Remote though some of the ‘fixed stars are, their 
influence is near at hand, especially those which, are of 
great magnitude aryd not too far north or south of the 
zodiac. Although astrologers pay more attention to 
planets, the influence of the principal fixed stars is by no 
means neglected for their influence is important in a 
horoscope when in the same degree of longitude as one 
of the planets, especially if in one of the angles. For 
instance, the life of Empei'or Franz Josef of Austria was 
a succession of disasters mafiy of which were explained 
as attributable r.ot alone to the conjunction of Sun, 
Moon and Saturn, but to the fact that they were in con- 
junction with "Alphard” whose influence is a 'mixture 
of Saturn and Vepus.

constellatjons, puts the equinoctial at 3* of Pisces.

TRIGONS 

Triplicity of Sign 

The four triplicfti<£ .of signs manifest in humanity as 
four specific temperaments.* 

The Fiery Triplicity is represented by the choleric or 
bilious temperamenj, exhibiting a rash, feverish* easily- 
excited, or impulsive nature with inflammatory and 
bilious affections, sudden illness generally acute, but 
usually of short duration. # 

The fiery aigua ^present the vital spirit, the bodily 
heat with its elements of combustion, all of which incline 
to activity: political, military and speculative interests. 

To this triplicity of fire and spirit belong the little 
nature spirits commonly called Salamanders. 

The Earthly Triplicity is represented by the nervous 
temperament more especially noticed through Virgo and 
Capricorn, exhibiting alert, restless, .worried, agitated, 
neurasthenic characteristics, with predisposition to rheu- 
matism and chronic disorders. The earthjy signs repre- 
sent thp physical or temporal states all of which produce 
artistic, imaginative, studious and organizing tendencies. 

“Little folk of the hills and vales,” or Gnomes, a& they 
are usually called, have especial affinity for the triplicity 
of earth. 

The Airy Triplicity is represented by the sanguine 
tenjper«fment, exhibiting active circulation, plump body, 
good complexion, genial nature, •cheerful anticipation, 
good fellowship and general dexterity. The airy signs 
are said fo represent the relative or connective conditions' 
both within and without the body. Fomnstance, Qeiftini* 
rules the nervous system whidh connects the entire body ; 
U also rules those of kin, rfs brothers, steers, pousins 
and even* neighbors, with whom the native is associated. 
In this airy family we fincl another “relative" not ordifi- 
'arily included in that category, — Sylphs.

Has tendency toward illnesses arising irpm exhaustion 
or oveV-exertion.\

pulsed, lack of red corpuscles in the f blood and deficient

triplfcity is known as emotional, plastic, contemplative.

The*First Trigon of Houses 
first — Fifth — Ninth 

The # First House, space or segment of a chart is re- 
ferred to as the Ascendant. Due tc*the line of its angle 
being level with the horizon, and due also to the garth's 
rotation from west to east, planets ascending from 
under the Earth become visible as they* appear at this 
line called the Cusp of First House. 

The zodiacal sign and degree passing over the cusp of 
First House at moment of birth bear a very important 
influence upon the native’s life, health, disposition, ten- 
dencies and personality, according to the nature of the 
sign occupying this cusp, much of which may be deter- 
mined by referring to the chapter headed, “Influence of 
Ascending Signs," in Part III. 

*The First House has more influence tlftm any other on 
the pdrson or "mask." It largely determines the various 
changes constantly taking place in tne health ^nd consti- 
tution, also the mental and emotional states, causing 
reflexes and reactions according to the nature <jf ttie sign 
and any planets which may be in that house. 

Any planets occupying that space designated as the 
First House also have a sv<*ng influence on the life, ac- 
cording to their nature. Likewise, do such other ele- 
ments as prominent fixed stars, the Moon's Nodes and 
Pars Fbrturtae. 

It is therefore very important to *have the* I^curate 
birth time in order to ascertain correctly, what sign and 
degree -are .parsing over this critical angfe. JThere is a 
vast difference between* the endowments of the twelve 
?igns.

The^ Fifth House indices our children or the lack of

life portrayed in the Jhoroscope : honor, reputation, pop-

they correspond with the earthly signs: Tenth House, 
Capricorn; Second, Taurus; Sixth, Virgo. The Tenth 
House also has reference to the government, the presi-

tinguished or famous persons; also national trade, credit, 
popularity oj power; the mother, father-in-law, the 
fathers and property of persons ruled by tfie Seventh

, House, counting the Fourtll as First.

The Fourth Trigon of Houses 

i * - 

Fourty ■ Eighth — Twelfth 1 * * 

The fourth trigon includes the Fourth, Twelfth and 
Eighth Houses, which are respectivejy^angular, cadent 
and succedent, and are in some respects similar to the 
water signs Cancer, Pisces and Scorpio.' 

Fourth .House: The lower angle or Fourth House is 
called the 0 bottom of Heaven, the depth of night, the 
grave, the den or cave of the planets. As the opposite 
house shows what man may rise to in the world, this, on 
the contrary, shows wha* and when shall be his fall, 
decrepitude and decay. This house bears significance 
to the termination, conclusion or end of every under- 
taking. It denotes elderly people, the father, wealth of 
first brother or sister, the private enemies of children, 
the profession of husband or of open enemies, the sick- 
ness of friends, the friends of servants or employees. 
This house denotes matters relating to land, property, 
houses, foundations, mines, buried treasure; homesteads, 
real estate, farms, ancient dwellings, gardens, orchards, 
fields, pastures, sheep folds. In the purchase of ground 
it shows whether it is fruitful, barren, stony, hilly, 
marshy, etc. 

I once greatly surprised a client, who was asking a 
question about some land, by telling him that at times 
it was as much as four feet under water. This was 
shown in the Horary chart by four degrees of Cancer on 
Fourth' Cusp, Moon in ( Fourth adverse to Mars. Investi- 
gation proved the. land subject to backwater from the 

tides. He did not purchase. 4 

< 4 

Twelfth House : The second in order of human afflic- 
tion is the Twelfth House, representing tribulation, sor- 
row and self-undoing, arising either from treachdhv or 
persecution by setref enemies, from the bofids of restric- 
tion, or .from want, poverty, ignorance or disease. It 
indicates ’such things as kidnapping, poisoning, bootleg- 
ging, opiufti* trade, smuggling, blackmailing. It • rules 
such places as jails, hospitals and other institutions of 
correction or detention, or any enclosure where liberty is

curtailed. Ttyis /is the Fourth from the Ninth House 
which relates to the higher mind; it is fou/* houses 
from the Third which relates to ftyc lower or ordinary 
mental processes; thesefore, this Twelfth House relates 
to the occult or jecret workings of the mind and denotes 
also schemes, plots/ frauds, swindles, intrigue, cabals, 
conspiracy, or obsession. * , 

I*once shocked a client, when after looking at his natal 
chart, I said, “Yoft have suffered greatly through fear of 
imprisonment due to having shot and killed a man in a 
public house." He paled and admitted that it was so, 
happening in a saloon brawl but done in self (Jefense. 
The Moon, ruler of the Tweltth, was in the First (fear 
# of imprisonment; (Moon rules public plafces, also houses) 
located in Leo right between Mars and Saturn. Mars 
conjunction Saturn indicates violence. Mars rules fire- 
arms and shooting; Saturn rules death. Leo being mas- 
culine indicated that the victim was male. The client had 
the Sun in the Ascendant sufficiently far enough away 
not to be afflicted by these planets, and Sun being the 
“dispositor” of them all, was king in his own domar 
consequently, he was never found out ; i. e., it was»nevcr 
discovered who did the shooting.

•Eighth House: The last phase of man’s experience 
is that of death: his transition or his rebirth into another 
state of manifestation, the Eighth* House denoting dis- 
incarnation as the First House denotes incarnation. It 
represents dissolution or the chemistry denoted by the 
words, “ashes to ashes, dust to dust," concerning the

By Way of Interesting'^esearch 
Let Us Investigate tRe Intriguing Subject of Twins, 

Not long ago a ^vell-knoWn Sunday newspaper devoted 
aft entire page to featuring a discourse on this subject by 
a noted writer who skillfully analysed tendencies, traits, 
“accidents/’ heritage, and whatnot in an effort to account 
for tvrins, but finally admitted the futility of nis effort. 
However, before letting the subject fall negatively flat 
he made the remark, (by way of apology, I suppose) : 
“Even astrology offers no solution !” 

But astrology does offer a solution. At least if astro- 
logical principles aro not taken into consideration, the 
subject of twin births remains very puzzling. Sometimes 
• twins look alike; sometimes they do not. Sometimes they 
are of the same sex; sometimes opposite. 

What woulcl be more natural than to surmise, when 
born very near the same time, that twins should look 
very much alike? But often they do not. To the con- 
trary/ often -when b<vn with the lapse of an hour or so 
between, they look very much alike, when in such cases 
it would seem that they should appear dissimilar. 

By way of illustration let us consider a case which 
came under my observation, — a very unusual oase and 
one very difficult to explain satisfactorily f except by 
means of jfetroiogy. I spent twenty years in ihe city 
where these twin% weVe bon\ and had fhe opportunity of 
watching their development from birtji to maturity. 

The case surely proved interesting, because, although 
born bjut fpur minutes apart, the boy and girl grew to 
manifest very dissimilar characteristics in addition to be- 
ing quite different in appearance. 

» Due to the earth's rotation on its axis; .making a com- 
plete ttim fcvery twenty # -four hours, a new degree of the 
zodiac appears on the eastern horizon every four mirfutes 
.and a new sign appears every two hours. .Astrological 1 



GEHETHLXACA L ASTROLOGY 


,592 


researchers are very particular as 'to the, time of a birth, 
in order to determine exactly what degree and sign were 
on the eastern horizon when the blessed event occurred. 

“Each star has its own glory/' quojte^. the Bible. As- 
trologers have determined that each of the twelve signs 
has its own distinctive qualities which are bestowed 
upon individuals according to their birthday. In fact, 
during the long ages in which some of the brighcest 
minds of t*ie world have given attention to the influence 
of the signs of the zodiac, it has developed that each of 
the 360 degrees, constituting the zodiacal circle, has a 
specific, influence, and i^ various ways tjiey express 
themselves in individuals in whose life they have associa- 
tion through relationship at time of birth. 

At the moment of birth one of the twelve signs of the 
zodiac, and one degree out of the thirty degrees consti- 
tuting that particular sign, are ascending on the eastern 
horizon, and these largely determine the personality and 
characteristics of the individual. 

It i^ obvious that, as the earth turns one degree in 
four minutes, thirty degrees or one sign will appear and 
pass up over the horizon every two hours ; while during 
the twenty-four hours of one day all the signs and de- 
grees will have passed the horizon. Consequently, it 
would seem that during the day (and night) there would 
be born twelve types (signs) of characters, with thirty 
variation* (degrees) in each sign-type. By this is seen 
one reason tvhy humars differ, instead of being all alike, 
when bojn on the same day. To accentuate this dissim- 
ilarity among thos£ closely related in point of time (but 
disassofiated in po\nt of space) we have also to consider 
such elements as race, caste and hereditary strain. 

However, we are mainly concerned with potent astro- 
logical influences.*. If the earth's rotation on its axis were 
the only factor involved, we would have the comparative- 
ly limiter, number of 360 ch* racters representing 360 
zodiacal degrees^ differentiated 1 (in groups of thirty) by 
the twelve signs. But in reality this number is augmented 
by the fact that during rotation on its axis in one day 
vhe earth is ?lso transiting one degree along its orbit,

593 


thereby each^ay/giving a new variety to the types and 
the 360 variations, of those born ir^ that rotation. This 
variety multiplies for approximately^thirty days during 
which time the # earth will have traversed thirty degrees 
in orbit and will ^h^n enter the next sign of the zodiac, 
to repeat the daily variations with a different lot of 
individuals under ti new month-sign. The orbital transit 
from one sign to another determines the type of individ- 
uality while, as before stated, the rotation on a*is deter- 
mines the type of [fersonality. Thus, people botn in the 
same birth-month sign inherently possess similar indiv- 
idualities, but the date of the month*and the time of day 
both operate to provide different personalities. • 

Astrologically the individuality is designated by the 
apparent zodiacal position of the Sun; personality is 
designated by the ascending degree and sign due to the 
relative axial position of the earth as indicated from any 
certain latitude. 

Individuality may be considered as inherent qualities, 
tendencies and* latent powers. Personality may be re- 
garded as the style or nature of expression. 

Individually he is what he knows himself to be; by 
his personality (expression) he is known to others. (Per- 
sonal meaning mask.) It has been said \fy esoteric stu- 
dents t+iat your ascenaing sign in this birth was your 
Sun-sign in inr; preceding incarnation; what # is your 
Sun-sign now will be your ascending sign in the next life. 
If your Sun-sign and your Rising-sign are now t Jie %ame, 
you are repeating because having fafled to express pro- 
perly or fully*in your previous incarnatioji.
