Transits throdgh whole zodiac in one year. 

Equals, 360 variations of individuality yearly. 

Adding the variations produced by these Earth move- 
ments, we have a t^tal of 131,760 astrological types. 

To* this great variety of differences manifested in 
human beings, ^other astrological factors must be reck- 
oned. Among a few of those which make considerable 
difference might be mentioned : The Sun either above or 
below the horizon at moment of birth; the Moon above 
or below ; the Moon's distance from the ascendant ; any 
planets (or none) in the ascendant; the location and 
position of the planet ruling the ascendant; the location 
and position of the other planets by sign and house, the 
latitude and longitude of the place of birth; etc. All 
these factors act to produce the innumerable specimens 
of humanity. 

It is amusing to hear people say, "I do not believe in 
astrology, because if it were true all those born >on the 
same day f would be^alike ” This simply sliows that such 
persons are basing a belief on something they do not 
understand, or have not investigated. In the foregoing 
it i$ clearly shown »that astrology, probably better than 
anything else, accounts for the difference m people, not 
only in nations and races,, but alio foV the differences in 
members of ou* own families.

minutes, thus, *on an average of every two hours a new

eighteen milc c ,per second and thus transiting from one

1,500,000 miles per day; a great journey of* over

Universe makers 


Kepler, Johann, a fan^ous astrologer ; knov*n as one of the 
founders of modern astionoray. Born December 27, 1571, at Weil, 
in the duchy of Wsirtpnjberg, where his grandfather was burgo- 
master. A theological occupation was selected for him and in 
1584 he entered the seminary at Adelberg tuid two years later 
entered that of Maulbronn where he brilliantly acquired degreea 
This won him admittance to the university of Tubingen, where 
he imbibed a store, of classical erudition, which in tho9e days was 
thoroughly impregnated with astrological lore. Through the 
private instructions of his teacher and life-long friend, Michael 
Maestlln, a mathematical astrologer, he Jearned the principles of 
the then new Copernican system. His classical erudition had led 
him through the Ptolemaic and ^istotllan ideas. ThiA astro- 
mathematical training and the new system caused him to turn 
aside from a career In ministry to that of Science, as the old 
theological concepts did not fit well with the new discoveries of 
the heavens. 

Early in 1594 he accepted the chair of science at Gratz. Of 
Gratz, the Encyclopedia Britannlca states that Kepler taught in 
the Paradise or Lutheran school where Charles II had burned 
20,000 Protestant books and through oppressive measures the city 
again fell under* the authority of Rome. But in Kepler’s time it 
had been restored to Lutheran regime. 

Kepler's engagement at Gratz ended in 1598 when the archduke 
Ferdinjind, on assuming Ihe government of his hereditary domin- 
ions, issued an edict of banishment against Protestant preachers 
and professors. Kepler fled with others to the Hungarian frontier, 
but by* favor of the Jesuits was recalled to his chair. However, 
conditions red- ;ned unfavorable and unlettled so h$ applied to 
his Alma Mater for a place on the faculty, but upon his refusal to 
subscribe unconditionally to the formula adopted by the*theolog- 
ians at Tubingen, the doors were closed against him. t 

i 

The first wgrk completed by Kepler at Prague was an ilnposing 
treatise on Astrologyfentitted, “De Fundaments Astrologiae Certior- 
ibus" (1602), in which he stressed*the importance of the infuence 
of planetary aspects, adding eight new ones to the science which 
n&intaln a placd among the sixteen planetary aspects In use by 
astrologers of today. If was in this *book he made a statement 
that has given pause to many a skeptic, a quotation which hUs 
become famous with the passing of time, one, which has with- 
stood every assault by critics of astrology ; for when -wroth 
it he was speaking as the result, of actual, practical observation, 
research and Experience, while /hose who have .attempted to dis- 
count the value of what he found, spoke only on 'the basis of what 
they thought or what they believed, unsupported by stay evidence 
which would invalidate Keplbr's findings. He stood on the solid 
ground of knowledge gained by investigation. Here is the state- 
ment which no amount of heckling can, gainsay : * , *»

“The planets forming angles (aspects) upofa the earth by their 
luminous Jbeams, of strength to stir up the virtu? of sublunary 
things, have compelled my unwillingly belief.” 

Limitations of space permit only a sketchy outline of his work, 
but his life merits detailed study. He was attacked by fever and 
died Nov. 15 (N. S.) 1630, in his flfty-niut/i year. 

He was an indefatigable worker in the development of the nejv 
branch of science for he saw that through the establishment of 
astronomy, the older science of astrology to which he % gave 
allegiance would accordingly advance. His idea of the universe 
was essentially Pythagorean and Platonic ; {he basis of his atui: 
logical knowledge was Ptolemaic, but his method of computation 
was Copernican, and th*s geocentric, considering the earth as a 
(not the) center of influence. His extensive literature still exists. 
It was 'collected and securedaby Catherine II, in 1724, and long 
inaccessibly deposited in the observatory Pulkowa, but at length 
came to light and was ably edited in the first complete edition by 
Dr. Ch. Frisch (Frankfort 1858-71, 8 volumes.) A careful biog- 
raphy is appended, founded mainly on Kapler’s private notes and 
other authentic documents. Various other later editions of h^s 
works were compiled by different authors, but each succeeding 
biographer omitted a little more of Kepler’s astrological knowl- 
edge and teachings, being concerned mostly in advocating his 
Copernican system of astronomy, until now the biographers omit 
all his astrological matter. 1

Galileo Galilei, born February 18, 1564, at Pisa, Italy; char- 
acterized by modern biographers as, “one of the greatest of the 
early experimental philosophers.” At \he age of eighteen he 
entered the univer&ty of Pisa and upon attendance at the lectures 
of the celebrated physicinn and bntanisV. Andrea Cesalplno. 

» * r 

At that time medicine and botany, as taught In universities, 
were according to the astrological doctrines of Hippocrates and 
Galen. Their prescriptions were invariably prefaced by a large 
symbol of Jupiter as a silent invocation to the healing Influences 
of that | planet. Indeed, the symbol prevails andcis in use by 
physicians of the present day, although in the course of time as 
first principles became neglected, the symbol became perverted 
from Zi to R and i!ts ancient significance no longer understood. 

Galilei, with his accustomed zeal, also wrote an exposition 
favoring the new Copernican, as opposed to the older Aristotllan 
system, for which he was cited to Rome by the Inquisition, where, 
on thf 22nd °f Jbne 1633, in the church of Snnta Maria sopra 
Minerva/ he was cczhpelled to recant on threat of torture, and 
sentenced to spend the remain! ngVy ears of his life iii strict retire- 
ment. He died qn the 8th of January 1042 closing a long life of, 
triumph ahd humiliation. It took a brave man to„be<an vp-to-date 
astrologer in those days.

YOUR BEST LOCATION 
How Change of Residence Affects the Horoscope 

Now and then a student inquires whether it is proper 
to make the progressed chart from Tables of Houses for 
native's present place of abode instead of for the place 
of .birth. 

it is not practical to substitute house cusps of present 
abode for those of place of birt£, because the further 
away the native has removed the larger the number of 
degrees difference it would make on the house cusps, and 
when tabulating aspects in the progressed chart it must 
be borne in mind that each degree is equal to one month 
by the Moon's motion; and one degree is equal to one 
year in the Sun's motion. This process of changing cusps 
would, therefore, change Lunar aspects to these cusps by 
several months. Solar aspects by several years, making 
the aspects fall due earlier or later, according to whether 
the native Had removed north or south of birthplace; 
that is, according to whether there would be a larger or 
smaller number of degrees on the cusps by the change. 

If the native has ^noved from the by-th place, making 
t"he chart for latitude of present residence changes Lunar 
aspects to, cusps by several months, whereas, in reality 
the person nfay be but a few hours time distance removed 
from place of birth. 

Using the radical latitude, or that of birth p\ace, makes 
a different in the falling due of aspects of only a few 
hours ; i. e., the difference in time between place of birth 
and present residence; thf time woulchbe earlier oft later, 
hccording to^whether native had moved east or west of 
birthplace. This difference of 'a few hours is of slight 
importance when one considers that it is a very difficult 
matter to set ^ particular day for the operation of, a pro- 
gressed aspect, especially those whdse influence lasts a 
month or more, such as Moon conjunctipn Ascendant, or 
Sun sextile the M. C., as such influences’ begin to exert 
an effect frotn four to -eight weeks prior to the actual 
completion of the aspect, by the Moon, or many months i 
by the Sun, v f iz., 2* orb = 2 years with the 0 ©. , »• 



efc GEN^THLIACAX ASTROLOGY 

■■■■ ■ ■■■■■ — ■■■ 1 1 — ; 

The progressed chart requires the natural progress of 
the house® cusps, just as it does of the planets. At the 
moment of birth ( *he planets have house poritions accord- 
ing to the latitude of the birth place.* At the first inde- 
pendent indrawn breath of the babe, thte prevailing vibra- 
tions “cast” or set the tendencies of the body according 
to those house positions, as well as by c the aspects and" 
the signs occupied by the planets. Those particular house 
positions of the planets at that time are “time markers” 
of when certain conditions will appear in the life of^tftiiw 
individual. Thus: if > is 3‘ below the ascending degree 
and it is ruler of 6th h&use, its position in relation to the 
Ascendant denotes that vhen the Ascendant has ad- 
vanced 3* it will be in conjunction with the place of Sat- . 
urn, denoting illness.* That is, due to the planetary 
rays as affecting that particular latitude at that birth 
time certain forces were timed for manifestation, and # 
those times can only be correctly calculated by use of • 
the original latitude from which the native received its 
“cast.” Planets thus affecting the “angles” of a chart 
are said to be more productive of manifestations than 
those |o other cusps, because the angles are more “ac- 
tive” ; producing more noticeable and quicker action than 
other cusps. 

Although it is not practical to substitute house cjisps 
of present abode for those of place of birth, *it may be 
well to use*the cusps made for the present abode as a 
supplement for investigation, (assuming you have moved 
from the -birth latitude), to see whether or not in such 
chart tl\e indications to cusps in any way Qoincide or 
conflict with the testimonies indicated by house cusps of 
the original progressed chart latitude.

To continue this example, let us suppose that - U had

of moving south of birth latitude to 40° N. as before illus-

move for the child as the beneficial influence of V opera-

influence of Ascendant 6 V timed (at birth) to be opera- 
tive now. 

In other words, moving from the original latitude does 
not change or negate the indications a* timed or meas- 
ured from birth, but a change of residence to another 
latitude may create other conditions which tend to ac- 
centuate or modify those which are denoted by the orig- 
inal birth place latitude. 

The New Locality 

The foregoing comments on change of residence are 
concerned with the reasons for not substituting the lat- 
itude of the usual progressed horoscope for that of a 
new latitude to which the native may have removed. 

We cannot evade the duly timed operations of ''Nature, 
which were "cast" or timed at birth to manifest* at cer- 
t tain periods of life, but removing to a more suitable en- 
vironment "may help to improve our expression of ,the 
influence of aspects as they occur, so that one place may 
„seem to be "luckier" or more fortunate thamanqther. >, 




004 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROU>GT 


The usual procedure in the case of removal " is as 
follows: 

Note the amount of time difference between the birth 
place and new abode. Add this amcvm* of time to the 
birth time if the removal is to the east of birth place; 
subtract the amount if removal is west.' 

With the new time thus derived make a chart in the 
usual way using the latitude of new abocfe for the hoj^ 
cusps, but insert the planets in the same signs and de- 
grees occupied at the feriginalbirth time. This will change 
the planets to new house positions. It is these house 
positions of the planets that are of particular significance 
in the new environment. As the houses show the direc- 
tion in which zodiacal tendencies and planetary influ- 
ences manifest, the importance of c the changed house 
positions is obvious. For instance, if Jupiter is thus 
moved into the midheaven the new environment is likely 
to improve the honors, credit, prestige and profession of 
the native; if Saturn has become tenant of the sixth 
house the native's health is apt to be impaired. 

o 

How to Find the Best Location 

In the effort tb choose a better location several charts 
should be made according to the foregoing rijle, each for 
a locality the native nas reason to believe*Vould be ad- 
vantageous as regards climate, chances for successful 
operation pi his particular kind of work, etc., and from 
them choose the locsfiity* indicated by the be^t chart. 

As before stated, removal only* changes the time of 
day when progressed aspects, or transits, culminate; 
which is a minor matter considering thtt when they 
actually begin is not definitely known, and for various 
reasons they are often set into operation earlier than the 

exact, moment df becoming partile. r 
» * 

In other words, changing the natal chart house cusps 
and positions of planets as regards their original house 1 
positions (but not changing planets’ sign' and degree) 
to conform with the amount of time difference and the 
npw latitude, provides p chart which shows the trend of

changes to b$ experienced in the new place and the con- 
dition^ to lie met through the ability and characteristics 
with which' the native was originally endowed; that is, 
the radix planetary 'influences are expressed through 
different circun^stynces and environment. For example: 
if Jupiter was originally in the tenth house and removal 
# took it into the eleventh, there would be tendency to 
carry on the profession more through social endeavors. 
If Jupiter were # carried into the ninth house, the tendency 
^ytftuld be to change the profession to embrace publishing, 
foreign affairs, law, science or religion. 

•

ruling plane't into the twelfth or sixth houses ; the former 
‘implies limitations ; the latter, illness.

Transits and Change of Residence 

A student having moved from birth place on the 
Atlantic coast to present residence on the Pacific coast, 
wants r to know whether it will have any effect on the 
aspects of transiting planets. 

Although eastern time is three hours in advance, i. e., 
later in the day than Pacific time, that makes practically 
no difference in the effect of transits. Whjle the change 
of residence altered the clock time in its relation to that 
of birth-place, it does not noticeably alter anything con- 
cerning' art aspect itself, jexcept that it appears to begin 
and to manifest at an earlier hour. However, the time 
of the beginning*of an aspect is an intangible matter, 
and e^en if knowp exactly would be of little importance 
because the inception of its influence is so subtle and 
gradual that some time must elapse before it produces 
any recognized or appreciable significant influence on 
either body or mind. 

For instance, the Sun's apparent movement in the 
zodiac is .a transit of approximately one degree per day 
and^it has "an orb of influence qf 12 degrees, therefore, 
when approaching a planet in a nativity it begins the 
conjunction at some time on the twelfth day prior to its, 

* • • r 



PART V # ADDITIONAL STUDIES 


609 


actual «cbn junction!, regardless of where the native may 
then be residing. # That beginning of the aspect, is imper- 
ceptible, but*day by day its influence grows in power 
and its expression becomes more notrceabve until the day 
of its climax (pajtije), after which its influence rapidly 
diminishes. 

*When the Sun reaches the final degree constituting 
the conjunction it transits through it during the whole 

^ and in a nativity it would indeed be difficult to 
n*ine what time of that day the influence would be 
most strikingly manifest, although > it would be but a 
simple problem in arithmetic to calculate the exact time 
of the conjunction, which of cfourse would occur at a 
time that is three hours earlier on the Pacific coast than 
clocked in Eastern time. 

•While, as before stated, removal from one place to 
* another does not alter the influence of a transit aspect, 
per se, there is a difference but it is psychological rather 
than astrological. That difference is in the human re- 
sponse to environment. In other words, a person's 
reaction to a planetary aspect when he is in Alaska 
will be # quite different from his expression of the same 
aspect/if he were resting in Florida, because environ- 
ment determines much of the nature anc> quality of hu- 
man activity. The channels for the manifestation of that 
aspect would be altered by the difference in climate, 
clothing, food, occupation, opportunities, personal con- 
tacts, mental attitude, etc. Physicians often wisely sug- 
gest “a change of climate" to patients, but un?ess they 
understand ^fetrology are not always able to suggest the 
place best suited to the* purpose. 

UNIVERSE MAKERS 

* 

Ptolemy (Claudius Ptolemaeus) was a Greek ajtrologer, astron-., 
omer and geograpner of the second century, A. D. Ptolebay’s 
great work entitled, Megale Syiftaxis tes Astronomias, ly more 
popularly known by the Arabic title, “Almagest,” **hich associates 
astrological and astronomical matters. His distinctly tysfrologlcal 
book is known ai the “Tetrabiblos or Quadripartite,” being four 
books on the influence of the stars (planets). His system of 
astronomy was finally superceded by that of Coperpicus. (Se^ 



610 


GENETHLIACAL * ASTROLOGY 


Astronomy — Ptolemaic system in the encyclopedia.) The' adoption 
of the Copernican system by modern astronomers, in no Way in- 
validated othe astrological Information compiled by Ptolemy from 
the Arabians, Persians, 'Greeks and Egyptians. While he taught 
that the earth wns round and turned on its axis, he took the 
apparent movement of the Sun around" tho earth into his calcula- 
tions instead of the earth's motion around the,. Sun. The influence 
of the planets per se remains the same Regardless of whether one 
uses the former or the latter method, that Js, Saturn is always 
binding, cooling, solidifying, congealing, retarding. 

There are many points in which modern sciences, and mcdern 
astronomy Jin particular, have reaped inc&’cufable benefits from 
the labors' and researches of this great scholar. He not 
transmitted to us the observations and principal discoveries of 
remoter periods, but ha£ enriched and augmented them with his 
own. He corrected Hipparchus’ catalogue of fixed stars, and 
formed tables for the calculation of the motions of the Sun, Mo n 
and planets. He was, in fact, the first who collected the scatter. d 
or detached observations of Aristotle, Hipparchus, Posidonius aud 
others on the economy of the world, and digested them into a 
system, which he set forth in a work oft thirteen volumes, known 
as “The Great Construction.” 

It has been truly said, “Ptolemy’s order, inexact as it was 
enabled subsequent observers to derive a plausible account of the 
motions of the planets, to foretell eclipses and to improve geog- 
raphy." That is to say: Ptolemy’s system reprekented the actual 
phenomena of the heavens as they really appear to a spectator 
on tlfe earth. It is clear, therefore, that Ptolemy’s astrology is 
just as applicable to modern astronomy as it was to his time. 

This conclusion cannot be invalidated r Dy the objection whicl^ is 
frequently urged against astrology that the signs are continually 
moving from their ancient positions by the precession* of the 
equinoxes. The objection is ill-founded in that lias no relation 
to the facti Ptolemy was aware of this movement of the signs, 
and has fully provided for it in the first book of the Tetrabiblos 
where ft is made clear that the respective influences ascribed to 
the signs were considered b§ him to be appurtenant to the places 
they oozupied, and not to the stars which might tte within their 
spaces. He clearly states that the point otf the vernal equinox 
is always the beginning of thfe zodiac and that the 30 degrees 
following that poltft always retain the virtues attributed to the 
sign Aries although the stars originally in Aries fray have shifted 
from those degrees. Ptolefny is so explicit on this matter that It 
appears he anticipated such objection ana took pains to show the 
argument had no v foundatlon in fact He was considered the most 
distinguished scientist of his day.

T ARIES V 

Aries is called fiery, hot, dry, inflammatory, choleric, 
sterile, violent, bestial, intemperate, equinoctial, eastern, 
cardinal, movable, positive, mascjali^e, mental-motive, 
and of short ascension. 

Aries is the "first, or head sign, of the zodiac, and is 
known to have influence upon the head and face. The 
Sun enters this sign on or about the 25st of Marcfi each 
year, constituting the Vernal Ingress. While it is nr uns 
eastern sign we celebrate Easter, and it is usually ob- 
served by wearing new headgear. So, although they may 
not be aware of it, every one who follows the fashion of 
donning a new hat at this time is paying homage* to 
astrology of the ancients. 

The Gospel says, “I have exalted thee, O Father,” and 
in this sign the Sun is exalted, particularly in the 19th 
degree. It is the fall of Saturn (Satan) for the Lord oi 
Hosts is exalted. It is the detriment of Venus, being a 
lustful sign. Aries has been represented by the cock, 
denoting boastful or fighting qualities, but is more gen- 
erally symbolized by the ram, being in nature rash, 
hardy, springy, lascivious and combative. Aries repre- 
sents the gate of gold (or) on the east, therefore called 
an or-ient-al fcign, denoting the, beginning of right as- 
cension (R. A. = Ra) or the original first Right Ascension 
Midheavren, R. A. M., (ram.) 

Indhe New Testament it probably answers to Mark, 
(Mar kt) The na*rne f ascribed to the eastern angle, or 
angel, is Malchidial, while of the twelve prophets it is 
said to represent Malachi ; of the twelve apostles. Mat* 
thi^s.

8 TAURUS 8 

Taurus* is called earljtily, fixed, cold, moist, semi-fruit- 
ful, bestial, femininf? melancholy, of short ascension. 

It is the second sign ctf the zodiac, the Sun beginning 
its apparent yearly transit through it oh or about April 
20tl\. It is the middle, or fixed sign, of the spring quarter 
% and is symbolized J>y a bull with unusual home on head 
aiiu \aes, appearing in a rage, crouched to rusfl forward 
with fierce energy; representing a stubborn and tena- 
cious nature. 0 

. Taurus is one of the four fixed sign^ comprising the 
Cherubim. It was the original Bull, or Baal, of the 
Assyrian religious rites, sometimes referred to as "The 
§acred Bull” or "The Golden Calf,” emblematic of the 
period a few thousand years ago when the solar system 
was precessing through that constellation. Our May 
day festival with its may pole and garlands are the re- 
mains of the ‘ancient Egyptian festival celebrating the 
entrance of the Sun into Taurus. 

The; Gospel apparently alludes to Haggai as a prophet 
and Thaddeus as an«apostle of this sign, and the name 
of this angle, or angej, is Asmodeus. Ephraim (said to

seems to be connected with this sign. In Genesis 49, 22

be represented by the garland usually pictured around 
tjie bijlPs neck.

and the biblical description of the wrafrh of the Lord:

% CANCER S 

’ Cancer' is called movable, watery* fruitful, maternal, 
domestic, feminifte, tropical, solstitial mule, cold, moist, 
phlegmatic, cardinal* bf long ascension. 

The Sun enters fancer bn or about June 21st, and ap- 
parently transits through the sign by July 22nd. Refer- 
ence to any Ephemeris will show that the Sun apparently 
reaches its highest p*>int of north declination (23 # degrees 
27 minutes) and remains in it for three days, apparently 
standing still, after which it slowly sfcarts, crablike, back- 
ward toward its south declination. The middle djy of 
the three mentioned is therefore the longest day of the 
year. 

The crab represents possession and retention; it car- 
ries its house on its back. The symbol is drawn from 
•tfie human breasts, emblematic of motherhood, to nur- 
ture, cherish, to ripen, to carry, to bear. Although it has 
land-travel facilities, the crab is a creature born of water. 
The Egyptians represented this sign with the Scarabaeus 
which apparently lives underground as a grub, tJien 
emerges, spreads wings and flies through the air, em- 
blematic of the soul's# incarnation, experiences and re- 
lease. The Egyptians made much of this emblem, not 
because of its beauty, 6ut because it was a figure of the 
zodiac, a staf-sign of perfected being*, symbolical of pro- 
gress from darkness to light, from bondage to* freedom, 
from death to resurrection, from the vicissitudes of time 
to secure possession of the treasure?# of eternity. 

Within the confines* of thg constellation Cancer is 
Praesepe, the manger or "mare's nest." # The word indi- 
cates offsprings the young^ the innumerable seed, the 
multitude. In moderji astronomy - it is often pictured as 
a beehives. Originally it was "an ass between two bui**w 
dens" — between two partitions forming a manger or crib 
a place where asses were fed, f a fold, stall, stable, a fiaven, 
a place of rest, an inn, a house, a home.

_ likened to .a strong ass which reclines between two folds

St LEO St 

Leo is called fiery % hot, dry, choleric, barren, masculirie, 
positive, bestial, fixed, northern, 6f tong ascension. Some 
of the older authors add : commanding, fortunate, feral, 
broken, strong, bitter. 

The Sun enters Leo on July 22nd. It is universally 
symbolized by a lion, whose nature if represents. It 
symbolizes the fervid heat of July and August when the 
Sun has attained its greatest power. It represents a 
type of the electric, fiery mind whose expression is often 
destructive, whose energy is often rapaciops and daring, 
kingly, commanding yet generous. The Sun is ruler pf 
this sign. It is the detriment of the recalcitrant Uranus 
who will bow to no king, and exaltation of the inspira- 
tional Neptune. Its colors are orarfge, yellow, flaxen and 
golden. 

Leo is represented by the ancients as the Nemean 
Lion, which leaped down from the skies and was killed 
by Hercules. Leo is the emblem of violence and fury in 
the * hieroglyphical writings. Properly, its destructive 
force is to destroy His enemies: darkness, evil and 
ignorance. In the ancient maps the lion is shown just 
above Hydra, tne great dragon, viith his claws about to 
rend the serpent. It also properly means to bring sal- 
vation (te bring light) to His people. Re&el, or Regulus, 
a mighty fixed star in the heart of the Lion (now about 
29° in Leo) just above the mighty paws, refers to “the 
feet that crush/' ?t i# said of the Messiah, “He shall 
tread < upon the ..serpent, and trapiple,.the dragon under 

His feet/'

its discovery?” I took the time when the article was 
missed and made a horary chart for that date. As the 
Sun was in the second house I said, “The article.was 
round and golden.” “Yes it was my watch.” The ruler 
of seventh house (tb£ thief) was the Moon and it was 
ir> the fourth house. So I informed h?r; “The person 
who took it was a woVnan and is right now in the build- 
ing where you* reside. She is dressed in white, (Moon) 
s a common sort of person, perhaps a servant* or house- 
ceeper; the Moon is in aspect to Uranus, therefore she 
s not young and is peculiar in %om$ ways; th<f Moon is 
n Aries, therefore she is of fair complexion; her part of 
the house should 1 be the top # and corrfer room; as the 
■uler of the Ascendant (V) is* in good aspect to Uranus, 
tiler of the second (the variable in question,) you can 
ecover it by adopting unusual methods.” The lady 
■ecognized jone of the maids from this description wfio 
.lad a corner, attic room, was middle-aged and quitf 
eccentric. Instead of accusjng her of fheft the lady'said, 
“I left my w&tch on the table ; I presume you put it away 
•for safe keeping?” The woman was speechless for a 
momenf the’n-said, "'Yes# ma'am, I took it upstairs and 
put it on. the top shelf of my closet where no one could 
see it.” This incident proves the practical vjlue of 
•astrological knowledge.

In the anatomy and functions of the body Virgo has^ 
an important bearing; its domain is the bowels or 'abdo- 
men. It indicates t{ie disorders mcidenfy such as colic, 
obstructions, coldness, worms, dysentery, diarrhoea, 
cholera, enteralgia, peritonitis, constipation, malnutrition 
and affections of the intestinal regions.^ 

Virgo affects the nervous system considerably. Mer- 
cury, “the messenger of the Gods,” is rqler of this 5>ign 
and Mercery's influence over the nervous system is an' 
astrological fact. Mercury rules the action of the* mind, 
hence, disorders of tire bowels react on the mental con- 
dition; likewise, disturbed mental states, business or 
domestic worries reflect adversely on the bowels, there- 
fore Virgo people are benefitted by paying attention to 
diet, hygiene, sanitation and mental poise. Hypochon- 
dria is characteristic of this sign, Y/hile absorption, as- 
similation, selection and utilization are Virgo functions'.* 
Its action is chylifactive. It is associated chiefly with 
the duodenum, jejunum, ileum, mesentery and periton- 
eum; with obliquus, pyramidalis, rectus and transversalis 
of abdomen muscles; with gastric, superior and inferior 
mesenteries arteries; with portal, hepatics, umbilical and 
intestinal veins. 

# 

Diet is an important factor to Virgo people. Meate 
should be regular and food well masticated. Foods, es- 
pecially cereals, wh>~h regulate the bowels properly 
should be d'hosen. It is said that a vegetarian diet, well 
proportioned, is favorable. Strong drinks or condi- 
ments sholild be avoided,. 

The 'principal characteristics of, desirable nature for 
further cultivation arc described by the words: thought- 
ful, active, serious, concise, dircreet, sensitive, intvitivej> 
efficient, cautious, intellectual, perceptive, contemplative, 
domestic, prudent, provident, industrious, methodical, 
thrifty. 

Undesirable characteristics for correction are expressed 
an follows: calculating, mercenary, selfish, anxious, 
worried, irritable, apprehensive, discontented, secretive, 
critical, skeptical, cold, unresponsive/' inconsistent, inde- 
cisive, quick-tempered, timid, backward, lacking self- 
coQfidence.

CAPRICORN 

Capricorn is called cardinal, movaDie, tropical, solsti- 
tial, earthly, feminine, f cold, dry, nfelancnoly, southern, 
quadrupedal, serving, /legative, of short ascension. 

• The Sun appartntly transits Capricorn between De- 
cember 22nd and January 19th. It is ruled by Saturn, 
is th*e exaltationof Mars, the detriment of the tyloon and 
\he fall of Jupiter* Capricorn in modern astrology is 
symbolized by a goat, but in ancient uranographies it 
was portrayed as half goat with th£ tail of a fish. 

Jn early religious history the goat ^yas a sacrificial 
animal, and to this day popular sayings like "He was the 
goat/' and "the scape-goat," carry out the same idea of 
sacrifice. God commanded the children of Israel in 
§ Ileviticus 9, 3: "Take ye a kid of the goat for a sin offer- 
ing/' The goat was pictured as falling down, in the 
attitude of dying; one leg is doubled under the body, the 
head is droopiftg, but the body of the dying goat termin- 
ates in the body and tail of a vigorous fish. It is often 
-referred to as the fish-goat. It is interesting to recall 
that J;he word Jesus means fish. This goat-fish symbol 
is.associated with various biblical references: "Thus it 
is written, and thus it behoved Christ fo suffer, and to 
rise from tfce dead the third day, that repentance and 
remission of sifts might be preached in His name among 
all nations." — Luke 24, 46-47. It is typical of going down 
and coming up, — resurrection. 

Pagan mythology tetys the story that*Pan or Bacchus, 
a divine personage, was feasting with other gods on the 
bank qf a river, when the fearful Typhon came ‘upon 
them suddenly compelling fhem.to assume other shapes 
in order t<^ escape hi/n. Bacchus took the form of a goat 
and plunged into the stream, that part of his body which 
was under watsr assuming the form of a fish. To com* 
memorate the event before dll nations, Jupiter placed hin\ 
.in the heavens (zodiac) in his metamorphosed jrfiape: 

The Philistines connected Dagon, the half-fish-god, 
with Catfricornus, and embodied it with the original idea.

s AQUARIUS ~ 

Aquarius called fixed, airy, moist, "sanguine, mascu- 
line, human, scientific, electric, serving, eloquent, intui- 
tive, of short ascension. 

The Sun appears to transit through this sign between 
January 20th and February 18th. Aquarius is ruled by 
Uranus and is the detriment of the Sun. It is symbolized 
by the fnan emptying a waterpot. (It is noticeable that 
in some of the zodiacs he holds a rod, branch or wand 
in one uplifted hand.) In some of the older zodiacs 
Aquarius is pictured as ‘a woman. This is interesting in 
connection wifrh the prevailing thought that this is the 
“woman’s age” or era, associating the idea of the ascend- 
ancy of woman with the dawn of a new era. The solar 
system is now at about the tenth degree of Pisces, by 
precession of the equinoxes, is receding toward the first 
degree of Pisces and, consequently, to the thirtieth de- 
gree of Aquarius, which, at the rate of one degree in 
seventy-two years, it should actually contact in 10 x 72 
or 720 years. The interim is the “dawn” or the period 
of gestation, the birth taking place at about 720 4- 1928 
or the year 2648. If we allow the^ usual orb of influence 
(12 degrees for the Sun,) then the influence began* to 
operate about two degrees ago, i.'e., 2 x 72 or 144 years 
ago, — at about the time of our Declaration of Independ- 
ence (freedom of a nation,) an epoch making event. It 
is recalled that in America Edison developed the incan- 
descent flight and the practical use of electricity; Ford 
developed the automobile and tractor; the Wright 
brothers developed the airplane^ Forrest perfected the 
features which made possible a radio in every home. 
Each* of these iri novations rqkd by Uranus are heralds of 
the airy, Aquarian age which will havd^ a duration of 
about 2,160 years. 

1 Always associated with Aquarius is the urn, or the 
cupbearer. The Hebrew name for Aquariu& was Delphi, 
or “water urq, v signifying a pouring out or baptism, re-' 
lated to* atonement, the risen, Surf or Sph, tKfe living 
waiter, spirit. Ganymedes of the Greeks was carried on 
to Olympus by an eagle to be a cupbearer. Olympus is

% the heavens or fiky, where dwelt twelve of the higher 
gods. This means that he was to be the distributor of 
watfcrs between fyeaven and earth; hence the connection 
with the myth of Olympus in Thcssal/, Greece. In 
Biochemistry Aquarius is the distributor of water and 
gases throughout the micrpcosmic man. Aquarius is an 
ais-water sign, the* waters on the earth Jbeing lifted in 
vapor to the air. This work is the office of Aquarius, 
providing the liquid in air which we breathe and in which 
the lungs are bathed (baptised,) thereby proving our 
salvation for otherwise they would quickly dry and burn 
up.. 

Xhis is one of four fixed signs constituting the Cher-

UNIVERSE MAKkRjS , 

Copernicus (or Koppernigk), Nicolaus: bom on February 10th, 
1473, at Thorn (th&i in Poland. ) fJ Attended the university of 
Cracow, studying mathematical science and devoting spare time 
to painting. At the age of twenty-three he* proceeded*,; to Bologna, 
attending the lectures of Dominico Maria Novarra, professor of 
astronomy. He vlso spent some years at t adua where he applied 
himself to the study of medicine, taking the degree of doctor in 
1499. In the year 1500, at Rome, he enjoyed the friendship of 
Regiomontanus (Johann Mullet;), a great* astrologer who was edu- 
cated af Leipzig, studied mathematics at Vienna ahd accompa ed 
Cardinal Bessarlon to Rome, where Beza gave him further Instruc- 
tions in Greek literature, which enabled him to complete a, new 
abridgment in Latin of the “Almagest” of Ptolemy. Regiomon' 
tan us returned to Rome af the invitation of Sixtus IV, who em- 
ployed him in the reformation of the calendar * 

His wide researches gave Copernicus the material for compiling 
a logical treatise entitled, “De Orbium Cselestium Revolutionibus 
Libri VI.,” a work which occupied him between 1507 and 1530, 
and which It will be seen, embraces the main theory of Pythagor- 
ean astrologers, although all works of preceding masters contrib- 
uted valuable matter which he skillfully chose or discarded as 
needed to conform with his own ideas of a ^rational, workable 
system. 

^However, it was not until just before he died on May 24, 1543, 
(at the age of seventy) that he saw the fruits of his labors in 
published form. In those days it took brave men to promulgate 
a new idea. After finishing his treatise In 1530 he delayed Its 
publication nearly thirteen years beifg content to defer the pop- 
ular outcry which Inevitably would occur against him/ which, as 
sponsor of truths hitherto unknown to science^anif as an assailant 
of time-hbnored dogmatism, he must endure. He was not excom- 
municated on account of it ! In fact, his work became the basis 
of thfe present day system of astronomy, just as the Tetrabiblos of 
Ptolemy* became the basis of present day astrology. Occasionally 
we meet someone not very well informed on the* history and de- 
velopment of astrology and the chfcracte* of its sponsors, who 
claims with a loud voice thht the Copernican theory “exploded 
astrology,” which shows they cIq, not understand that the funda- 
mentals of astrology are based upon the infli^mce of the planets 
and are not dependent upon a theory of whether the planets go 
*around the Sun or the Sun around the planets. Chwging of man's 
theory does not change the established influence* of the planets, 

> and nativities are calculated in accordance with present-day 
astsodomical rules.* The so-calUd “explosion” obliterated the then 
prevailing theory of astronomy,— not astrology. 



PART V, * ADDITIONAL STUDIES 


063 


TIME FACTORS 

The following* explanations will give an idea of the 
time-mea|ure distinctions and define why one sort of 
time, ratier than Another, is used in astrology, in order 
that calculations n^y be utilized which synchronize with 
Nature's movements. 

t There are four # kiqds of time : astronomical, apparent, 
mean and sidereal, (Standard time is treated separately.) 
but theife are only two measures of^time and these are 
called the Mean Scale and the Sidereal Scale. 

Astronomical Time is measured from midnight to mid- 
night since January 1st, 1925. Prior to that the astro- 
nomical day began at noon. The astronomical day is 
reckoned as 24 hours continuously, therefore no A. M. or 
•P. M. is mentioned. For instance when a civil clock 
shows 11 p. m. the astronomical time is 23 o'clock. This 
time is measured on the mean time scale. However, it 
is not employed in the erection of horoscopes. 

Apparent Time or apparent Solar time: (The word 
apparerft is here undystood to mean clearly perceived 
or perceivable, evident, obvious, manifest.) At the middle 
of every day at every place on earth the Sun will reach 
its meridian.* The moment of this elimination is known 
as apparent noon. The interval of time between two 
successive apparent noons is an apparent Solar day. The 
Sun moves daily at a rate varying between 57, 10" to 
1° T 11". Although the apparent Solar day is a natural 
day, its varying dliraticn renders it unsuitable for civil 
purposes, hence it is not usedf for clock time. As ,it is 
aKvays longer than a sidereAl^day it is noi* used for erect- 
ing horoscopes. 1 

Mean* TirAe/ mean Solar time, civil tijne, true local 
time: Because, as seen by the foregoipg paragraph, an* 
apparent Solar day is not of uniform duration, the idea i 
has been conceived of using an imaginary mean Sun 
which ia'assmned to move at the uniform rate«of 59' 8", 
this being the average motion of the apparent Sun. Whin 
this mean Sun reaches the meridian of any place it is 



064 


GENETHLIACAE ASTROLOGY 


mean noon. The interval between any two successive 
mean noons is always the same and is known a£ a mean 
Solar ,day. In other wprds, it is simply acr average 
apparent day, but 'because of its unifo/irfity is suitable 
for civil time measurement. It w nothing but clock time, 
that is, Mean local clock time. It has,become Renown by 
various names: A mean Solar day, mean time, fc.ivil time, 
mean local time. Its duration is uniformly 23 hrs. 
mins. 60 sec. or 24 hours, and constitutes the civil day 
beginning at midnight. The 12 hours before noon are 
called A: M. (antemeridian), and the 12 hours after meaii 
noon are P. M. or postmeridian. (Note: Wherp Stand- 
ard time is used, as#n the U. S., it is necessary to reduce 
the Standard time to terms of the Mean local time for 
place of birth in horoscope work.) As the mean Solar 
day is always linger than a sidereal day by approximate- 
ly 3 minutes 55.91 seconds, the Mean local time of birth 
is always changed to terms of Sidereal time for erecting 
horoscopes. 

Sidereal Time 

Sidereal Time: Expressed in hours, minutes and 
seconds at any moment is the angular distance of the 
first point of Aries from .the meridian of any place. Right 
Ascension of the Meridian (R. A.,M.) is practically the 
same thing but is expressed in degrees and minutes, 
instead of hours, minutes and seconds. Sidereal time is 
that shown by an , astronomical clock ^yhich indicates 
twenty-four hours in 23 hours 56 minutes 4.090 seconds 
of Mean Solar time which is civil or ordinary clock time, 
reganfless of Standard time. Sidereal time therefore 
increases over Mean time at the rate of approximately 
3' 55.#l" seconds per day and is ciuntetf straight through 
from^O hr. 0 min. to 24 hrk. 0 min. without considering 
A. or P. M.'as with civil 'clocks. Th^ Sidereal time 
at noon for any place is shown daily in the ephemeris. 
From the fact that Sidereal time is the measurement of 
the angular distance of Aries 0° from the meridian of 
any pl^pe, it is readily seen that Sidereal time is in reality 
astrological time; hence the 'mean, mean Solar, civil or 
ordinary clock time of birth is always converted into 
terms of Sidereal time for horoscopal purposes.

Correction to Sidereal Time 
• 

Beoaifse, before stated, 'Sidereal time advances or 
increases at the rate of approximately four minutes per 
day, (Refer to Sidereal time in an’ ephemeris and note 
how it advances from noon on one day after another until 
in 365 mys it has gone through the 24 hours and begins 
at 0 hours again "on or about March 24st each year.) a 
correction to it should be made at the rate of 10 seconds 
Jto be added for'evyy hour that birth occurs after noon; 
"subtracted from it if born before noon. If a “Midnight” 
type of Ephemeris is used the correction is for the 
amount of time birth occurred after midnight. 

Correction to Sidereal Time for Longitude 

Another factor necessary to consider in an effort 
towards accuracy is a correction to S. T. for the long- 
itude of any place in question. 

Change thejongitude into time by multiplying it by 
4 minutes, and divide the product by 60 minutes (to get 
hours and minutes) or 60 seconds if less than 15 degrees 
from Greenwich, and get minutes and seconds: the an- 
swer is the time distance from Greenwich. For every 
hour of this time allow 10 seconds, and for parts of an 
hour in proportion. 

See “Table of Correction to Sidereal Time/' page 64. 

% 

If the longitude of the place «is aast, (of Greenwich) 
subtract 10«seconds for every hour east, from the S. T. 
at noon. 

* 

If the place west longitude, add 10 seconds for every 
hour west, to the S. „T. 

.This correction applies in the same Vfray to SidereaJ 
Time in a “tytidnight” ephemeris. 

A special polumn ^s devoted to Sidereal" time *at noon 
daily in all ephemerides (pronounced ef' i mer' i doez) 
except the midnight ephemeris, in hours and minutes of

the Mean time scale, fin some of tl*e very old ephem- 
erides the Sidereal time was given in a£>parefit time, 
consequently an “equation of time” was then necessary.) 

The foregoing explanatory remarks Are give^ for the 
benefit of those students who c are desirous of knowing 
the different kinjls of time in use, and which kinds are 
used in astrology. Mean time and Sidereal time are the 
principal factors. However, if at this moment the 'stu- 
dent feel?* he does not entirely comprehend these various* 
times he need feel no concern about the matter* as the 
remarks are explanatory only. The instructions for us- 
ing Sidereal time and Mpan time (local clqck time) are 
simple and are appropriately applied in various previous 
chapters and examples. 

Standard Time 

However there is a factor which must be considered 
for all horoscope work and that is the difference between 
Standard time and Mean local time. In £bme cases the 
difference between these two amounts to half an hour. 
Unless Standard time is converted into Mean local time, 
(the time used astrologically) errors will occur 4a the 
signs and degrees occupying the twfelve houses in a horp- 
scope. As you have already learned that a degree, of the_ 
zodiac crosses the midheaven every four minutes, in the 
half hour mentioned dbove the discrepanc/ would amount 
to seven and one-half degrees error which might cause 
the wrtmg sign to be placed on the ascendant and make 
the chart misleading* I\ would be well for ±hc student 
to sperid all the time necessary tO|undej-stana the princi- 
ples of the use of Standard time and how to convert it 
into Mean local time for astrological uses. Standaitf time 
is the common clock time in 'use in the U.*S. Except in 
thpse places which are right on the center of ^ standard 
time zone, where clock time and Mean locUl time* corre- 
spond, all other* places will need a correction from their 
cjock'titne to Mean local timd. That is, whfcn a birth is 
sVated iq clock time that clock time must be .ignored and 
the equivalent* Mean local time used in the 'calculations 
for erecting the horoscope. 



PART ADDITIO NAL 8TODIB8 067 

Summary 

For astrological purposes the entire matter concerning 
the kinds and $he»measures of time may 1 be summarized 
as follows : 

Stanlard Tim* as usld in the United States, Canada 
*and Mexico, is the term employed to designate that all 
tha clocks in e^ch time zone register the same time, re- 
•gardless of whether a place is east or west of Jhe center 
of a zone. Standard time is therefore an artificial method 
of time and for horoscopal work th« standard time should 
always be changed to Mean time, sometimes called local 
mean time, ’mean Solar time* civil time, or true local 
time; all of which titles are intended ‘to designate the 
real time of a place as distinguished from its standard or 
artificial clock time.* The birth time given for horoscope 
# work should always be reduced to this mean time which 
is then changed into Sidereal Time to determine the S. T. 
at birth. See page 67 for rule converting Standard time 
into mean tirpe ; pages 63, 64 for making corrections to 
Sidereal time. 



GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


SIDEREAL TIME 

ancl the 

Midnight Ephenmris 

A civil day is measured frora midnight to n£xt mid- 
night by two revolutions on our comtnon clocks of 12 
hours. 

An astronomical day was formerly measured from 
noon to noon by an astronomical clock having 24 hours 
marked on its dial, to register one complete revolution 
of the^ Earth on its axis. 

This Sidereal Time clock was set at the moment of 
Vernal Equinox, that is, when the Sun reaches the zodia- 
cal sign Aries, 0° 00' 00", the hands of the clock are set 
at OH 0M OS noon. When the sun arrives at mid- 
heaven next day, due to Earthy revolution, the hands 
of the clock have traversed over the entire 24 hours, but 
as the Sun has traveled one degree, apparently, in its 
orbit, the Earth must turn one degree more before Sun 
is actually on midheaven again. This turn requires ap- 
proximately 4 minutes, consequently the clock shows 0 H 
04 M, or 4 minutes more than 24 , hours for the Sun's 
culmination. On. the following day f it registers 0 H, 8 M 
at Sun's culmination, and so on each day the clock reg- 
isters approximately 4 minutes later until f.he “next entry 
of Sun into 1 Aries one year later. 

Thus^ at this rate each month during the year the 
clock's hands register v 2 liours more past 0*.0CV':00" n6on, 
when the Sun culminates at civil iioon. ^Referring to an 
Ephemeris, which gives S. T. for every day in the year, 
we finil, for instance, that on«September 1st, 1910, at' 
civil noon anywhere, S. T.., as shown by the*'astronomical

How Tq Use Midnight Sidereal Time 

To esect a chart by an Ephemeris w^ich quotes S. T 
and planets’ places at midnight: 

Simply change tile midnight S. T. to noon by adding 
12 H, plus 2 M (or 120 S) correction for the 12 hours 
elapsed, and append the correctidti for distance from 
Greenwich and the correction *f or amount of time^born 
before or after noon. When thus provided with the 
cdrrected S. T. at noon add or subtract the amount of 
time born after or before noon and you then have the 
S. T. at birth with Vhich to enter a T. of H. for the 
appropriate latitude to insert the house cusps as usual. 

How to Correct Planets’ Places In a Midnight Ephemeris 

Simply change the midnight recorded places of the 
» planets to noon* and then correct the places to Wrth 
timejn the usual way for the amount of time born be- 
fore or after noon nftrk. 

* Note the, recorded places at midnight (OH 00 M 00 S 
a. m.) of the da£e desired, and their places next- midnight. 
One half their distance between is their place at noon. 
Most of this is easily done by mental equation. Some of 
the “anidnigjit” Ephemerides als<5 qubte the Modn’s posi- 
tion for noon. 

• ^ 

Sinci the Nautical Almanac and Ephemeris, issued by 
the Government of several countries, changed their astro- 
nomic^ fig&res to read for midnight instead of noon, the 
makers of astrological Ephemerides find it easier to quot£ 
for midnight because nearly all their calculations are 1 
‘transpositions from, the governmental observatory rec- 
ords quoted In such works as the “American Nautical 
Almanac?”

GEN3THLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


Although the midnight astrological Ephemeris is 'easier 
for the compilers, it complicates matters for students of 
horoscopy. For instance, for births in places west of 
Greenwich the time equivalent to midnight is in the day 
previous, because Greenwich time is in advance places 
in west longitude. 

To illustrate: A birth in Los Angeles on January 1st, 
1935, (or any year since the beginning o* 1925) at 9.30 
p. m. 

Los Angeles being A 18* west longitude, or 7 H 52 M 
earlier, than Greenwich time, the time equivalent to 
Greenwich midnight (OH 00 M 00 S on January 1st,, 
1935) is 4:08 p. m. on December 31st, 1934. The differ- 
ence between 4:08 p. m. on one day and 9:30 p. m. on 
next day being 29 H 22 M makes it oLvious that although 
birth occurred on January 1st it is necessary to use the 
Ephemeris for January 2nd. 

Therefore, we discard the foregoing and p-oceed to use 
the planets' places as recorded in the ephemeris for, Jan. 
2nd. «The time equivalent being 4:08 p. m. on January 
1st and birth at 9:30 p. rfi., Jan. 1st, it is evident, that 
birth occurred 5 H 22 M after the time for which the 
planets are recorded in Ephemerjs on January 2nd, 
which it should be understood is midnight of January 
1st or 0.00:00 a. m. of January 2nd. Th^ planets are 
therefore corrected for their motions in 5 H 22 M be- 
yond thpir places shown as of January 2nd. 
c " 

When using a midnight ephemeris remember that time equiv- 
alent is really the midnight mark for an> place" other than Green- 
wich. The midnight mark for Loe Angeles is approximately 4 :08 
p. m. It*is the time e quivalent to the-next midnight for all hirths 
which occur after 4 :08 p. m. ; or for the previouu midnight for 
births which occur before 1:08 p. m. in Lc3 Angeles. On pages 
78-7S Is a table of NOON marks; by adding 12 hou r a to ir them 
you acquire the midnight mark. 

JHowfcver, students working with the ‘‘midnight” 
Ephemeris for jvrest longitude charts can eliminate the 
complications by simply changing trie S. T. ana the 
planets’ places to noon, as shown in the rule given on 
°page 669.

tarius, judging by the topography, as it rises steadily ii^o high 
hills, where the majority of the wealthiest people hgve located 
and built fine residences. (Jupiter, toiler* of SagittariiA. )

HOROSCOPES 

In ccjhnection with* tl^e aspects that may be formed 
£mong planets in*a Progressed Chart, qr aspects of pro- 
gressed planets to radix planets, it may be of interest to 
students to have a simple rule illustrating the method 
t>f calculating the* date and time when the influence 
reaches* its maximum or peak, or in other words, the 
d^te and* time an aspect becomes p^rtile. 

TIME KEY or MEASURE OP TIME 

24 hours = 1 d&y, or 1 year by progression 
2 hours motion = 1 month (30 days) 

1 hour motion = month (15 days) 

4 minutes motion = one day (24 hours) 

1 minute motion = 6 hours (J4 day) 

10 seconds motion = 1 hour of time 
1 second motion = 6 miiyites of time. 

Us£ ’the above table to determine date and time for 
sueh calculations as are shown in following examples. 

Note : The resultant time of day derived through use 
of the following methods must be approximate, and not 
exact, due to various technical factors involving flight 
irregularity in motion of the planets^ etc., which*for the 
sake ef simplicity are omitted. However, the variation 
is slight and so will rarely cause your answer to vary 
more than a few minutes in time and therefore for prac- 
tical purposes may be considered correct. •

THE' pre-natal epoch 

• • 

• • 

A Review of Its Development 

* 

The If w pertaining # to the Pre-Natal Epoch is based 
upon tbi: observed relationship between the Moon and 
the asctjridant of ff horoscope. 

Since Ptolemy in the “Tetrabiblos" propounded the 
Ancient doctrine which has become known as “The Ani- 
modar pf the Tetrabiblos," the subject has been dealt 
with by such eminent authors as, Obley, Gadbury, Wil- 
son and L. H. Weston. Heyflen treated the doctrine 
upjdei* the title, “The Truitine of Hermyes." Sepharial 
anti E. H. Bailey have modernized the rules for present- 
day application. Originally, its purpose was its use as 
a means of correcting given birth times; modern expon- 
ehts apply it for that purpose and also for study of the 
9 pre-natal chart, even going so far as to “progress" it. 
The entire subject is rich in interest and possibilities for 
research and <for that reason let us start back with 
Ptolemy and review the subject to its present day de- 
velopment, as regards its value as a birth time corrector. 
It is especially valuable in that respect for correcting the 
horoscope of infants Who are as yet too young to have 
ha*d any~-st*iking evenly by which to rectify the time as 
shown *by the chapter on “Rectification By Arcs of 
Events," in Pari IV. 

In the following excerpts from “Ptolemy's Tetrabiblos" 
it wilj be noticed that he makes «it a point to stress the 
importance <!f the value of the natal chart in these words 
particularly, “Ancf, although the birth snould in strict- 
ness be called the secondary beginning, # while the •con- 
ception -might fce insisted oij as the primary beginning, 
it is still found to be equal to* the conception in its 
efficacy t ami njuch more complete." 

Ptolemy jaysf 

“The actual moment in which human gei\eratioji com- 
mences is, in tfact, by*nature.the moment of th# concep- 
tion itself; but, in efficacy with regard to subsequent 
events, it the parturition or birth.

“But, if the time of conception <;annpt« be precisely 
made out, that of tl\p birth must be received at orig- 
inal date of generation ; for it is virtually^the most impor- 
tant and is in no r£sprct deficient 6a comparison with 
the primary origin by conception, except in one view 
only; viz, that the origin by conception affords the infer- 
ence of occurrences which take effect* previous!!' to tl\e 
birth, whereas the origin by birth can of course, be 
available only for Mich as arise subsequently. And, 
although Ihe birth should in strictness be called thU 
secondary beginning, while the conception plight be 
insisted on as the piimaiy beginning, it is stilf found, to 
be e(|ual to the conception in its efficacy, and much more 
complete, although later in time. For the conception 
may. in fart, be said to be the generation ->f mere hunian 
seed, but the birth that of man himself; since t,iu infant 
at its birth acquires numerous qualities which it would 
not possess white in rhe womb, and which are proper 
to human nature alone; such, for instance, as the partic- i * * 4 
uiar action of the senses and the movement of the body 
and limbs. Besides, even u the position <«f the Ambient, 
actually existing at the birth, cannot be considered to 
assist in forming and ejigendeiing the particular shape 
and qualities of the infant, it is nevertheless still auxiliary 
to the infant's entrance into the \Vorld : because nature, 
after completing t lie formation ,in the womb, always 
effects the birth in immediate obedience to some certain 
position of the Ambient, corresponding arid sympathising 
with the primary position which operated the incipient 
formation. It is therefore perfectly admissible, and con- 
sistent ‘with reason*/ tlfat the configuration of the Q stars, 
as it r cxists at *the time of birth, altly>ugh # it cannot be 
said, to possess any share of the creative cause, should 
still be consider' act in signification, as fully as the 
configuration at iL time al conception j because it has, 
of necessity, a pow .orresponding to that configuration 
whic; actually possessed the creative cause/

“It must, however, be observed that if the actual dis- 
tance of the degree, iq which the ruling planet may be 
posited, from the ordinary degree ascending, be found to 
liXceed its distance from the ordinary degree of the mid- 
heaven, the numerical denomination, found in the way 
above mentioned, is then to be considered as applicable 
to the actual degree in culmination, and the other angles 
are to be arranged in conformity therewith. (The pre- 
cepts delivered in this chapter have obtained the name of 
Ptolemy’s Animodar. § The term is probably Arabic, if 
it is not a corruption of the Latin wor^s animum, or 
animam,*dare, ‘giving animation or life;* yet this mean- 
ing seems scarcely close enough.)**

yhe Truitin% # of Hermes 

“The JLng^sh astrologer Heydon, who flourished in* 
Eoqdon during the 18th century, t is knswn to have 
taught a sy^tjrrf of rectifying the estimated time* qf a 
birth- by a prenatal epoch plan which he called The 
Truitine pf Hermes. .Whether or not Heydon's $lan is 
the real Truitine of Hermts Trismagestis, the \nythicnl 
Greek philosopher, cannot at this time be determined,

but it is quite certain, nevertheless, that of the 

modern prenatal method? for correction p? the; birth are 
based on Hey don rules, and so far as *we can gather 
that teacher really /lid secure them from ancient sources. 

“A full history of this prenatal rectification system of 
Hevdon has never been written and its real origin is lost 
in the night of ancient times. Comparalivelyli recentf-y, 
however, a wntei under the pseudonym of Sep,harial 
brought forward this very ancient r plan under a slightly 
change*! form. 

"In 1891 an English magazine printed some of, the 
rules of this system, an^i previous to that time they had 
been printed more or less completely m other publica- 
tions. Again in 1907 The Astrologer’s Magazine printed 
these same old rides, and thus they have been reprinted 
in various publications from tiineTo time. 

"The Truitine, as we thus have it, is based upon tlie 
proposition that the true hour of birth is already cer- 
tainly known to very nearly the exact nvnute, for in any 
case where the hour cannot at all be estimated to almost 
exactly the truth this Truitine plan is manifestly useless. 
Indeed, the very first article of the rule is that an estimate 
figure must be erected and used as a basis of the Calcula- 
tion, hence whenever this estimate is impossible ’the 
whole plan is also impossible. Tf in the estimated figure 
you change the values a few degrees ,it vC’ill produce a 
change in your results, hence we see that it is the esti- 
mate which actually decides the result, and the rectifica- 
tion's thus manifestly a mere result of the estimate and 
not<of anything else whatsoever. 

"But since the Truitine really preserves a concealed 
truth, and alscr because it has long been the preijatal cor- 
rection method, I here give the ancient fules:

Mr. Weston sums it up thus: 

Retriew qf These Ancienty Methods* 

"Here, then, we have the famous Antmodar of Ptolemy 
and the celebrated Truitine oi Hermes both dhpendiijg 
entirely upon an estimated map of the horoscope and an 
estimated degree that is presumed to h/ive possibly been 
on the ascendant at a birth-time which is in question, 
using what is almost certainly, an erroneous map and a 
false ascendant. They both propose to rectify an esti- 
mated ascendant to the true degree by shifting its posi- 
tion from an inexact aspect to one that is exact, for if 
the estimate hkd accidentally fallen at the exact aspect 
no shift and no correction would be necessary. They 
make an estimate ascendant to sh'ft to an exact aspect 
with some Promittor which they elect by a set of rules 
presumed to be competent to give such election. 

"The Truitine takes the Moon's longitnde at the mo- 
ment it is on the rising horizon first previous to a pre- 
natal epoch, and elects that degree of longitude as the 
Promittor to which the 'ascendant must be shifted. 

"The Animodar, by means of certain rules, elects the 
longitude of some planet as a Promittor to which the 
ascendant must be aspected in terms of duodecimal seg- 
ments, 

( 

"The,6tie supreme principle which renders the, Ani- 
modav at all possible is the shift to an authorized aspect 
(the authorized' aspects are the duodecimal divisions of 
a circle, being 30, 60, 90, 120, 150 and 180 degrees) and 
if there is any truth at all ip ‘this famou^ plan it surely 
must be the truth that the duodecimal divisions of a 
circle of Life Rays are eternal points of specific , energy 
or efficacy. 

<- 1 < % - 

' "If any truth at all is in the Truitine it is that a pre- 
natal epoch is* Useful for finding theorising degree at any * 
bir$ and that it is the Moon which brings on the effect. 
Upon these ancient authorities and widi these broad 
hints, let us take one step forward towarcf the light."

OF* THE PRE-NATAL* EPOCH 

Elements For Rectifying 

A ;Oiven Birth Tim# By The Pre-natal Epoch 
i 

Make a horoscope for the given birth time and note 
which of the following elements apply : 

Element No. 1 : 

A. .When the Moon at birth *is increasing in light and 
above the horizon the period is less Ithan ten lunar 
months. 

. B. When the Moon at birth is decreasing in light and 
, below the horizon the period is less than ten lunar 
months. 

C. When the moon at birth is increasing in light and 
below the horizon the period is more than ten lunar 
.months. 

D . • When the Mooij at birth is decreasing in light and 
above the horizon the period is more^ than ten lunar 
months* 

Element No. *2: 

* 

A. When the Moon is increasing at birth, it. will be 
found on tl»e epoch day in the sign which is riqfng at 
birth, i. e., the eastern horizoij or cusp off the first house. 

• i 

• B. When the Moon is decreasing at birth, it will be 
found on the tpoch day in*the sign which is setting at 
birth. 

Clement No., 3 : 

♦ • • 

, That day approximately ten lunar months previous to 
birthday on .which Ihe Moon transits the* ex^tt degree 
of 'the ascendant at birth' (or its opposite), is the da^ of 
the Pre-Natal Epoch. 



688 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


A. If the Moon is increasing at birth, its piacv birth 
will be rising at the time pi the epoch on epoch day. 

B. If the Moon is decreasing aj: birth, its place at birth 
will be setting w at the time of epo*ch on epoch d^y. 

To Find The Day Of Epoch’ 

Count ten lunar months or 273 days backward from 
the date of birth and note the date on 'which the Moon 
is in tho' sign it held at birth, or the sign opposite, as 
may be required by the foregoing elements. (If the birth 
took place earlier than September, it will be necessary 
to procure an ephemeris,-for the year preceding that of 
birth.) Having found the lunar place, count forw&rd-or 
backward, as the case may require, until you come to the 
day when the Moon is in the sign ascending at birth, or 
descending at birth, according to the rules. Take that 
day on which it transits the exact degree on the horizon 
at birth. Call this the Epoch Day. 

To Find The Time Of Eponh 

1. Ascertain where the mother of the native was re- 
siding on the day of epoch, and refer to the Tables of 
Houses for the latitude of that pl?ce. 

2. In that Table of Houses bring the Moon's longi- 
tude at birth to the ascendant or descendant, as the case 
requires, 2nd note the Sidereal Time in^hours, minutes 
and seconds. This will be the Sidereal Time at Epoch. 

3. From the ephemens take the Sidereal Time at'noon 
on the day of epoch. The difference between the Sider- 
eal Time at noon, and the S. T. at the epoch will give the 
correct time before or after moon at which the epoch 
occurred; therefore subtract the S. T. at noon from the 
& T. of the epoch. If the answer is less thap 12 hours 
it is in P. M.; if the answer is more than'12 1 hours sub- 
tract 1 £ from it apd the answer is A. M time of epoch. 
/Note : A chart made for the time thus found is the Pre- 
Natal hproscQpe.) 

4? Find the Moon's longitude for this* derived time on 
the day of epoch, and it will represent the degfree to be

on the~ascen4a$t or descendant at birth, as the case may 
require. • 

To* Fin<^ The True Time Of Bjrth 

When the ascepding degree has been found, find that 
• degree ascending in a T^ble of Houses for the latitude 

? ^f the place of birth. Note the S. T. given # for that degree ; 
rom it subtract the S. T. at noon on the birthday. The 
result will be thfj time of birth. Should these figures be 
tess than 12.00:00 it*is P. M. time of birth; if m»re than 
12.00 :00j subtract 12 hours from it and the remainder is 
the time of birth in A. M. 

• 

The foregoing is a brief review of the main rules for 
finding the Epoch Day, and the corrected time of birth, 
as given by Sepharial in “The Manual of Astrology.” 
The text* goes on giving sex degrees, etc. E. H. Bailey 
has published an excellent volume treating the entire 
•subject entitled, “The Pre-Natal Epoch.” It presents 
the philosophy, theory and practice, the complete modern 
doctrine in interesting form, including examples of var- 
ious variations from normal to “irregular” epochs. 

My own application of the preceding rules in many 
yearsf of practice has bfen quite satisfactory. In addition 
to .using them for the correction of birth time for infants, 

I often used them to check the time derived by the “Arc 
of Events” method, and, when they # differed slightly, I 
preferred the tiAie derived by the A. of E. metfiod. The 
following examples from my note-book should ajd in 
illustrating the method of using tlje pre-natal epoch rules. 

An Example illustrating the Manner ot Jtsirtn lime 
Rectification by the Pre-Natal Epoch Method *

times, but tp rectify Sirth times that have been recorded 
with sotne degree of. certainty so as to b§ able to erect 
a reliable chart, ^instead of qtherwise using one which is • 
only approximately correct. 

This i« a method ot rectification resorted to ^specially 
in fhe case of* children, when as yet no events have Oc- 
curred in their lives by which their true birth time could
