, 704 GfcNETBnyfiCAL ASTKOLOGT 

RESEARCH METHODS 
FOR ASTROLOGICAL STUDENTS 


An Introduction to the Principles of ^Statistical 
Analysis as- Applied to Astrological Research 


Studehts of astrology are increasingly aware *f the 
need and value of tjje statistical method as an organized 
treatment of whatever branch of our science they are 
studying. They realize that the future of astrology rests 
in our ability to demonstrate its validity with data which 
will withstand scientific tests of significance. More and 
more, astrologers are coming to, recognize that their 
science will be awarded the approval of universitatus 
just as soon as their claims are even partly proven cor- 
rect by methods acceptable to scientists. It will teach 
us much to review in our minds a brief history of mod- 
ern psychical research. 

The past few decades .have seen a complete turnabout- 
face on the question of psychical phenomena. But i f was 
not the many independent test-Condition experiments, 
the vast well-aitthenticated literature on the subject, nor 
the roll-call of famous personages and intellectuals con- 
vinced of its reality, which wrought this academic revol- 
ution. The respect of the erstwhile materialistic school 
of thought was given only after many years of patient 
and exhaustive restarch into extra-sensorv perception 
proved the reality of transcendentals The results of 
this research were reported to the world in terms of 
statistics. Rigid statistics tested for significance and 
proved significant are the prroving-ground for scientific 
acceptance 

* The subject of astrology is now aoout to undergo this 
( sam£ sort of status reversal. The name of astrology will 
stand for all that is admirable and worthy of acceptance 
in Amefiqan education. The great popularity of the sub- 
je<ft, the roll-call of intellectuals interested in it, and the 
practicable worth of applied horoscopy to individuals, 


706 


PART V,i ADDITIOl^AL STUDIES 

however? wfll not taring about thi recognition and atten- 
tion which a£t/dlogy deserves, however. Only systematic 
research *by established, rigid statistical methods will 
win for qpr cosftiid science the statjjpn in » education we" 
seek for it. 

Many statisfical^technicjues are elaborate and rigorous 
to applj. This fact has discouraged :nany ambitious 
students of astrology who are willing to engage in order- 
ly research. Arfoth^r deterrent to more wide-spread 
enthusiasm has been the total lack of any available, sim- 
ple set instructions describing hqw the less difficult 
methods of analysis may be adapted and applies) to 
astrolpgy in particular. AstrolvJgical research is subject 
to;numerous peculiarities of approach not shared by 
research in the other social sciences. Elementary text- 
books on statistical analysis have confined their instruc- 
tions and examples to social problems, economics and 
.biometry. And advanced textbooks are usually unintell- 
igible to the individual without a prerequisite mastery of 
higher algebra and the calculus. 

Astrological students are constantly aware of the po- 
tential contribution to scientific, progress that lies un- 
organized in their files and notebooks for the reasons 
staged. Even the beginner often possesses a wealth of 
raw, information (birth and inception data^ historical and 
cas£ notes, etc.) that he has compiled in the passing of 
time. If collated and treated, this refined information 
may be of great value to astrologers and astrology as 
well as the ijvorld as a whole. 

^ests of Significance 

j * 

• It is our aim in this article to introduce to students 
tlfe general nature of statistical analysis 2nd to present 
the more common and doubtfess most valuable methods 
for determining whether the data one has collected is 
significant from a scientific viewpoint. Any analytic 
method in research is ordinarily called <a test of signifi- 
cance. A test Of significance may be defined as a deter- 
mination whether or gi<Jt a measure of unusualress is 
greater than ckn Reasonably be attributed to* chance 
alone. In standard laboratory practice it is assumed that 



706 


QElJETHL^lCAL ASTROLOGY 


if the characteristic in Question actually is found t n occur 
a certain number of times more than its probability of 
occurrence, it has ap corresponding degree of unusualness 
*and is therefore significant. 

In statistical terminology, the probability of its occur- 
rence is known as its theoretical frequency or its expec- 
tation. The actual number of its occuirences is called its 
observed frequency or its incidence. 

For example, in tossing a coin forty* times, the theor- 
etical frequency of its turning up heads is 50% of the 
time, or twenty times. But suppose that actu ^observa- 
tion, by counting the number of heads and tails in the 
series of forty tosses, Shows that heads appeared 23 
times and tails only 17 times. The actual, observed fre- 
quency, then, differed from what we might expect. It 
then behooves the coin-tosser to subject this situation to 
a mathematical analysis, to learn whether 23 heads could 
reasonably turn up by chance alone. The steps to take 
are very simple. In our illustration here, it is found that 
there was nothing unusual about the higher percentage 
of heads, because, according to probability theory, it is 
nqt unusual when between 14 and 26 items occur out of 
40 if their probability *of occurrence is 50%. We will 
learn shortly how to reckon these margins of allowance 
for chance-occgrrence. 

How might thi$ scientific theory ot probabilities be 
applied fo astrological research? Let lis take sign-posi- 
tions of the Moon as an example. The probability of 
any.eyent or individual's birth with the Moon in a given 
sign is l/12th of the time, or 8.333%<of the time. For 
mathematical Yeasons we state that < the probability is 
.08333, or, writing it, we # say, r = .08333. Suppose wc 
collected one ‘'hundred birthcharts of persons t sufferifig 
from a " specific disease or possessed of^Si common trait. 
•And that twenty, rather than eight, of , these persons 
were born with the Moon in one sign, while only two of 
the hundred haw their nata^l Moons in t.notl^er sign/We 
refer to the tally of 100 Lunar positions as a frequency 
distribution, ‘ < 

c e, o 

• It is apparent, then, that the observed frequency for 
one sign is 20% while we should expect only.8.333% of 



partV, additional studies T«r 

the fliArts to show the Moon so situated. And that only 
2% # o£ # the .charts' show the Moon in the other sign, 
whereas, if th^r^was nothing to tfie astrological theory, 
we should anticipate the birthdates in Question to have 
the Moon fairly evenly distributed among the zodiacal 
sectors. ItJbrfiooverf ug, however, to test the signifiance 
of the observed «frequencies before we can be justified in 
assuming that the uneven distribution found did not oc- 
cur by cnance. # 

In making this test , 9 it is important that we proceed 
on thcMiypothesis that the observed distribution of the 
Moon among the signs actually did take place by chance 
alone and that there are no # grounds for a belief in an 
Astrological explanation of the deviations. In other 
words, we must try to disprove astrology, rather than 
nurture any bias in# favor of an astrological tenet. Every 
test of significance is made in an effort to prove that 
chance and nothing else accounts for the discrepancies. 
In standard statistical practice, we call this negative 
approach a iyill hypothesis. Then, if the data under con- 
sideration passes the test of significance, we will be forced 
to reject the null hypothesis and concede that there ac- 
tually is an influence or ccfnditioning factor in the 
position whith accounts for the variations noted. 

Statistical Indilction: The Sampling Theory 

Let us now learn the rudiments of the metfiod by first 
considering the scientific theory underlying probability, 
and give the reader simple directions how to*ipake the 
test* of significance in sign-positions of faster-ipoving 
celestial bodies, fmd o^her important astrological factors. 
The basic? principle involved is universally known p as the 
’samplijig theory. A simple comprehension of the sam- 
pling theory ft essential fdr proficiency in any handling 
of data by statistical techniques. In order to give the 
reade^ an appreciation and adequate description of the 
sampling t theory, let us. dramatize* its purpose ana 
practice. # ® ® 

It' wpuld surely an impossible task to aoihpile 9 the 
birthdata of. every victim of a given disease. The mass 
of data Would iJe too great to handle in its entirety even 



708 


QENttfrHLIA^JkL ASTROIfOGY 


if it were possible to obtain. Should an entbmotegist 
wish to accurately determinp the wing-spafi t df th^ adult 
gall wasp, it would be foolish for him to even ittdmpr 
the capture and6iomqtry of every .adult gill wasp in the 
world. The average of his measurements of the wing 
spreads of several thousand specimens 4 ,* • g^aned from 
their habitats everywhere, however, would be nearly or 
exactly the same* as the average spread of every gall 
wasp in th world. In preparing his scientific report, .he 
could confidently state ^the wing-span of mature gall 
wasps. Or should a botanist wish to mention in his 
textbook the structural characteristics of a particular 
specie? of swamp fern, he would undertake the careful 
study of a large number 'of representative 1 specimens. 
Averaging the measurements of their fronds, the size ofc 
stomata and other constitutional features, he could 
authoritatively describe the characteristics df that 
species of fern. In other words, definite information 
about an entire species can be arrived at through statis- 
tical analysis of a sample number of representative 
items. 

The entire mass of data relating to one characteristic 
is called a universe, inasmuch as it represents the total 
population of the data concerned. The attribute of £he 
population which is being estimated from the study i<> 
termed a parameter. The items selected from that pop- 
ulation as representative of its whol : are inferred to 
collectively as the sample. Finally, the results from study 
of the sample are expressed mathematically and are 
called statistics. It is important that the student learn 
the meaning of these* terms and to use them^in practice 
if he is intent upon appreciating and becoming deft in 
statistical procedures. 

The term ‘‘population" has^a special and frequent use' 
in statistical analysis. We speak of a* “population" when- 
ever we are referring to an aggregate. T.hreraggjregate 
ijnay be of persons, things, individual measures, or sim- 
ply any class of objects or subjects which ljav*e in com- 
mon 'certain characteristics or causes. The word may 
be used ivhere we mean the parent population of ^hioh a 
given sample is held to be representative.* Or It may 
designate a purely hypothetical population ' from which 



PART V, ADDITIONAL STUDIES 700 

',~i 

the sample may ojr may not have been taken. Hence, in 
statistical literature, we read of \ population «of pump- 
kins, a population of deaf mutes, a population of ccanr 
modity^prices, ad infinitum. 

We understand noVjthat a sample of the total popula- 
tion of anything;, a cross-section of its universe, enables 
us to*genejalize concerning the universe from which the 
simple is drawn. The sampling process is itself a 
special problem, as it becomes the duty of the investi- 
gator to see to it that the variates (individual cases) 
composing his cross-section are selected at random and 
are therefore truly representative of the whole* The 
.process of ‘random sampling in some pases has become 
•itself an art within science. 

In a'strological research, however, we are seldom con- 
fronted with this problem of random sampling, by virtue 
of the fact that the human race is so immense numerical- 
ly and births are nearly continuous (one baby born every 
thirteen seconds in the United States alone), that any 
ordinary compilation of birthcharts will meet the re- 
quirements of true randomness of selection. Neverthe- 
less, even in a sample array eff birthcharts, break-dowm 
arts often necessary* A break-down is a further arrange- 
ment of the items in the array by natural classifications 
inherent in the sample itself. For instance, in any fine 
analysis, the charts of males and females should be dealt 
with separately at first, then combined in the final inter- 
pretation. Age, occupation, social class, educational 
level, marital status, nationality, pnd even religion, are 
customary brehk-downs. Why such stratifications are 
desirable and dften qecessa/y in an aStrological project 
should b*e obvious even to the layman. 

In "order #0 make as comprehensive an analysis as 
possible, tjje number of variates in the sample should* be 
as lsflrge as ^possible without its becoming too bulky to 
handle convejiientfy. Furthermore, Jhis* sample t must be 1 
a selectlbn* without bias" or prejudice for precoryceivfd 
ideas or notions. Astrological workers .should not be 
g[uilt/of rejecting from their sample cases whfch on the 
surface appear # to be contrary to their beliefs, or of using # 
only causes which they believe will corroborate their con- 



710 GEN^THLIAC^-L ASTROLOGY 

cept. It will harm more tfian help the causq o{ ast'roTogy 
if the sample is deliberately misrepresented. On , the 
other hand, it is wise to reject cases whjch are question- 
able in authenticity or whose infusion will bring ad- 
verse criticism for slip-shoddiness by ^scientists who 
doubtless will check the validity and accufacy of the 
data used after it has been published. A 1 ’ recent problem 
of this nature presented itself in an analysis of Stock- 
market fluctuations during a fifty-year period for which 
daily records are available. It was decided to exclude 
from the project any items which represented market ac- 
tivity during the first few months after expiration of the. 
bank moratorium of 1933. f Rates of increase in stock 
prices were so hectic and rapid, inclusion of that action, 
in a market analysis would so distort arithmetic means,' 
et cetera, it would be foolishness, even a scientific 
"boner,” to do so. 

According to theory, a random sample of thirty or more 
cases will serve to give reasonably accurate results from 
a study of frequency of distribution. Because of the 
great number of variables comprising an astrological dis- 
tribution (12 in the case of signs, 36 in the case of decan- 
ates, 4 in the case of Lunar quarters, 144 in the case of 
dual sign combinations, etc.), it is wige to establish min- 
imum sample sizes in accordance with the number of 
variables in question. A sample of *100 cases would b<> 
a confident minimum t for truly valid results fbr a sign 
analysis; 306 should be quite adequate for* decanate an- 
alysis. Where Lunar quarters are entailed, 36 are suffi- 
cient. fhe* size of the adequate sample obviously de- 
pends upon the number of variables and^upof. the size 
of the universe. For instance, an ?nalysfs of factors in 
the charts of American Presidents will be made of the 
total population of Presidents t6 date, and the issue of 
sample size does not enter-the problem . But 'if the l unar 
positions of infantile paralysis victims are Xe /be tabu- 
lated, no less thaji one hundred cases <should be consid- 
ered with confidence. 

Probability and the Standard Deviation 

This is not the place to engage in a lengthy discourse 
' on the technical phases of probability theory, but we are 



Til 


.. PART v! ADDITIONAL STUDIES 
n rv 

obligated ta make .mention of certain facts which the 
student "should always bear in mind* The- mechanics of 
probability are father simple, as was suggested by the 
coin-tossfrig illustration* The mathematics, however, are 
quite complicated, which limits the average student to 
the lone responsibility 9 f becoming familiar with the 
*ise of tables and the elementary arithmetic required 
for their use. The purpose of this article is purely to 
introduce the subject and instruct in the use of the 
Simplest tests of significance. We wish to show you 
how to determine whether an observed incidence of an 
astrological factor varies enough frdm evenness to have 
a significant .degree of unusu^Jness. 

•The expected frequency of a Lunar sign transit in any 
birthchart or given moment of time is l/12th of the time, 
or 8.333%, as we have stated. But we also learned that 
# it is not unusual to find more or less than .08333 of birth- 
"charts in a sample to have the Moon in any given divi- 
sion of the ecliptic. In other words, there is a definite 
“margin for error” around the exact figure of the theor- 
etical frequency, in which a factor might fall and con- 
tinue to be classed as normal. The standard deviation 
is a mathematical device which* defines the limits of this 
allcftaance. The value for the standard deviation of any 
sampling distribution depends upon the probability of 
occurrence of the parameter, the probability of its not 
occurring, *!and the numerical size af the sample itself. 
For instance, the standard deviation of a sample, of 100, 
where the probability of an occurrence is .08333, is» 2.764. 
This tells us that we can always anticipate the’observed 
number of Irials^n a series of 100 to lie within 8.333 plus 
and minus 2.764, *or 5.569 and, 11.097. In actual practice, 
^his means that it is normal, that, out of 100 items, 8 
items will ha\;e a particular characteristic, and we may 
reasonably suppose* that no significance shojild be at- 
tached to tfie series if between 5 or 11 actually are fourfd 
possessed of it. 

The odds hre even (“50^50”) that the distribution oc* 
curjred by chance alone when the obsenreij frequency is 
0.6745 4he aftiount dl the^ standard deviation. * H the in- 
cidence is equal to the standard deviation, the odds 
against the disproportion occurring by chance alone are 


712 


gbJkethli/oal astr 6 logy 


2.15 to 1. If the incidence is twice the sife pf tile 'stand- 
ard deviation, the odds are 21 to 1 against this/happen- 
i/ig by chance alone*. When the incid^n^e is 3 times the 
standard deviation, the odds amcfunt to 369 to T that the 
deviation found existing might have taJr.en place by pure 
coincidence.

It&*use % is readily mastered. The first column lists the 
sample size! The second column shpv/s what l/12th of 
this number is. The third column gives the standard 
deviation for the theoretical frequency shown in column 
two. Table II applies, in precision yvork, only tq Lunar

frequency is a twelfth ®f the Jime. 

The values listed in tht first column ,of Table I are 
usually* referred to ^as levals of significance, for they 
represent definite, discrete probabilities so small as to 
•immediately reveal a measure of unusualness. Unusual- 
ness may be ssyd to begin where the o^ds*are 10 oj more 
to 1 against ’chance occurrence for the noted deviation » 
> In ip.ost scientific work, the .05 level (ratio, = 1.96, odds 
20 to If is g€nerally w consid?red to be the line»oi? demar- 
cation between ^normalcy and abnormalcy. Where ‘as- 
trological projects are concerned, unusualness should 





714 GENETHLJACAL ASTROLOGY 

begin at the .10 level, due to the myrir.ds of 'variables 
which ere the components of every birthcjiart. <*In other 
, words, one’s attention should be arrested by the unusual- 
ness of any situation in an astrological array oi adequate 
size where the ratio exceeds 1.64. Ary scientist would 
agree to our establishing the .boundary df normalcy at' 
this point. Whenever a ratio exceeds' 1.64, we state that 
the statistic lies in the region of rejection. The term 
rejection here means that we have a vahie which departs 
sufficiently from the norm (or, mean) of expectation that 
we are behooved to reject the *.ull hypothesis yye started 

with, as discussed Earlier. 

•> 

Use of Table II may be illustrated for the benefit of 
the reader. Let us say that we have on hand a sample 
of 200 birthcharts of ence halitis patients, and that 27 of 
these are found with the Moon in' Sign X. Normal ex- 
pectation has it that 16.667 of these should have the , 
Moon so posited by sign. The standard deviation for 
this size sample is 3.909. The difference between ex- 
pectancy and incidence is 10.333, the ratio of which to 
3.909 is 2.64. Entering Table I with this ratio, we see 
that it is greater than ^.58 which marks the .01 level of 
significance. This tells us that the odds are greater ,*han 
100 to 1 that the incidence should* so greatly exceed t;he 
expectation by ‘chance alone. The ratio found , lies t far 
into the region of rejection, so we must desert the null 
hypothesis and confede that Lunar occupancy of Sign X 
is associated with the disease in question. 

Tfie ‘simplest test or significance, tfyat which we are 
describing, is usually called the u-test to distinguish it 
from other tests. In ordet to outline the subject for the* 
reader, let us jjiake use of abbreviations which come i f. 
handy in actual fieldwork. Let P stand for the jfrobabil- 
ijty of a given event occurring, and *Q for the probability 
that the event will not occur. Q is always (<i-P)f or the. 
•difference between the decimal P and unity (1.0000. .<* ). 
c Thep will be tfie number 6f items (variitet, cases) in 
the sample. M is the number gi items in the sample t 
which sfiare in this event (atttibute, positi6n, char- 
acteristic, nature, etb.) if the distribution is normal. ‘ M 
therefore is the theoretical .frequency, or expectation. 


.PART V, ‘ADDITIONAL STUDIES ‘ 718 


Expresslhg P %s a decimal at alf times, multiply N by 
P to find ¥1 i.e», M =' NP. Then multiply M by Q, or, 
expressing it otherwise, N times P times Q. The stand- 
ard deviation is the square root of NPQ. TJone of this 
procedure is at all. difficult, for simple multiplication is 
all that is necessary up*tq the point where one wishes 
to. derive the square root of NPQ. The square root is 
most easily found in a table of square robts, familiar to 
everybody. Where no such table is available, logarithms 
arc* handies}, five-plax:e logarithms sufficing in* every 
case. In fact, logarithms will shorten the process of the 
earlier multiplication itself. 

In order to figure the ratio of the observed deviation 
to the standard deviation as computed, simply find the 
difference between the incidence and the expectation. 
Incidence may be symbolized by the letter F, and the 
standard deviation by the letter S. The difference be- 
tween F and M may be abbreviated as X. The all-im- 
portant ratio is therefore: (F-M) divided by S, or, better, 
X divided by S s « There is nothing in any of these steps 
that is formidable or rigorous from the viewpoint of 
w m ork. The u-test is the easiest, as well as the most pro- 
lific, test of significance at the Command of an astrol- 
oger. 0

WHO'S WHO IJf AMERICA. We could ha#;e selected,

soiirce-volume and obtained similar if not nearly iden- 
tical results. As preliminary steps, we computed the 
theoretical frequencies and standard deviations in the 
manner prescribed for our two samples (cf differing 
size), the probabilities being the same. Our aim was to 
determine whether there was anything unusual about the 
distribution of the Sun's position through the signs at 
the births of men in these two somewhat divergent fields 
of activity and thought. Our arrays and primary data

GflUETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


According to astrology, Scorpio and C^pri^crrn are 
essentially “coarse” signs, falling, as they do under the 
rulerships of Mars# and Saturn, respectively. * 'Hence it 
«is no surprise, that these sign^ register lowest as pro- 
ducers of artistically inclined individuals. Aquarius, the 
introvert, the harmony-lover, wit^h Prcipethean farsight- 
edness and Uranian appreciation of subtilises, stands out 
with both artists and clergymen, corroborating the as- 
trological theory. Simple little research projects prove 
astrology, vindicate the wisdom of its scholars, and serve 
to enrich the fund of our knowledge. 

Mars and Major" Marine Disasters: Making use # of 
the Mars sign-probability table, we tabulated the posi- 
tions of that planet for the dates of the 339 major niarjne 
disasters since 1833 catalogued in the 1947 World Alman- 
ac and Book of Facts. More than 82,000 persons lost 
their lives in these tragedies on the high seas. No posi- 
tive ratios found in the array exceeded the .05 point, but 
two negative ratios are 50 to 1 that the abnormalcy can 
be accounted for by chance. Mars avoids the signs Cap- 
ricorn and Pisces so frequently at the eventuality of 
marine risasters, that the odds are 63 and 65 to 1, re- 
spectively, that the observed frequencies are so sparse 
by chance. That Mars in Capricorn should be a negative 
factor is understandable, for that position is a soit of 
damper on the trouble-making influence associated with 
that planet. Why Mars in Pisces is the rarest compon- 
ent is not quite ckar to the writer; theories advanced 
by thoughtful students will probably prove enlightening 
as wcdl as interesting. In passing, it is well t» mention 
that the frequency of Mars in airy signs for these marine 
disasters is significant. This is fasciitating in the light 
of the mundane astrological viewpoint which relates airy 
signs to the earth's atmosphere. With Mars as the di^ 
turbing factor* little wonder is it that the vast majority 
of marine disasters, quite naturally, are the dismal re- 
sults of storms and other bad atmospheric conditions. 

Coalmine iSisasters and Sun-square-Mars: The U. 
S. Bureau of Mines published a list of the dates, locali- 
ties and casualty tolls of the 143, worst mine disasters in, 
Ameri<&n> history (in which 20 or iliore meh were killed). 
We decided to tabulate the longitudinal differences be-

tween the* Sun and Mars for these dates, to determine 
whether frnfav&rable aspects Were more frequent than 
might reasonably ascribed to coincidence* We learned 
that 34 of these 143 infamous days found the Sun and 
Mars within 1^° *Jf a square aspect, of which 24 quad- 
futures were within an orb of 10°. Making use of the 
values given in Table VII, we discovered that this ex- 
cess gf square aspects over the expected mean was too 
significant to pe sheer, coincidence. The u-test rafio for 
the 15° orb squares was 2.24, for which the odds are 40 
to 1 again*:* chance. The ii-test ratio »for the closer 10° 
orb square aspects was 2.16, for which the odds amount- 
ed to 32 against 1. Surely, astrologers for ages have not 
beeii mistaken in their interpretation of the changing 
relationships between the heavenly bodies as viewed 
geocentrically. 

The Chi-Square Test of Significance 

It matters very little to the scientist and statistician 
whether the odc^s against chance-occurrence are 100 to 
1 or 1000 to 1, so mastery and use of probability calcula- 
tion is not essential to skill in statistical an^i sis. Iq 
practical worth, to demonstrate that a deviation has a 
measijre of unusualness^ beyond the .10, .05 or .02 levels 
of significance is all that is required undermost circum- 
stances. , 

A simple, yet extremely useful, test feasily adapted to 
the analysis of arrays in astrological research is common- 
ly known a"s the chi-square test. It is so named because 
the critical factor used in making the test is convention- 
ally abbreviated by) the small Greek letter, chi, with tui 
exponent of ?, meaning that the'* value of chi is squared. 
Cty-square is a measure of discrepancy between observa- 
tion and hypothesis. The va]ue chi-square reveals the 
magnitude of departure* between numbers in an observed 
fr$quenc>» distribution and what these numbers actually 
should be if the distribution were normah The hypoth- 
esis, as always itf such tests t>f significance, states that 
the distribution is norma] and that an y deviations ob- 
sA"ved*ar 3 »the product of chance conditions. 

To every ,valtie of chi-square there is a corresponding 
probability rating. When any computed value of phi- 



722 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


square equals or exceeds the .05 level, we, are behooved 
to. attach significance to fche data under test. .Naturally, 
when the computed chi-square equals pr exceeds the .05 
point, the distribution under 'inspection obviously de- 
parts far enough from normalcy to Reserve further at- 
tention. The significance of„a 'calculated chi-square is 
evaluated by its size as compared with what the valye 
should be if the distribution in each category were nor- 
mal, or within reasonable proximity to normalcy/ This 
evaluation is made by reference tci a table of chi-square 
values for the various levels ,of significance at different 
class intervals. Tne class intervals used mosc often in 
astrological research ar^ the twelve zodiacal signs (n = 
12), the lunar* quarters or sign-triplicities (n = 4), ,and 
fourfold groupings of the signs (n = 3). The letcer- 
symbol n is used to distinguish this factor, and is usually 
called the number of degrees of freedom, for it tells in 
how many ways an item may vary in the series undpr 
scrutiny, i.e., the number of classes it can fall into. 

Chi-square may technically be defined as the sum 
the squares of observed deviations from an expectev 
npean or means. It is readily apparent from this defin- 
ition that the chi-squaVe analysis is but another form o l 
the simple u-test already treated with one important 
difference. T ( his difference is that the u-test treats' the 
class intervals singularly in the search for significance, 
while the chi-squa^c test deals with the distribution as a 
whole. r 

«• # 

In piactice we wish* to know whether the class interval 
portions of a plarfet in the cases mailing our ‘sample 
vary significantly from that of a normal distribution. If 
our calculated chi-square* exceeds the chi-square value in 
the table at selected levels, frhen we must reject the .orig- 
inal null hypothesis and concede 4 that the observed dis- 
tribution of the planet among the signs <[$ such that it 
cannot be rationally imputed to qhance alone. * * 

T&ble VIII &ives the corresponding waiues for chi- 
square at three levels of significance for numbers of de- 
greestof freedom most often encountered in aptrological 
lesearcn. Its use 4 will be explained shortly, if it is not 
already apparent to the reader. ' #

columns.

cupies the class interval in each row. »

number of times the planet occupies each re- 
spective class. This step is undertaken by muV- 
tiplying the probability of the position and the 
number of variates in your sample. (The num- 
ber of variates is N, the number of cases in your 
sample, and is not to be confused with n which 
is the number <?f class intervals.) Under ordin- 
ary circumstances, give the figures of theoretical 
frequency to hundredths, or two decimal places. 

* # 

Column (4) is a tabulation of the differences in 
each row r>l the observed ancL theoretical fre- 
quencies. Successively subtract the values given 
f \n column (3) from the values given in column 
(2), entering tiiem in (4). 

Step 5. Square the values in column (4) and enter 
them in (5). 

Step 6 . Column (6) is a series of values* derived by 'suc- 
cessively dividing the values in column (5) by 
the values in column (3).

chapce, for the odds At too great that the discrepancy is 
coincidental! 

• If the *hi-square te*t # is passed and we ate obligated

tion for the discrepancy elsewhere tnan in 3ur primary 
data. In other words, we are still unjustified in assum-

to our primary dat'd are not to blame for the apparent

To show our readers what the worksheet for an aver- 
age chi-square test looks like, following the directions 
just given, we present herewith a chi-square table, of 
six columns for the Solar distribution among signs at the 
birthdates of 2492 eminent clergymen (the same array 
dealt with earlier from the u-test angle). The chi-square 
is most valuable, probably, for the reason that it reveal^ 
whether the departures from normalcy in the individual' 
class intervals, as shown by the u-test ratios, are com- 
pensated for in the over-all distribution. In other words, 
a significant ratio from the u-test is “cinched” as scien- 
tifically valid if the chi-square test shows the over-alj 
distribution sufficiently disrupted by normalcy in any of 
its class intervals. In preparing^ conclusive scientific 
report on any astrological research project, it is a scho- 
lastic obligation to state the chi-square value for-*the 
whole distribution ^whenever an array i^ u-tested. 

r * * 

< In r our summation of the 2492 clergymens natal Sun 
positions, the calculate/! chi-square is found to "be 25.800, 
which* exceeds both' 21.026 at the .05 level ^and 24.054 at 
the .02 level. Hence, we must reject the null hypothesis 
and admit that the placements of the Sun in the array is 
sufficiently abnormal to be in influential factor in the 
psychic make-up of religious leaders. The non-uniform- 
ity found demands another hypothesis to r e/cplain itself. 
The logical lijie of reasoning leads, directly *to the astro* 
logical theory which claims, evidently w^th good reason, 
that the Sun is the primary differentiator of personality 
types. In turn, individual differences of temperament 
and behavior-patterns are influential in Vocational apti- 
tude or selection. 4

Correlation of Attributes* 

f The analysis of* degrees of correlation between ^ny two 
sets of items or information is one of the more elaborate 
brafiches,*of statistical method. Hawever, one doing 
astrological research will not have frequent need for End- 
ing correlation coefficients of tlfle complex sort taught in 
thtf textbooks, except in certain instanced. There is a 
simple test of significance, though, which makes use of 
the chi-square principle in order to pfove or disprove the 
existence of any correlation between two sets. This 
.system makes use of what is known as a 2-by-2 classifica- 
tion, in the form of a fourfold table, for which reason it 
is often called Jthe fourfold test. Its technical name is 
generally termed the correlation of attributes. 

. This test finds a wide variety of applications in astro- 
logical .work. Because it is but* another adaptation of 
the chi-square analysis! it is easily comprehended by stu- 
dertts who have assimilated the fundamentals of the 
common’ tests already discussed. Again, simple arith- 
metic is all fhat js required, and thosvf processes may be 
greatly simplified By the use of logarithms in perform- 
ing every «step but the preliminary addition. The^aceom- 
panyiqg figure is a standardized f bo*-diagram with its 
various portions' febelled by letters. Stpdy the figure 
briefly and ^you will quickly acrive at an understanding 
of the entire procedure. The data used in every fourfold 
test*mus*«be put into a form in which two groups of data 
are possessed oi common atfributfes in different propor- 
tions. That* speaking conveniently, Category A is 
possessed by Conditicfns X and Y, and Calegory B also 
share in X and V. However, the proportions of X and 
Y in either Category are different. The fourfold test is 
devised Jo leacn how#a?tually different, frohi |h$ “sig- 
nificant” -point of view, are* these proportions in the twb 
sets.

•Step (3). This quotient is the sought-for chi- 
square value.

PIGKING WINNERS 
A* 'tfest of Astrological SkiP 


The principles and Practice of Contest Analysis 


• In spite of certaia philosophical objections tq the use 
of^astrology as a helpmeet in speculation, training in the 
technique of predicting the outcome of contests is part 
arid parcel of every astrological curriculum. It must be 
conceded by*all that our science stands or falls with its 
capacity for describing trends or indicating qualities at 
particular points on a time scale. This being so, it fol- 
lows that the outcome of a competition, all entries being 
# equal naturally or by handicap, is predictable with the 
’same degree of certainty to be relied upon in any other 
type of astrological judgment. 

Contest analysis is an art embraced Dy that branch ot * 
our study known as horary astrology, but calls into con- 
sideration all of the information and artifices gleaned 
fryn *a mastery of £oth natal and horary techniques. 
Hence, more than a cursory knowledge of astrology is 
n$ccssaj-y for proficiency in genuine contest analysis, for 
familiarity .with astrology as a who^e is an absolute pre- 
requisite. Readers delving into this article, attracted by 
its title, and in high hopes for a sure-fire formula for 
riches, should therefore be diseguraged at this* outset of 
our discussion.. 

Contest* analysis was an important share of profession- < 
*al .astrology in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries 
of our era, judging 1 jy the numerous elaborate techniques 1 
and divinajorjr methods which were in vogue And treated 
upon St length in tlje literature of those tymes. Then, in 
the ninet^entlj century, with the advent of new, canons 
of .spiritual thought, astrological literature lost its 'Cryp- 
tic* kabbalistic savoum The new trend, .which carried 
over iiAo the* early decades .of the twentieth century, jvas 
characterized by a Victorian dogmatism strangely mixed 
with occult precepts of Oriental origin. The leading 



734 


GENETHLIACAL ASTROLOGY 


astrological authors breathed new life into the stellar art 
and maintained a h/.gh degVee of consistency despite the 
G&irious viewpoints which crept into every textbook and 
thesis. The new attitude, however, was more religious 
than scientific, and it became a heresy to insist upon an 
astrology divorced from mysticism. It was held that to 
use astrology as^a means toward profit was a perversion 
of the divine science. 

The seme attitude gained ground among astrologers 
in the United States, but American astrology t was des- 
tined to become scientific and practical. A few‘eminent 
astrologers freed themselyes from the stereotype of a 
mystical basis far their subject and produced a worthy 
literature which reduced astrology to its workable essen- 
tials. Countless individuals now benefit daily from a 
practical application of astrological information. Our 
literature now has a minimum of metaphysical conjec- 
ture. In the place of literary abstractions rising as a 
morbid derivative of astrolatry, we now read the phil- 
osophy of astrology. That philosophy is the natural 
c philosophy of modern man in quest for security, discov- 
ery t and peace of mind. 

There are a number of excellent books on the rules f or 
analyzing horary charts of the usual kind, i.e., the simple 
inquiry concerning decisions or ventures, or the request 
for information not evident on the surface of a situation. 
Every student should attempt to perfect himself in sim- 
ple ho t rary techniques, with the old texts as guidance 
material and actual experience as the final authority. 
Restricting oneself to genethliacs always proves to be 
a deterrent to full appreciation of astrological inference. 
Horary astrology is the natural complement or expan- 
sion of natal techniques, for through horary procedures 
one becomes adept at recognizing the expression of plan- 
etary influences in “the little things'* of life, 'which ordin- 
arily escape attention. 

* t < t#1 

c This thesis is confined to a generalization of horary 
procedures as c they relate to contest analysis. Proceed- 
ing on the ^assumption that it is possible td predict cor- 
rectly the results of at contest via horary jnethods, let us 
devote our consideration to the sufficiencies and limita- 



PART V, ADDITIONAL STUDIES - 735. 

« i 

tiorrs ofacortfe^J: analysis, outline? the traditional rules for 
makipg»judgments, and relay, to our^readers what exper- 
ience has taught Us to be valid in th*e way of interpreta- 
tion. 

The traditional rules for making sound judgments ap- 
pear to»be simpte when we integrate in our minds the 
*scatterpd directions for delineations gVen in the text- 
books. Actual, fieldwork, nevertheless, always proves 
much more complicated than expected due to the endless 
stream of contradicting factors, modifying conditions 
and ovrftones of influence that one J despairingly finds in 
almost every horary chart. Even more discouraging to 
tfce novice is the seeming irrationality pf the theory it- 
self, it being replete with loopholes often irreconcilable 
.with a logical view of the matter. By this we mean that 
the customary rule<f for delineation are often such far- 
-flung departures from seeming common sense that the 
intelligent student is destitute of any self-confidence. 
Confidence in one's ability to arrive at a stable conclu- 
sion is a requirement for all successful application of 
astrology. It is our purpose in this paper to integrate '

The How and Why 

A horary chart for the time and place of the occasion 
— be it a football, basAall, basketball or ice hockey 
game, boxing or tennis match — will usually suffice to 
reveal the winner,, independently of.the personal fortunes 
of the players. The skeptic may well ask, “Why, and 
in what manner, does such a horoscope indicate the 
winner of *an as-yet-unplayed game?*' Let us answer 
that question. 

It is 7>ur firm corfviction, based upcyi.the combined 
principles of astrology and social psychology, that the 
outcomes of competitive spArts events, publicly* attended, 
are surely more amenable to accurate prediction than are 
individual affairs. This is true beesyuse, psychologically 
speaking, fhe “collective mind" is far moiSL, susceptible 
to planetary influences (indeed, to alj external stimuli in 
genera^ than tne singular mind of ah individual. 

* Thfs is true also because the student’s mind is focussed 
on one affair* and its specific outcome. The affair 6 is 
restricted to a knowrf set of “variables” and proper allo- 
cation of planetary and zodiacal “rulerships” is eleraen-

Charting the Game 

Where analysis of the actual event is concerned, a 
chart for the time ?nd place of the game's inception is 
expedient. In football, this would be kick-off time at 
the stadium, while a race should be charted for its post- 
time. A prizefight chart should be calculated for the 
beginning of the first round. The inception of any com- 
petitive or combatant athletic event is the critical mo- 
ment. In most cases, this moment is predetermined by 
schedule; a slight delay of a few minutes at actual game 
time should make little or no difference in the analysis 
if the chart is "radical.” 

In agreement with the horary tradition, a t chart for an 
inquiry a^ to the dlitcome of a game often identifies the 
pinner. We discourage this practice, however, except 
wheft the querent has only a temporary and 'relatively 
intense interest in the 'particular evenj;, for^all questions 
amenable to horary analysis should st$m directly from a 
"personal equation.” W fry this \s so should be apparent 
to all student^ of astrology. * Unless a personal stake is 
involved, it would be rathe^ unreasonable to expect' the

the fortunes of two opponents or teams, as Jvell i as their 
emotional impacts on two vast throngs of fans. A hor- 
ary figure, to be f valid, must? be based oh c th€ significant 
motnent of a jerisis in experience

A “void chart” is one in which* the last three degree: 
or first three degrees of any sign are found on the 'as- 
cendant. A “void-of-course Moon” means that* our sat- 
ellite is .within the last three degrees .of any sign and 
makes no further aspects before its egress from that 
Vigm pither of these' conditions often has a* complete 
nullifying or reversing effect on the remaining ch?rt in- 
dications, so that the least-favored ^eam ‘‘wins or the 
game is tied at its finish. Hence, it is never wise to 
work with such a chart, *for it is, to use a familiar astro- 
logical phrase*, "not fit to be judged.”

LOUIS versus WALjCOTT

June 24 1948 — New Yprk, lieV York

The event which will probably be remembered By ring 
fans as the .outstanding prizefight of all time was fought 
between Joe Loiiis apd “Jersey Joe” Walcott on June 
25(h, 1048, This fight was a re-match by public demand 
for a technical settlement of a dispute rising over .the 
fairness of the judge's decision after th^ fifteen-rounder 
of these boxers on December 5th, *1947. This "battle of 
the century" was necessary in, order to appease a vast 
proportion of the public who contended .that Walcott

ABOUT HORSERACINC 

' Having covered the gamut of published and quasi- 
bonded “systems” for playing the ponies star-wise, we 
have come to the conclusion that *hey irz as groundless 
as their originators are poundlcss. We have found no 
foundation in statistical fact for the persistent belief in 
a numerologicaf system. We have failed to find any- 
thing significant in the (pseudo-Pythagorean) numerical 
values of the horses' names, their post positions, handi- 
cap numbers or dat^s of triumph, singularly or in com- 
bination, to provide reason for any belief in a numero- 
logical system. Evidently*- the equine species does not 
believe in, or is not beholden # to, modern numerology. 

On the other hand, „ the so-called “kabbalistic” scheme 
of numerical values and phonetics (rather than letters of 
the alphabet) is promising, although we have not tested 
it thoroughly enough to be emphatic. This system, al- 
most unknown andewidely divergent from ordinary num- 
' erology, is based upon assigned values to the nuances of 
the voice. In other words, the sounds rather than letters 
of a* name are used. There is a definite astrological tie- 
up in this method, which is often fascinating along cer- 
tain lines of interpretation. 

% “ 

The kabbalistic theory is a derivative^ of Tarot phil- 
osophy. I^claims to identify every thing and subject in 
existei^ce by classification in one or more of twenty-two 
categories 1 termed Arcanu by its exponents. The twenty- 
two basic nuances of the voice are also di stributed appro- 
priately in these 'categories which are qualitatively de- 
fined by association with an astrological factot (sign or, 
planet). Exposition of this scheme would require a com- 
plete artielp in itself, if not a volume, so We shall forego 
all but the rudiments of the theory 

' For the benefit of readers who like to ptnsue such doc- 
tf ines. for either intellectual edification or simply as di- 
version, we give the following tabulation of the twenty- 
two' cate{j|b/ies with their supposed allocations to sounds 
and astrological correlation.

Serious Responsibility of Astrologers 

The fine, spiritual significance of Astrology^is defiled 
by. thosg who make a bugbear of "‘adverse" aspects and 
“evil" planets, for in reality “there is no evil but think- 
ing makes it so." Those interpreters who lay undue 
stress on the so-called “adverse aspects"' not only pervert 
fhe purpose and value of Astrology but often do actual 
harm to the one who receives such a horoscope by arous- 
ing unnecessary fear and thus reducing the moral stam- 
ina through dreadful anticipation. Long before the 
aspects actually culminate the native is bereft of the 
resistance and resourceful power necessary to meet and 
handle the situation successfully. Usually when the ad-- 
verse conditions actually manifest they are not so “bad" 
as predicted nor so dreadful as the native anticipated, 
therefore the client is worse oflTthan before the “reading" 
because he was not properly informed. 

- ?fime and again I have received letters from those 
who had been, given horoscope readings w,hich struck 
terror in their minds, asking if I thought it was going to 
be as “-bad" as predicted. Their “reading" yras* doing 
more harm than good. A beatftifi\l opportunity to ren- 
der a uplifting service through Astrology had been lost 
because the principles of <^stro-science had hot been 
properly -interpreted. 

j * 

In reading the delineations of planetary aspects, natal, 
pi ogr f essed 4>i/ transitory, it should always be remember- 
ed that they refer tp “tendencies" in the, native to react 
inr the m*nn<j£ described. * The expression of a tendency 
is its reaction to circumstances, and the reaction^ will 
be* of ,value according to our knowledge -of controlling 
and directing ourselves ^wisely. “Ail thing's *work* to- 
gether for good to those who love the Lord."

ecliptic, except the beginning of Aries and Libra, #> as 
declination because the plane of the ecliptic is not pa r - 
allel with that of the equator and they meet at those two 
points. The Sun is never more than 23 degrees 28 min- 
utes in declination, which it reaches in the signs Cancer 
(North Dec.) and Capricorn (South Dec.) due to the 
earth's inclinatiom in orbit. See Parallel.

Easter — [AS, eastre, pi. ejstron (akin to German 

ostern,)* fc*on\ name of old Teut. goddess of spyng, AS. 
Eastrje; ikin t6 §Ri, usra dawrf, and E! east.] 1. An annual 
church celebration commemorating .Christas resurrecticJh. 
The early churdi was divided as to the nature and the 
;time of this celebration. Some communions (see Quarto- 
’deciman) were influenced by the coincidence of Christ’s 
# death with the Jewish Passover; others, especially in 
Gaul, placed Ejster always on the 25th* of March, the 
Vynal equinox (in the Julian calendar,) the d^y of the 
resurrection of the vegetation god Attis. Uniformity was 
established by the first Nicene Council, which determined 
that Easter is always the first Sunday after the firsjt full 
•mpon after the vernal equindk (March Jlst in the Gre- 
gorian calendar.) (Note by L. G. : If the full moon hap- 
pens on Sunday then Easter is the Sunday after.) The 
date of the full moon is ascertained, according to certain 
calendar rules, and may differ from that of the actual 
‘(astronomical) full moon. These rules include as pre- 
liminaries the finding of the golden number, epact, and 
dominical letter. These data, and the dates of Easter 
from 1943 to 1953 are:

Ecliptic — The Sun's apparent path in the orbit of th£ 
Earth, but in reality it is the path of the Earth around

occur therein. The belt of zodiacal Tigris through whidh

Elevated — A planet posited above another in a chart.

strength to the planet, especially when ascending. 

Elongation — The distance of a planet from the Sun. 
Mercury cannot acquire greater Elongation than 2£f, 
Venus 48°. It is 'helpful to remember this when figuring 
their aspects to the Sun in natal charts. In a grogresrseci 
chart, as the native grows older, M^rcvry and Venus 
n\jLy progress to* greater distances from the radical Sun 
and fOrrrv larger aspects such as sextile, square or trine.

formtall aspects to the natal Sun. But ^trictfy speaking, 
elongation has reference ,to the*, greatest distance in 
zodiac (and consequently hi nat^iviti^s) which the inferior*

wherein it shows Miat nothing will come of the matter 
in question. A testimony that the horary chart is not 
radical. 

Watery Signs — Cancer; Scorpio, Pisces. 

Zenith — Actually it is the point directly overhead. 
Often incorrectly referred to as the midheaven.- Note 
that the Midheaven .is south of this point at the ecliptic. 

^ » . * 

Zodiac — A belt or jring around the heavens between 
15 and 1% degrees wid^ divided into twelve sighs each 
containing 30 degrees of longitude, through which .the 
planers transit, each in an orbit of its own. n 

Zodiacal Aspects — Aspects in thj zodiac measured by 
sign and degree. Major aspects are: conjunction, par- 
allel, opposition, trine, square and textile/' The minor 
aspects o( favorable quality are vigintile’, ,18 degrees; 
quindecile, 24 degree; semi-sextife, 30 degrees; decile, 
36 degrees; qufntile, 72 degree,?; treTlecile,,10§ ; degrees ; 
bi-quintile, 144 degrees. Minor aspects of adverse quality : 
semi-square, 45' degrees; sesquiq^adrate, ,135 degrees; 
quincunx, F50 degrees!
